44:4844 minutes, 48 secondscheck each row whether it meets this condition or not. So for example for Maria she doesn't fulfill the condition
44:5444 minutes, 54 secondsbecause her score the 350 is not higher than 500. So she doesn't fulfill the condition and SQL going to go and remove

49:5049 minutes, 50 secondsdata using the order by. So let's go. Okay. So what is exactly order by?
49:5949 minutes, 59 secondsYou can use this type of clouds in order to sort your data. And of course, in order to sort your data, you have to decide on two mechanism. Either you want
50:0650 minutes, 6 secondsto sort your data ascending from the lowest value to the highest value or exactly the opposite way using descending from the highest value to the
50:1550 minutes, 15 secondslowest. And the syntax kind of looks like this. So as usual, we start with the select and then from and after the from you can specify order by and with
50:2350 minutes, 23 secondsthat you are telling SQL we have to sort the data and you have to specify two things. First you have to specify for SQL the column that should be used in
50:3150 minutes, 31 secondsorder to sort the results. So for example you can say score and after the column name you have to specify the mechanism. So for example you say
50:3950 minutes, 39 secondsascending from the lowest to the highest. And in SQL if you don't specify the mechanism the default going to be ascending. So you will not get an error
50:4750 minutes, 47 secondsif you don't specify anything after the column name. But my advice here is always to specify something after the column easier because it's just
50:5650 minutes, 56 secondsstraightforward and easier to understand and if someone reads it can understand immediately it's going to be ascending because maybe not everyone knows what is
51:0451 minutes, 4 secondsthe default in SQL. So always specify a value even if it's like easier to skip it and if you want to store the data from the highest to the lowest then you
51:1251 minutes, 12 secondscan specify descending. So as usual SQL going to go and start from the from it's going to go and grab your data from database. Then the second step is SQL
51:2051 minutes, 20 secondsgoing to go and sort the result. So the order by going to be executed and SQL going to see okay I'm going to sort it by the score and using the sending
51:2851 minutes, 28 secondsmechanism and still going to go and start like moving around your rows where the first row going to be the customer with the highest score and in this
51:3651 minutes, 36 secondsexample John has the highest score the 900. So John going to appear as a first row at the result and that's because his

3:44:043 hours, 44 minutes, 4 secondsall the possible combinations from both tables. So we are doing something called like cartesian join. So now if you look at our two circles, we want everything

3:44:203 hours, 44 minutes, 20 secondsthis example, we have two rows in A and three rows in B. If you do a cross join,
3:44:253 hours, 44 minutes, 25 secondsyou will get six possible combinations by just multiplying the number of rows between A and B. So be careful using the cross join. If you use it, you will get
3:44:333 hours, 44 minutes, 33 secondslike crazy number of rows in the results and you're going to make the database really busy finding out the result for you. So now about the syntax, it's going to be the easiest. So you start as usual

4:32:044 hours, 32 minutes, 4 secondsAll right friends, so now we come to the part where I'm going to show you how I usually use the set operators in my projects for data analyszis or for data
4:32:124 hours, 32 minutes, 12 secondsengineering. So here are the most important use cases for the set operators. All right, the first use case is combining similar tables before doing
4:32:204 hours, 32 minutes, 20 secondsdata analyzes. In some scenarios, we want to generate a report and we end up writing similar queries on top of similar tables and we go at the end and
4:32:294 hours, 32 minutes, 29 secondsjoin all the results from the queries in order to present the final report. And now instead of doing that what we can do first we can go and combine all the
4:32:384 hours, 32 minutes, 38 secondssimilar informations into one table and then we can do on top of it a query a data analyzes in order to generate a report and we can do that using the
4:32:474 hours, 32 minutes, 47 secondsunion or union all. Let's have few examples. So let's say that we have four tables employees, customers, suppliers and students. So as you can see all of them are sharing the same informations.
4:32:574 hours, 32 minutes, 57 secondsThey hold data about persons. So now let's say that you are generating a report that requires all the individuals in the organization in the database. So
4:33:064 hours, 33 minutes, 6 secondswhat you're going to end up doing is writing SQL query for the employees,
4:33:104 hours, 33 minutes, 10 secondsanother one for customers and as well for the suppliers and the students. And then you're going to go and merge all the results from those queries into the
4:33:194 hours, 33 minutes, 19 secondsfinal report. Now the issue with this setup is that you are having a lot of queries, a lot of similar queries. So you have it here four times. And now
4:33:274 hours, 33 minutes, 27 secondswhat might happen is that you go and change the logic of the first two queries and you forget later to do it for the other two and you will get really inconsistent data in the reports.
4:33:374 hours, 33 minutes, 37 secondsSo instead of that what we can do we can go and use the set operators in order to combine first all those tables in one big table. So what we're going to do
4:33:464 hours, 33 minutes, 46 secondswe're going to go and use a union in order to combine those four tables into the table persons. So we're going to have it like this. So we will get all
4:33:544 hours, 33 minutes, 54 secondsthe rows from the employees and put it in the persons all the rows from the customers from the suppliers and as well from the students and put everything in
4:34:034 hours, 34 minutes, 3 secondsone big table that holds all the informations about the individuals that we have inside our database. And now the next step after we combine the data now
4:34:124 hours, 34 minutes, 12 secondswe write an SQL query in order to analyze this new big table and the result going to be presented in the
4:34:194 hours, 34 minutes, 19 secondsreports. And now of course the advantage here is that we have only one SQL query for the data analyzers on top of this table instead of having it four times.
4:34:284 hours, 34 minutes, 28 secondsAnd now if you go and change the logic of the SQL query, it going to be applied automatically on all the data that we have in the database. And we have done
4:34:364 hours, 34 minutes, 36 secondsalready this example where we have combined the data between the employees and customers. Another scenario where we have to combine data before doing any
4:34:424 hours, 34 minutes, 42 secondsreporting. That's sometimes the database developers tend to divide a table one big table into multiple small tables in
4:34:504 hours, 34 minutes, 50 secondsorder to optimize the performance. For example, here splitting the orders by the year. We have orders 2022 2023. Now

4:40:084 hours, 40 minutes, 8 secondsincorrect data which leads to incorrect analyzes. So here the best practices to not use the star and to clearly list all the columns. Now one more technique that

4:41:424 hours, 41 minutes, 42 secondsdifferent tables. All right. Now we have another use case for the set operators. It's
4:41:504 hours, 41 minutes, 50 secondsmore for data engineers. We can use the except in order to find the delta between two batches of data. For
4:41:574 hours, 41 minutes, 57 secondsexample, data engineers build data pipelines in order to load daily new data from the source systems to a data
4:42:044 hours, 42 minutes, 4 secondswarehouse or a data lake. Now, in those data pipelines, we have to build a logic in order to identify what are the new data that is generated from the source
4:42:134 hours, 42 minutes, 13 secondssystem in order to insert it in the data warehouse. One way to do it is to use the set operator except in order to compare the current data with the
4:42:224 hours, 42 minutes, 22 secondsprevious load. Let's have a very simple example. So in the day number one we have two customers one and two. So what going to happen in this day we're going to go and load those two customers into
4:42:314 hours, 42 minutes, 31 secondsthe data warehouse. So in the data warehouse we will get as well one and two. So this is for the first day nothing is crazy. We just load the data
4:42:384 hours, 42 minutes, 38 secondsas it is. Now for the second day we will get the new data from the source system and it's going to look like this. So now if you check the second day you can see
4:42:454 hours, 42 minutes, 45 secondsthat we have again the customer number one we have already loaded to the data warehouse. So we have it as the previous day but we have a new customer ID number
4:42:554 hours, 42 minutes, 55 secondsthree. So now in order to load only the new data we don't need to load again the customer number one. What we can do? We
4:43:024 hours, 43 minutes, 2 secondscan do an accept between the day number two with the previous load with the day number one. So now if we simply do an
4:43:094 hours, 43 minutes, 9 secondsaccept between those two sets we're going to go and identify the new data that is existing in the source system which is only the record number three.
4:43:184 hours, 43 minutes, 18 secondsSo now what going to happen if we do except between day two and day one we will get one record the new record that we're going to go and insert it inside
4:43:264 hours, 43 minutes, 26 secondsour data warehouse. So as you can see this set operator except is very powerful in order to compare two sets and not only for data analysis we can
4:43:344 hours, 43 minutes, 34 secondsuse it as you can see for data engineering in order to identify what is the new data that is generated from the sources in order to insert it inside our data warehouse.
4:43:484 hours, 43 minutes, 48 secondsOkay, one more use case for the set operators that I personally use a lot in my project is that if you are doing data migrations, you can use the accept in
4:43:564 hours, 43 minutes, 56 secondsorder to check the data quality and more specifically we can use it in order to check the data completeness. Okay, so we have the following scenario where we are
4:44:044 hours, 44 minutes, 4 secondsdoing data migrations between two databases. So let's say that we would like to move this table from database A to database B. So we're going to go and
4:44:124 hours, 44 minutes, 12 secondsload the table to the new database. And now what is very important after you move the data is that to check whether
4:44:184 hours, 44 minutes, 18 secondsall the records did move from database A to database B we are not missing anything even one record. So we want to do data completeness test and there are
4:44:274 hours, 44 minutes, 27 secondsmany methods on how to do this test. One of them is to use that set operator except. So how we going to do it? We're going to do an except between the table
4:44:364 hours, 44 minutes, 36 secondsfrom database A and the table from database B in order to find any record that is still in database A which is not
4:44:434 hours, 44 minutes, 43 secondsmigrated to the database B. And of course the best result is that we will not get anything. The result should be empty. If we get an empty that means all
4:44:524 hours, 44 minutes, 52 secondsthe rows from database A exists in the database B. And now of course we are not done yet. We want to do the comparison
4:44:594 hours, 44 minutes, 59 secondsbut the way around. We want to find any new rows that is in database B that we don't find in database A. Those two
4:45:074 hours, 45 minutes, 7 secondstables must be identical. So now what we're going to do, we're going to do an except but the first table going to be from the database B. And then we're going to compare it with the database A.
4:45:154 hours, 45 minutes, 15 secondsAnd we have the same expectation. The output should be as well empty. And now after doing the except twice for both sides and we are getting empty in the
4:45:244 hours, 45 minutes, 24 secondsresults. That means those two tables are identical and we are not missing anything. So this is another amazing use
4:45:314 hours, 45 minutes, 31 secondscase for the set operators in order to improve the quality of your data migrations and in order to do data completeness
4:45:414 hours, 45 minutes, 41 secondstest. Okay. So now let's have a quick summary about the set operators. So the set operator is going to go and combine the rows of multiple queries, multiple
4:45:494 hours, 45 minutes, 49 secondstables into one single result. And we have four different types of the asset operators. The first one is the union where it's going to go and combine all
4:45:574 hours, 45 minutes, 57 secondsthe rows but without including any duplicates. The second one we have the union all it's very similar. And the third one we have the except it's going
4:46:054 hours, 46 minutes, 5 secondsto show all the rows from the first query that cannot be found in the second query. And the fourth one we have the intersect where it's going to show the
4:46:124 hours, 46 minutes, 12 secondscommon rows between two queries. And of course we have SQL rules in order to use the set operators. Both of the queries should have the same number of columns,
4:46:214 hours, 46 minutes, 21 secondsthe same data types and the order of columns. And the last rule, don't forget that the first query controls the aliases, the name of the columns and the
4:46:304 hours, 46 minutes, 30 secondsdata types of the entire result. And we have found amazing use cases for the set operators. Like for example, using union and union all in order to combine similar informations into one big table.
4:46:414 hours, 46 minutes, 41 secondsOr we can go and use the amazing except operator in order to compare two different results in order to find the differences between them. And I usually

6:06:586 hours, 6 minutes, 58 secondsSo two small m means minutes but two capital m means month. Then double point and small 2s. So now the whole code is
6:07:076 hours, 7 minutes, 7 secondscalled the date format. So this is the date format representation of this value. Now in the world there are different representations on how to
6:07:166 hours, 7 minutes, 16 secondsrepresent a date. So for example in SQL we have the international standard ISO6801 and the date format is like we
6:07:236 hours, 7 minutes, 23 secondshave learned first it start with the year. So four digit for the years minus two digit for the month minus two digit
6:07:316 hours, 7 minutes, 31 secondsfor the day. So year month day but in the USA we have different standards. So first it start with the month. So we
6:07:386 hours, 7 minutes, 38 secondshave mm and then after that it is followed with the day. So we have then the day and after that at the end we have the year. So this is the sentence
6:07:476 hours, 7 minutes, 47 secondsformat that is used in USA and in Europe we have different representations of the day. So it start first with the small.
6:07:536 hours, 7 minutes, 53 secondsSo it starts with the day then the month and then the year. So this is exactly the opposite of the international standards. So as you can see we don't
6:08:016 hours, 8 minutes, 1 secondhave one standard. We have different ways on how we represent dates. But in SQL the SQL server is following the format of the international standards.
6:08:106 hours, 8 minutes, 10 secondsSo SQL server start always with the year then month then day. So all dates that are used in our SQL database can be

7:11:467 hours, 11 minutes, 46 secondsAll right. So now let's have a quick summary about the differences between the kowalis and isnull. So as we learned the isnull is limited only to two values
7:11:547 hours, 11 minutes, 54 secondswhere the kowalis is amazing where you can have a list of multiple values which is a great advantage compared to the isnull. Now if you are talking about the
7:12:027 hours, 12 minutes, 2 secondsperformance the isnull is faster than the kawalis. So if you want to optimize the performance of your query then go with the isnull. Now there is another
7:12:117 hours, 12 minutes, 11 secondsproblem with the isnull is that we have different keywords for different databases. So for Microsoft SQL server
7:12:187 hours, 12 minutes, 18 secondswe use the isnull as we learned but in Oracle they have different implementations they use the NVL and other database like MySQL you have if
7:12:287 hours, 12 minutes, 28 secondsnull and all those three functions are doing the same but we have different implementations for different databases but in the other hand the cowis it is
7:12:357 hours, 12 minutes, 35 secondsavailable in all different databases. So here we have like an agreement or standards between the databases of using the kowalis. So here again this is a
7:12:437 hours, 12 minutes, 43 secondsgreat advantage for the kowalis because if you are writing like scripts and someday you want to migrate from one database to another. If you are using
7:12:507 hours, 12 minutes, 50 secondsthe kowalis you don't have to change anything but if you are using the isnull then you have to go and adjust your queries and scripts with the correct
7:12:587 hours, 12 minutes, 58 secondsfunctions. That's why I tend always to use the kalis and avoid using the isnull. Only if it's really necessary
7:13:067 hours, 13 minutes, 6 secondsthat I have really bad performance, I go and try the isnull. But I usually stick with the kowalis. So that is my advice for you. Go with the kowalis and stick with the standard.
7:13:197 hours, 13 minutes, 19 secondsNow the use cases of the kowalis and the isnull are very similar and we mainly use them in order to handle the null before doing any SQL task. For example,
7:13:307 hours, 13 minutes, 30 secondswe can use them in order to handle the null before doing data aggregations. So let's understand what this means.

7:14:097 hours, 14 minutes, 9 secondsaggregate functions like the sum count if you are counting the sales min and max. There is only one exception about the aggregate function count. If you are

7:23:097 hours, 23 minutes, 9 secondsOkay, moving on to the next use case for the kowalis and is null. We can use them in order to handle the null before doing joins. This is little bit advanced use
7:23:187 hours, 23 minutes, 18 secondscase but it's very important to understand it. So let's understand why this is important. Let's have for example two tables table A and table B.
7:23:267 hours, 23 minutes, 26 secondsAnd in some scenarios we have to go and combine those two tables using the joins. And now in order to join two tables, we have to go and specify the
7:23:347 hours, 23 minutes, 34 secondskeys between the table A and table B in order to join on it. So in this example,
7:23:387 hours, 23 minutes, 38 secondswe have two keys in order to join the tables. Now here comes the special case.
7:23:437 hours, 23 minutes, 43 secondsIf those keys don't have any nulls inside it and all the data are filled,
7:23:477 hours, 23 minutes, 47 secondsthen your join going to work perfectly and you will get the expected results.
7:23:517 hours, 23 minutes, 51 secondsAnd now you might have a special case where there are nulls inside the keys.
7:23:557 hours, 23 minutes, 55 secondsSo there are missing values and this is a big problem because in the output you will get unexpected results and some records will be totally missing. So in
7:24:047 hours, 24 minutes, 4 secondsthis scenario we have to handle the nulls inside the keys before doing the joins. Let's have a very simple example in order to understand this behavior.
7:24:127 hours, 24 minutes, 12 secondsAll right. So now let's have this very simple example where we have two tables and we want to combine them. So in the first table we have a year type orders
7:24:197 hours, 24 minutes, 19 secondsand in the second table we have as well year type and we have sales. So now we would like to go and combine those two tables in order to have all informations
7:24:277 hours, 24 minutes, 27 secondsin one result. Now we can go of course and use the inner join between the table one and table two and the keys for the joins here. As you can see we have the
7:24:367 hours, 24 minutes, 36 secondsyear in both of the tables and as well the type. So we're going to go and use both of those columns as a key for the
7:24:427 hours, 24 minutes, 42 secondsjoin. So let's do it step by step how going to execute this. So we need the year type and the results. So it's going to go and take those two columns to the
7:24:517 hours, 24 minutes, 51 secondsresults and we need the orders and sales. So it's going to take as well the orders and the sales from the second
7:24:587 hours, 24 minutes, 58 secondstable. So now let's start doing it row by row. So the first key going to be those two columns. So we have 2024 and
7:25:067 hours, 25 minutes, 6 secondsthe type A. So now it's going to start searching for those two informations in the second table. And as you can see we have here a match, right? So the first
7:25:157 hours, 25 minutes, 15 secondsrow is as well matching since it's inner join it going to present in the output only the matching rows from left and right. So in the outputs we're going to
7:25:247 hours, 25 minutes, 24 secondsget the whole row from the table one and we will get the sales from the table two. All right. So that's all for the
7:25:317 hours, 25 minutes, 31 secondsfirst row. Now let's move to the second row over here. So what are the values of the keys? We have 20 24 and null. So now
7:25:407 hours, 25 minutes, 40 secondsif you check the matches on the right side you can see we have a match here right it is logical so it's as well 20
7:25:477 hours, 25 minutes, 47 seconds24 and null so everything is matching and we should get it in the result right SQL cannot go and use the equal operator
7:25:557 hours, 25 minutes, 55 secondsin order to join tables so even though that is logically it makes sense to have it at the output but still SQL cannot go
7:26:027 hours, 26 minutes, 2 secondsand compare the nulls that's why this is a problem for this combination SQL will not find any matching So we will not get
7:26:107 hours, 26 minutes, 10 secondsany informations for the combination of 2024 and null. So for us of course in the business this is missing
7:26:177 hours, 26 minutes, 17 secondsinformations and as well inaccurate results. So we're going to miss this row and it's still going to go and jump to the third row. So here what are the values of the key. We have 20 25 and B.
7:26:287 hours, 26 minutes, 28 secondsNow it's going to go and search it in the second table and it's still going to find a match over here. So in the outputs we're going to get those values.
7:26:377 hours, 26 minutes, 37 secondsThe the orders going to be 50, the sales 300. Now it's going to go to the last row and we have here again the same
7:26:447 hours, 26 minutes, 44 secondsproblem. We have here 2025 and null. And of course if you check the data you will say yes we have a matching over here but
7:26:527 hours, 26 minutes, 52 secondsSQL would ignore it. So we have exactly the same situation and we will not find it at the results. So at the output we
7:26:597 hours, 26 minutes, 59 secondswill get only two rows even though that those two tables are like identicals if you compare the keys. So with that we
7:27:067 hours, 27 minutes, 6 secondsare losing data at the results and we are providing inaccurate results. So my friends if you have nulls inside your

7:45:467 hours, 45 minutes, 46 secondsto understand the new types. So basically we have two sets or let's say two tables the left and the right. And if you go and use an inner join what we
7:45:557 hours, 45 minutes, 55 secondsare doing here we are finding only the matching rows between the left table and the right table. So at the result we will get only the matching rows. Now we
7:46:047 hours, 46 minutes, 4 secondshave another type of joints called lift outer join. And if you use this type at the result you will get all the rows
7:46:117 hours, 46 minutes, 11 secondsfrom the left table and as well only the matching rows from the right table. Now we have another type which is exactly
7:46:187 hours, 46 minutes, 18 secondsthe opposite the right join. And here we're going to get all the rows from the right table and only the matching informations from the left table. And
7:46:277 hours, 46 minutes, 27 secondsnow to the last type that we learned. We have the full join where we will get all the rows from the left and as well all the rows from the right. So we will not
7:46:357 hours, 46 minutes, 35 secondsbe missing anything. So those are the four basic joints that we have learned in SQL. But in SQL we have as well other types that are more advanced. But we don't have in SQL any keywords for that.
7:46:477 hours, 46 minutes, 47 secondsSo the first one called lift anti-join.
7:46:517 hours, 46 minutes, 51 secondsSo what we are saying here we need all the rows from the left table but this time without the matching rows. So all
7:46:597 hours, 46 minutes, 59 secondsthe informations that are matching with the right table we don't want to see it at the results. And as I said we don't have here an extra keyword for this type
7:47:077 hours, 47 minutes, 7 secondsof join. But in order to get this effect we're going to go and combine the left join together with the isnull. And with that we're going to get all the data
7:47:157 hours, 47 minutes, 15 secondsfrom the left side but without anything that is matching the right side. And this we call it left anti- join. And we have another advanced type for the
7:47:247 hours, 47 minutes, 24 secondsjoints called the right anti- join. This is exactly the opposite. So we are saying all the rows from the right table
7:47:317 hours, 47 minutes, 31 secondswithout having any matching rows from the left table. So all the informations on the right side that is not matching the left side. So again here we don't
7:47:397 hours, 47 minutes, 39 secondshave a keyword for that. We're going to go and work with the right join plus and is null. So with that, as you can see,

8:06:298 hours, 6 minutes, 29 secondsAnd in scale we have different functions in order to handle the nulls. So now if you want to replace a null with the value we can go either with the function

8:07:038 hours, 7 minutes, 3 secondsand so on. And we have to handle the nulls as well before doing any mathematical operations like using the plus operator to concatenate two
8:07:118 hours, 7 minutes, 11 secondsstrings. And in some scenarios as we learned we have to handle the nulls as well before doing joins. And in other cases we have as well to handle the

8:07:488 hours, 7 minutes, 48 secondsgoing to move to a very special topic called the case statements. This is very important tool in order to do data transformations. So let's
8:07:598 hours, 7 minutes, 59 secondsgo case statements. It can allow you to build a conditional logic in your SQL query by evaluating a list of conditions
8:08:078 hours, 8 minutes, 7 secondsone by one and return a value when the first condition is met. So now let's understand the syntax of the case statements and what this
8:08:188 hours, 8 minutes, 18 secondsmeans. Okay. So now let's see the syntax step by step. It start with the keyword case. This case indicates now we are
8:08:258 hours, 8 minutes, 25 secondsstarting a logic a conditional logic in SQL. It's like programming languages as you start with the if else. So the if is the keyword of a logic and the whole
8:08:348 hours, 8 minutes, 34 secondslogic as well ends with another keyword called end. So once SQL sees the end. So this is the end of the conditional
8:08:418 hours, 8 minutes, 41 secondslogic. So the case is the start and the end is the end. So now what we're going to have in between is the conditional logics right. So the conditional logic
8:08:498 hours, 8 minutes, 49 secondsstart with the keyword when. Now we are telling SQL we have a condition to be evaluated and then we're going to go and specify that conditional logic. So now
8:08:588 hours, 8 minutes, 58 secondswe have to tell SQL what can happen if this condition is fulfilled. So now we have to use another keyword called then.
8:09:058 hours, 9 minutes, 5 secondsSo now we are telling SQL show this results if the condition is true. So as you can see it's very simple. It's like the natural language, right? It's like
8:09:138 hours, 9 minutes, 13 secondsin English when the condition one is met then show the results. It's very logic,
8:09:188 hours, 9 minutes, 18 secondsright? And now of course we can go and add a second condition inside our case statements. So we're going to have the same setup. When condition two if this is true then show the result number two.
8:09:288 hours, 9 minutes, 28 secondsWe specify the keyword when then we have a second condition. And if this condition is true then we tell SQL to show another results. And of course it's
8:09:378 hours, 9 minutes, 37 secondsvery important to understand in the syntax of that SQL going to go and process the conditions from the top to the bottom. So the first most important
8:09:458 hours, 9 minutes, 45 secondscondition should be at the start. So SQL going to first check this condition. If it fails and it's not true then it going to go and jump to the second condition.
8:09:548 hours, 9 minutes, 54 secondsSo the order of the conditions is very important in your logic. And now of course we can go and add multiple conditions depend on the logic using the
8:10:028 hours, 10 minutes, 2 secondskeyword when. And now once we are done defining all the conditions we can go and specify an else keyword. The else
8:10:098 hours, 10 minutes, 9 secondscan introduce the default value and it is optional. You can go and skip it. So the value of the else or the default going to be used only if all the
8:10:188 hours, 10 minutes, 18 secondscondition failed. So that means all our conditions are not true and nothing is fulfilled then SQL going to go and use the value from the else. So it is the
8:10:278 hours, 10 minutes, 27 secondsdefault value that's going to be used if all conditions are false. So those are the keywords that you must use inside each case statement. So we have case
8:10:358 hours, 10 minutes, 35 secondswhen then and only the else is an optional. So you can go and use it or skip it. So this is the main structure
8:10:438 hours, 10 minutes, 43 secondsand the syntax of each case statement. Now let's have a very simple
8:10:508 hours, 10 minutes, 50 secondsexample in order to understand how SQL execute the case statements behind the scenes. All right, let's have this very simple example where we have only one
8:10:588 hours, 10 minutes, 58 secondscondition. So as you can see in the syntax, it starts with case and end and then we have only one condition and we are evaluating here the sales. So the
8:11:078 hours, 11 minutes, 7 secondscondition says if the sales is higher than 50 then show as a result the value of high. So it's very simple only one

8:11:248 hours, 11 minutes, 24 secondslogic and see what the output going to be with the case statement. So let's do it one by one. Let's start with the first sales. It is 60. So here we're

8:11:418 hours, 11 minutes, 41 secondstrue and we're going to get in the output the value of high. So here we're going to get the value high in the output. So that means the first sales is
8:11:508 hours, 11 minutes, 50 secondsfulfilling the requirement the condition and SQL going to give us the value from this condition. All right. So now SQL going to go to the next value and we're
8:11:578 hours, 11 minutes, 57 secondsgoing to start evaluating the 30. Now we're going to ask the same question the same condition is 30 higher than 50.
8:12:038 hours, 12 minutes, 3 secondsWell no. So that means in the output for this condition we will get false. So we will take the path of the false. Now if
8:12:118 hours, 12 minutes, 11 secondsyou take the path of the false we will not get any value. Right? So that means the output going to be a null. So the
8:12:188 hours, 12 minutes, 18 secondsoutput for the 30 is null. And that's because we didn't define in our logic anything about the default option. So we don't have here an else. And this is
8:12:278 hours, 12 minutes, 27 secondswhat going to happen. If you don't use else, you will get a null in the output for the case statement. So now let's move to the next one. It's going to be
8:12:348 hours, 12 minutes, 34 secondsthe same thing. So 15 is smaller than 50. So it's not fulfilling the condition. And as well we're going to get a null. And for the last one since
8:12:438 hours, 12 minutes, 43 secondsit's null we will get as well a null since it will not fulfill the condition.
8:12:478 hours, 12 minutes, 47 secondsSo now after evaluating all those sales only the first sales is fulfilling that condition and that's why we have only one value the high. All right. So now
8:12:558 hours, 12 minutes, 55 secondslet's keep moving and adding stuff to our case statements. Now we are adding a second condition. So it says after checking the sales whether it's higher
8:13:038 hours, 13 minutes, 3 secondsthan 50 and it fails check again the sales whether it's higher than 20. If yes then show the value of medium. So
8:13:118 hours, 13 minutes, 11 secondsnow in our workflow we are adding a second condition to be checked if the first one is false. So now let's go and evaluate our sales again and check the
8:13:208 hours, 13 minutes, 20 secondsoutput the first one the 60. So as you can see the 60 is higher than 50. So we are fulfilling the first requirement
8:13:278 hours, 13 minutes, 27 secondsthat's why we will get the value of high. So it's same like before. So here we're going to get high in the output.
8:13:338 hours, 13 minutes, 33 secondsAnd now here very important to understand one thing is that SQL didn't evaluate here in this scenario the second condition. So SQL didn't waste
8:13:428 hours, 13 minutes, 42 secondsany time by checking the other condition. It skipped everything once it get a true from one condition. So this is exactly how SQL process the case win.
8:13:508 hours, 13 minutes, 50 secondsIt going to check each conditions from top to down and once it finds a true it's going to stop everything immediately and throw the value from
8:13:588 hours, 13 minutes, 58 secondsthis condition and it will not evaluate any other conditions. So now it's going to go and jump to the next value. We are at the value of 30. So let's evaluate the conditions. Is 30 higher than 50?
8:14:108 hours, 14 minutes, 10 secondsWell, it's not. So it's false. So now what can happen is going to go and jump to the next condition and start evaluating the second one whether it's
8:14:178 hours, 14 minutes, 17 secondstrue or false. So now we're going to check here. Is 30 higher than 20? Well,
8:14:218 hours, 14 minutes, 21 secondsyes. So it's going to be fulfilled and we will get the value of medium. So it's going to stop everything and show in the
8:14:288 hours, 14 minutes, 28 secondsoutput for this value the medium. So we're going to get medium here. So in this scenario, we have evaluated both of
8:14:368 hours, 14 minutes, 36 secondsthe conditions that we have in the case statement. Now it's going to go to the third one. We have 15. Is 15 higher than 50? Will no. So we will get to false for
8:14:458 hours, 14 minutes, 45 secondsthe first condition. Then it's going to go and jump to the second condition and check it. Is 15 higher than 20? Will as well no. So now what going to happen?
8:14:548 hours, 14 minutes, 54 secondsThe false going to be activated over here. And we will not get any value as a return. So we will get the value of null
8:15:018 hours, 15 minutes, 1 secondin the output. And now for the last one we have null. We will get as well null because it will not fulfill any of those conditions and that's because we didn't
8:15:108 hours, 15 minutes, 10 secondsdefine an else in the case statement. So if we define these conditions like this,
8:15:148 hours, 15 minutes, 14 secondswe will get the category medium for the 30. And this is how SQL evaluate multiple conditions in the case statement. All right. Now we're going to
8:15:228 hours, 15 minutes, 22 secondsgo to the final form of our case statements and we're going to go and add an else. So we're going to have a default value. So we are seeing here if
8:15:298 hours, 15 minutes, 29 secondsthe sales is not higher than 50 or higher than 20 then show a default value as low. So that means any sales that is
8:15:378 hours, 15 minutes, 37 secondsequal or smaller than 20 going to get the value of low. And now very interesting if you check the workflow over here you can see that we have now a
8:15:458 hours, 15 minutes, 45 secondsvalue for each path. So for the first condition we're going to get high for the second one medium. And if nothing is fulfilled we're going to get always the
8:15:538 hours, 15 minutes, 53 secondsvalue of low. So there is no way in this chart to get any nulls. Right? So let's go and evaluate again our values. I think you already get it. The 60 is
8:16:028 hours, 16 minutes, 2 secondsfulfilling the first requirement and SQL going to stop everything immediately and just show the value of high. So on the

8:16:378 hours, 16 minutes, 37 secondsscale going to move to the next one. And here things going to get interesting. So the value of 15. We're going to evaluate the first condition. Is it higher than 50? Well, no. Is it higher than 20?
8:16:468 hours, 16 minutes, 46 secondsWell, no. So now we are in scenario where none of those conditions are true.

8:16:588 hours, 16 minutes, 58 secondsget the value of low. So in the output we will not get this time a null because we have else we will get the value of low. The same thing now for the null.
8:17:088 hours, 17 minutes, 8 secondsNull will not fulfill the first condition as well the second condition and that's why we will get as well the value from the else. So here in the
8:17:178 hours, 17 minutes, 17 secondsoutput we will get as well the value of low. So now as you can see if you use an else inside the case statements you will make sure that there will be no nulls in

8:23:068 hours, 23 minutes, 6 secondsmust be matching. So what this means if we check again our example over here we can see that the result of each condition is a string. So as you can see
8:23:148 hours, 23 minutes, 14 secondswe have here high, medium and low and all of those informations are following the same data type. So it is correct. So now if I go and break this rule for
8:23:228 hours, 23 minutes, 22 secondsexample after this then let's have the value two. So now we have a number and we have characters. So let's go and
8:23:298 hours, 23 minutes, 29 secondsexecute it. And now of course we're going to get an error because now SSQL is trying to convert the value low to an integer which is incorrect. So the data
8:23:378 hours, 23 minutes, 37 secondstypes of the output of the result must be matching and that's not only include the value after the then but also the
8:23:458 hours, 23 minutes, 45 secondsvalue after the else because this value is as well part of the output. So let's have here again medium. And now let's go
8:23:528 hours, 23 minutes, 52 secondsand change this to let's say one. So let's go and excuse it again. Isl going to throw an error because this is an integer in number and the others are
8:24:018 hours, 24 minutes, 1 secondstring characters. So this is the rule of using the case statement. The data types after then and after else must be matching. And if you ask me whether
8:24:098 hours, 24 minutes, 9 secondsthere is restriction about where you can use the case statement in which clauses you can use it everywhere in select, in joins, from, where, group by, order by,
8:24:198 hours, 24 minutes, 19 secondseverywhere. So there are no restrictions and we have only this one rule.

8:31:528 hours, 31 minutes, 52 secondsuse only one column and it's only for the equal operator. So that means only for these scenarios you can go and use
8:31:598 hours, 31 minutes, 59 secondsthe quick format. If things get a little bit complicated where you have to mix and make complex logic, you cannot use the quick formats. So I would say if you
8:32:068 hours, 32 minutes, 6 secondsare sure that the logic will not get complicated and you can stay always with the same column, you can go with the quick format. But I would recommend always to go with the full format
8:32:158 hours, 32 minutes, 15 secondsbecause for one simple reason if you add one small logic you have to go and rewrite the whole case statements back to the full format in order to add any
8:32:248 hours, 32 minutes, 24 secondssmall logic. But of course there is nothing wrong using the quick form in order to do the case statements if the logic can stay static and you are sure

8:50:398 hours, 50 minutes, 39 secondsgo. Okay guys, so now let's start with the first question. What are SQL window functions? They are functions that allows you to do calculations like
8:50:488 hours, 50 minutes, 48 secondsaggregations but on top of subset of data without losing the level of details of the rows. So it is something very
8:50:568 hours, 50 minutes, 56 secondssimilar to the group pi but here we have special case you don't lose the level of details. So now in order to understand the definition let's have a very simple
8:51:048 hours, 51 minutes, 4 secondsexample. Okay. So now let's understand how SQL works with the group by clouds.
8:51:088 hours, 51 minutes, 8 secondsLet's say that we have the very simple example. We have four orders. two orders for the cabs and two order for the gloves. And let's say that I would like
8:51:168 hours, 51 minutes, 16 secondsto see the total sales for each products. So now if we decided to use the group by what SQL going to do going to take the first two orders for the
8:51:258 hours, 51 minutes, 25 secondscaps and put it in one row. So in the output we're going to have only one row for the caps with the total sales of 40.
8:51:328 hours, 51 minutes, 32 secondsAnd the same thing going to happen for the gloves. So we're going to take the two rows of the gloves from the input and in the output we're going to have only one row for the gloves. So that
8:51:408 hours, 51 minutes, 40 secondsmeans the number of rows going to be depending on the number of products we have on our data. We have two products,
8:51:468 hours, 51 minutes, 46 secondswe get two rows. So that means SQL is really like smashing or squeezing the results in the output. And this is
8:51:538 hours, 51 minutes, 53 secondsexactly what the grouper does to our data. It aggregate the rows, aggregate the data into different level of details. So now on the left side we see
8:52:028 hours, 52 minutes, 2 secondsfour rows. On the right side we have two rows and with that we are losing some details in the results. But still we have solved the tasks. So now let's see
8:52:108 hours, 52 minutes, 10 secondswhat going to happen if you use window function in SQL. Okay. So now we have the same data and as with the same task we have to find the total sales for each
8:52:178 hours, 52 minutes, 17 secondsproduct. Now if you use window function SQL going to do the following. It going to go and execute each rows individually
8:52:258 hours, 52 minutes, 25 secondsfrom each others. So what going to happen it start with the first row the order ID one. In the output we're going to get as well the same stuff the order
8:52:338 hours, 52 minutes, 33 secondsID one the same row but we will get the total sales for the caps. So here the total sales is going to be 10 30 we will
8:52:408 hours, 52 minutes, 40 secondsget 40. Then it's going to jump to the second row and it's going to process it as well. So in the output we will get the order ID two the product caps and as
8:52:488 hours, 52 minutes, 48 secondswell we have the same aggregation since we are talking about the same product.
8:52:528 hours, 52 minutes, 52 secondsSo we will get 40. Then it's going to go to the third order and here we have the gloves. So in the output again we have the order ID 3 the product gloves and
8:53:008 hours, 53 minutesthe total sales this time going to be 5 + 20 so we will get 25 then it goes to the last row to the order ID number four
8:53:088 hours, 53 minutes, 8 secondsin the output we're going to get four gloves and as well 25. So now we can notice that if you use the window function you will not lose the level of
8:53:168 hours, 53 minutes, 16 secondsdetails of your data. So we are doing something called rowle calculations. So if in input data we have four orders in
8:53:238 hours, 53 minutes, 23 secondsthe output we're going to get four orders and as well we will get our aggregations correctly. So now if you compare both of the methods side by side
8:53:318 hours, 53 minutes, 31 secondswe can see that we are solving the same task. So we are finding the total sales for each products but with the group by
8:53:388 hours, 53 minutes, 38 secondswe are smashing squeezing the results from four orders into two rows one row for each order. So that means with the
8:53:468 hours, 53 minutes, 46 secondsgroup by the granularity is changing right in the input the order ID is controlling the level of details but in the output of the group by the product
8:53:548 hours, 53 minutes, 54 secondsis controlling the level of details. So we have different granularity but in the other hand with the window functions we are still able to do aggregations but we
8:54:028 hours, 54 minutes, 2 secondsare not losing the level of details. So the granularity of the input going to be the same like the output in the results.
8:54:098 hours, 54 minutes, 9 secondsSo this is exactly the main difference between the group eye and the window function. If you want just to do simple aggregations, then go with the group by.
8:54:178 hours, 54 minutes, 17 secondsBut if you care about the level of details and you need to add more details to your results, then you can go with the window function where you can do aggregations plus having more details.
8:54:278 hours, 54 minutes, 27 secondsAnd now if you go and compare the functions between the window and the group by, we can find that both of them has exactly the same functions for the aggregations. So we have the count, sum,
8:54:368 hours, 54 minutes, 36 secondsaverage, min, max. And here comes another difference between the window and the group by. The group I has only the aggregate functions. So that's it.
8:54:448 hours, 54 minutes, 44 secondsBut in the window functions, we have way more functions to use for analytics. So for example, we have the ranking functions. And we have here another
8:54:528 hours, 54 minutes, 52 secondsgroup of functions for the value or we call it analytical functions. So that means in the SQL window, we have a lot of functions. We can cover a lot of
8:55:008 hours, 55 minutesanalytical use cases and advanced complex stuff. But with the group by we,

9:23:039 hours, 23 minutes, 3 secondsworks and we're going to do it step by step. So don't worry about it. All right. So now let's understand what is going on with the frame close from the basics. So now if you do aggregations
9:23:129 hours, 23 minutes, 12 secondsand you don't use window function you're going to consider the entire data or rows inside the table. But what we can do we can go and divide the data using
9:23:209 hours, 23 minutes, 20 secondspartition pi to a window. So for example here we have window one and window two.
9:23:259 hours, 23 minutes, 25 secondsNow if you go and do aggregations all the rows in the window one going to be aggregated and then scale going to go to the window two and aggregate all the
9:23:339 hours, 23 minutes, 33 secondsrows. What we can do in scale is that we can say you know what I don't want all rows inside the window. I want a subset
9:23:409 hours, 23 minutes, 40 secondsof rows inside the window. So what we are doing over here is that we have those two windows but we specify a scope
9:23:479 hours, 23 minutes, 47 secondsor we specify subset of data from each window to be involved in the aggregations. And of course not only aggregations we can do ranking other
9:23:559 hours, 23 minutes, 55 secondsstuff. So I mean calculations. So here like we have a window inside a window.
9:23:599 hours, 23 minutes, 59 secondsSo we are defining a scope of rows. Not all rows should be involved in the calculation but only specific subset of
9:24:079 hours, 24 minutes, 7 secondsdata. And we can do that using the frame clause. So again the partition by you can use it in order to divide the entire
9:24:149 hours, 24 minutes, 14 secondsdata set into multiple windows. And now for the frame close. If you don't want to consider all the rows within each window in the calculation, you want to
9:24:239 hours, 24 minutes, 23 secondsfocus and specify only a subset of data within each window. Then you going to go and use the frame close. All right. So now let's go and understand the syntax
9:24:319 hours, 24 minutes, 31 secondsof the frame close. Let's have the following example. We are saying the window function is the average of sales and then we define the window. So we have the first partition by categories,
9:24:419 hours, 24 minutes, 41 secondsorder by order dates and then we have the frame close. It's going to be the following rows between current row and unbounded preceding. This is the frame
9:24:509 hours, 24 minutes, 50 secondstypes and we have two types. We have the rows and groups. Then we have like between and range. So the first range going to be the frame boundary, the
9:24:599 hours, 24 minutes, 59 secondslower value. And here it accepts three types of keywords like the current row or number of preceding or the unbounded
9:25:079 hours, 25 minutes, 7 secondspreceding. And then we have another frame boundary. It's going to be the higher values and it accepts the following stuff. We can use the current row in following or unbounded following.

9:47:239 hours, 47 minutes, 23 secondsaggregated value. SQL going to go and summarize all those values and in the output you're going to find for example here the total sales it's going to be

9:48:359 hours, 48 minutes, 35 secondssum, average, min, max. And as you can see, only the count accepts all data types as an expression or arguments. All

10:31:5410 hours, 31 minutes, 54 seconds[Music]
10:31:5710 hours, 31 minutes, 57 secondsextremes. All right everyone so now we're going to focus on very important use case. One of the must know use cases for data aggregations is doing running
10:32:0510 hours, 32 minutes, 5 secondstotal and rolling total. These two concepts are very important for data analyszis and doing reporting that you
10:32:1210 hours, 32 minutes, 12 secondsmust know. The key use case for those two concept is to do tracking. For example, we can go and track the current
10:32:1910 hours, 32 minutes, 19 secondstotal sales with the target sales in our business. And as well, it's great in order to do historical analyszis for the trends. Okay. So now the question is
10:32:2810 hours, 32 minutes, 28 secondswhat is running a rolling total. They are basically very similar. They're going to go and aggregate a sequence of members and the aggregation going to get
10:32:3610 hours, 32 minutes, 36 secondsupdated each time we add a new member to the sequence. A sequence could be like a time sequence. That's why we call this
10:32:4310 hours, 32 minutes, 43 secondstype an analyzes over time. So now we still have the question, what is the difference between the running and the rolling totals. The running total going
10:32:5110 hours, 32 minutes, 51 secondsto go and aggregate everything from the beginning until the current data point without dropping off any old data. Where
10:32:5810 hours, 32 minutes, 58 secondson the other hand in the rolling total it going to go and focus on a specific time window like the last 30 days or the last two monthses and each time we add a
10:33:0710 hours, 33 minutes, 7 secondsnew member or a new data point to the window we will be dropping off the oldest data point in the window and with this we're going to get the effect of
10:33:1610 hours, 33 minutes, 16 secondsrolling or let's say shifting window okay I totally understand if this might be complicated now let's go and have very simple example in order to
10:33:2310 hours, 33 minutes, 23 secondsunderstand this concept and as well how we can solve it using SQL all right guys so now We have very simple example. We have the months and sales and we have it
10:33:3110 hours, 33 minutes, 31 secondstwice because I want to show you side by side how SQL works with the running total and the rolling total. So now what is the task on the left side? We want to
10:33:3910 hours, 33 minutes, 39 secondsfind the running total of sales for each month and on the right side we would like to find three month rolling total

10:53:0310 hours, 53 minutes, 3 secondsand in the next step it's going to be very important. We will learn how to rank our data using window functions. So let's
10:53:1210 hours, 53 minutes, 12 secondsgo. All right. So now let's say that we have the following data. We have products and their sales. If you want now to go and rank your products first
10:53:2110 hours, 53 minutes, 21 secondsyou have to sort the data based on something like for example ranking the products based on their sales. So that means SQL first is going to go and start
10:53:2910 hours, 53 minutes, 29 secondssorting your data from the highest to the lowest. So sorting the data is always the first thing SQL has to do
10:53:3610 hours, 53 minutes, 36 secondsbefore ranking anything. Now in order to rank our data we have two methods. The first method we call it the integer based ranking. So that means SQL going
10:53:4510 hours, 53 minutes, 45 secondsto go and assign for each row an integer a whole number based on the position of the row. So now by looking to the
10:53:5210 hours, 53 minutes, 52 secondsexample the first row we have the product E with the sales 70 it's going to be rank number one then the next row
10:54:0010 hours, 54 minutesthe product B with 30 sales we will get the rank number two then the next one going to be three four and the last one going to be five. So that means SQL here
10:54:0810 hours, 54 minutes, 8 secondsis assigning an integer for each row based on their position in the sorted list. So this method we call it integer
10:54:1610 hours, 54 minutes, 16 secondsbased ranking. Now let's go to the second method we have the percentagebased ranking. So in this methods going to go first and calculate
10:54:2410 hours, 54 minutes, 24 secondsthe relative position of the row compared to all others and then assign a percentage for each row. So here in the
10:54:3110 hours, 54 minutes, 31 secondsoutput is going to start assigning percentages instead of integer and we're going to have a scale from 0 to one. So now if you go and compare both of the
10:54:4010 hours, 54 minutes, 40 secondsmethods you can see that on the left side on the integer base ranking we have discrete distinct values. So it starts
10:54:4710 hours, 54 minutes, 47 secondsfrom 1 then 2 3 and end up in this example by five. So it really depends on how many rows do we have in the results.
10:54:5510 hours, 54 minutes, 55 secondsSo it could be five, it could be 500, 5 million and so on. But in the right side we have always the same scale from one
10:55:0210 hours, 55 minutes, 2 secondsto zero. So between 0 and one we have infinite number of data points and this scale we call it a normalized scale or
10:55:1010 hours, 55 minutes, 10 secondswe call it continuous scale continuous values. So now the question is when to use which method. So for example for the percentage based ranking it is great to answer such questions find the top 20%
10:55:2210 hours, 55 minutes, 22 secondsproducts based on their sales. So this method is a great way in order to understand the contributions of data values to the overall total and we call
10:55:3110 hours, 55 minutes, 31 secondsthis kind of analyszis a distribution analyszis where in the other hand in the integer based ranking we can answer questions like find the top three
10:55:4010 hours, 55 minutes, 40 secondsproducts. So with this question we are not interesting about the contributions of each product to the overall total. We are just interested in the position of
10:55:4910 hours, 55 minutes, 49 secondsthe value within a list. So this is as well very commonly used analyzes and reporting. We call it top button in analyzers. So now let's group up our
10:55:5710 hours, 55 minutes, 57 secondsranking functions based on those two methods. For the first group in the integer based ranking we have four functions. Row number rank d rank and
10:56:0610 hours, 56 minutes, 6 secondsinile. But in the other hand we have only two functions that generate percentage based ranking. We have the cumid list and as well the percentile.
10:56:1510 hours, 56 minutes, 15 secondsSo now that was an introduction an overview of those methods and how we group up those ranking functions. Next we're going to go and learn about the
10:56:2310 hours, 56 minutes, 23 secondssyntax of the ranking functions. Most of them follow the same rules. So for example we start always with the function name. So we have here the rank.
10:56:3110 hours, 56 minutes, 31 secondsBut as you can see we don't use any expressions. So they don't allow you to use any argument inside it. It must be empty. So this is the first rule using
10:56:4010 hours, 56 minutes, 40 secondsrank functions. Then about the definition of the window as usual the partition by it is an optional thing.
10:56:4610 hours, 56 minutes, 46 secondsYou can use it or leave it. And now to the second part we have the order by it is as well required. So you must order the data or sort your data in order to

11:27:3411 hours, 27 minutes, 34 secondsnumber, rank and dense rank. Now we're going to talk about the inile. Okay. So what is inile? Intile in
11:27:4411 hours, 27 minutes, 44 secondsSQL is very simple. It's going to go and divide your rows, your data into specific number of almost equal groups
11:27:5111 hours, 27 minutes, 51 secondsor sometimes we call them packets. So now in order to understand this and how it scale works with this function, we're going to have a very simple example. So
11:27:5811 hours, 27 minutes, 58 secondslet's go. Okay, we have the following setup. We have four rows for sales and we would like to divide it into two groups or into two buckets. So in order
11:28:0711 hours, 28 minutes, 7 secondsto do that we can use the entile function. It has different syntax than the other ranking functions. So it starts with entile then we must define a
11:28:1611 hours, 28 minutes, 16 secondsnumber. So we cannot leave it empty like the other ranking. So here we have two buckets then over and here again we have
11:28:2311 hours, 28 minutes, 23 secondsto sort the data. So it is must order by sales descending from the highest to the lowest. So now as usual SQL going to go and sort the data. We have it already
11:28:3211 hours, 28 minutes, 32 secondssorted in this example. Then it going to start assigning each of those rows into buckets. But SQL first has to calculate
11:28:4011 hours, 28 minutes, 40 secondsthe bucket size. So how many rows we can like insert inside each bucket. So the calculation is very simple. It says the
11:28:4711 hours, 28 minutes, 47 secondsbucket size equals to the number of rows divided by the number of buckets. So what is the number of rows here? We have
11:28:5511 hours, 28 minutes, 55 secondsfour rows, right? So we have four over here. Then the number of buckets we define it in the syntax of the query. So
11:29:0211 hours, 29 minutes, 2 secondshere we defined two buckets. We need two groups. So that means we are dividing four by two. And the size of the bucket
11:29:0911 hours, 29 minutes, 9 secondsgoing to be two. So now with this SQL is ready and going to start assigning each row to a bucket. So it's going to start on the top. The first one going to be in the bucket number one. Then go to the
11:29:1911 hours, 29 minutes, 19 secondsnext one. It's going to say okay we still have enough space in the bucket.
11:29:2211 hours, 29 minutes, 22 secondsRight? So it's going to sign as well to one. But with this we reach the maximum number of rows within each bucket. So the next row going to be assigned to
11:29:3111 hours, 29 minutes, 31 secondsanother bucket. So it's going to be two and the last one going to be as well too. So as you can see it's very simple.
11:29:3711 hours, 29 minutes, 37 secondsWe have just assigned our sales based on the sorting of course into two buckets.
11:29:4111 hours, 29 minutes, 41 secondsThese two sales belongs to the bucket number one and the other two belongs to the bucket number two. Very easy. So that was very straightforward because we
11:29:4911 hours, 29 minutes, 49 secondsare dividing even numbers and we got perfectly sized buckets. But now what going to happen if we have an odd number? So we have here five instead of
11:29:5711 hours, 29 minutes, 57 secondsfour. So the bucket size going to be dividing five by two. We're going to get 2.5. And now of course SQL will not go and divide like two half for each
11:30:0611 hours, 30 minutes, 6 secondsbucket. Then we are splitting this into two packets. Of course this will not be working. We should has now a bucket with three and another bucket with two. So now the rule in SQL make it very clear.
11:30:1711 hours, 30 minutes, 17 secondsIt says larger groups comes first then smaller. So that means if we have here an even number like this, the larger
11:30:2411 hours, 30 minutes, 24 secondsgroup going to be the first group. So that's going to look like this. It's going to like reset everything. So let's
11:30:3211 hours, 30 minutes, 32 secondssee what's going to happen. The first one going to be one. The second one has bill one. The third one going to be as well one. So it going to has a larger
11:30:4011 hours, 30 minutes, 40 secondspackage than the second one. Then the rest going to be two. So as you can see the larger group comes first then the
11:30:4811 hours, 30 minutes, 48 secondssmaller. And this is how a scale going to work. if you have odd numbers. So you don't have here perfectly sized buckets.
11:30:5411 hours, 30 minutes, 54 secondsYou have approximately or roughly equally sized buckets. So this is how the intel works. Now let's go back to scale in order to practice this

11:41:5011 hours, 41 minutes, 50 secondsone. And this is really amazing in order to do distribution analyszis. So those functions going to consider in their calculation the overall total the whole
11:41:5911 hours, 41 minutes, 59 secondssize of the data set which can help us in order to find out the contribution of each value to the overall total. And now

11:42:4611 hours, 42 minutes, 46 secondshow SQL works with this function. So let's go. All right. Again we have our very simple example of the sales and we have the following query. So dist
11:42:5511 hours, 42 minutes, 55 secondsthen we don't give any argument inside it. So it's going to be empty and the window going to be like usual order by sales descending from the highest to the
11:43:0211 hours, 43 minutes, 2 secondslowest and the order by is must. So the first step is SQL going to go and sort the data. We have it already sorted from the highest to the lowest. So now the

12:28:0012 hours, 28 minutesframe going to get extended. So now moving to the last one and with that we will get the whole data set inside the frame and the first cell is going to be

12:28:1612 hours, 28 minutes, 16 secondsfive. So now if you go and compare them side by side you see that on the left side the task is solved and everything is working correctly right. So we have
12:28:2412 hours, 28 minutes, 24 secondsfor each row always the sales of the first row and what is the first row it is January. So we have everywhere a 20
12:28:3112 hours, 28 minutes, 31 secondswhich is correct. But now if you check the right side you can see there is something wrong right? We are getting not the last value. We should always get
12:28:3912 hours, 28 minutes, 39 secondsApril right? We should have here everywhere a five. So we have here exactly the same result as the sales. So it's really useless to use it like this,
12:28:4712 hours, 28 minutes, 47 secondsright? And that's of course because SQL is using the default definition of the window frame. Last value is the only function from all window functions that
12:28:5512 hours, 28 minutes, 55 secondsyou cannot use the default frame definition. You have to go and customize the frame definition in order to get the effect of the last value. For the first
12:29:0412 hours, 29 minutes, 4 secondsvalue, everything is working. If you're using a default frame, if you are not specifying anything, but for the last value, you will not get the effect correctly without customizing the frame
12:29:1312 hours, 29 minutes, 13 secondswindow. So my friends, you can go and use the first value function like all other window functions without defining a frame. You can go with the default and
12:29:2112 hours, 29 minutes, 21 secondsyou will get the effect of the first value, but the last value you have to go and define a frame. So let's see how we can solve that. All right. So now in order to solve this, we going to define
12:29:2912 hours, 29 minutes, 29 secondsthe frame like this. It's going to be the rows between the current row and the unbounded following. So we just switch
12:29:3612 hours, 29 minutes, 36 secondsthings around. So now let's see how this going to work. Now of course it's going to go and sort the data and so on. Now it's still going to have a pointer to
12:29:4412 hours, 29 minutes, 44 secondsthe unbounded following. So it's going to point always to the last row in our data set and then it's going to proceed step by step. So the first row going to
12:29:5212 hours, 29 minutes, 52 secondsbe like this and the frame going to be the whole thing, right? So from the current row until the unbounded following. So what is the last value the last row? It's going to be the five,
12:30:0312 hours, 30 minutes, 3 secondsright? The April. So we will get in the output five. Now let's proceed to the next value. The frame going to be shorter and smaller. And what is the
12:30:1112 hours, 30 minutes, 11 secondslast value? It's going to be as well the five. Right? So now we jump to the next one. And the frame going to be like this. What is the last value? As well
12:30:1912 hours, 30 minutes, 19 secondsfive. And then we will get the last value like this. Current row is equal to the unbounded following. We have only one row and it's going to be as well
12:30:2712 hours, 30 minutes, 27 secondsfive. So as you can see it's very simple just fix the frame clause and you will get the last value working as expected.

12:48:0112 hours, 48 minutes, 1 seconddatabase architecture because I want you to understand what happens behind the scenes and how the database execute the
12:48:0912 hours, 48 minutes, 9 secondsqueries from these five techniques. So by understanding this architecture you will understand how things works. So
12:48:1612 hours, 48 minutes, 16 secondslet's go. For each story there are two sides. We have the server side and the client side. In the client side it's like for example you you are writing an
12:48:2412 hours, 48 minutes, 24 secondsSQL query for a specific purpose. Now in the server side we have many things. So the server is where the database lives
12:48:3212 hours, 48 minutes, 32 secondsand it has many components like the database engine. The database engine is the brain of the database that handles different operations like storing,
12:48:4212 hours, 48 minutes, 42 secondsretrieving and managing data in the database. So each time you execute a query, the database engine going to take care of it. And now in the database we
12:48:5112 hours, 48 minutes, 51 secondshave very important component that is the storage and the two main types of storage in a database are disk storage
12:48:5812 hours, 48 minutes, 58 secondsand cache. The disk storage is like a long-term memory where the data is stored permanently. So it's like the
12:49:0512 hours, 49 minutes, 5 secondsdisk at your PC. It stores the data permanently even if you turn off the system. And one important feature of the disk is that it can stores a lot of
12:49:1412 hours, 49 minutes, 14 secondsdata. But the disadvantage of the disk storage is that it is slow. So it is slow to write and to read. Now in the
12:49:2112 hours, 49 minutes, 21 secondsother hand we have the cache is a short-term memory where the data is stored temporary. It's like the RAMs at your PC. It holds the most frequently
12:49:3012 hours, 49 minutes, 30 secondsused data. So the database can access it quickly in order to retrieve data. And the big advantage of the cache is that it is fast. So it is very fast for the
12:49:3912 hours, 49 minutes, 39 secondsdatabase to retrieve data from the cache compared to the disk. But the disadvantage of the cache, the data is stored there only for short period. So
12:49:4712 hours, 49 minutes, 47 secondsit's like tradeoff between the speed and how much data you can store and how long. Now let's talk about the disk storage. This is very important in
12:49:5512 hours, 49 minutes, 55 secondsdatabases. There are typically three types of storage areas. There we have the user data, the system catalog and
12:50:0212 hours, 50 minutes, 2 secondsthe temporary data and each storage type has a different purpose. So what is user data storage? It is the main content of
12:50:1012 hours, 50 minutes, 10 secondsthe database. So it stores the actual data all the informations that are relevant for the users. So it's stored
12:50:1812 hours, 50 minutes, 18 secondsthere all the important data that the users cares about. So this is the storage where the users are interacting all the time. So where do we find the
12:50:2612 hours, 50 minutes, 26 secondsuser data? If you go to our database sales DB and then you go to the tables now we find all these tables that we are already used the customers employees
12:50:3412 hours, 50 minutes, 34 secondsorders and so on those tables are the user data. So now if I go and say select
12:50:4112 hours, 50 minutes, 41 secondsfrom sales orders and all those informations that we are seeing now are the users data. So this is what we users
12:50:4912 hours, 50 minutes, 49 secondsactually care about. All other stuff that we see inside databases as a user we don't care about it. We care only about our data. But in the database, we
12:50:5812 hours, 50 minutes, 58 secondsdon't have only the user data. We have many other informations. So this is what we mean with the user data storage. Now what is system catalog?
12:51:1012 hours, 51 minutes, 10 secondsThis is the internal storage for the database for its own information. So it's like a blueprint that keeps
12:51:1712 hours, 51 minutes, 17 secondstracking everything about the database itself. So that means the main purpose of the system catalog is that it holds
12:51:2512 hours, 51 minutes, 25 secondsthe metadata informations about the database. So what is a metadata?
12:51:3012 hours, 51 minutes, 30 secondsMetadata is data about data. Now let's understand what this means. What we have done so far is that we have created a
12:51:3712 hours, 51 minutes, 37 secondstable called customers and we have defined inside it like multiple columns like the customer ID, first name, last name and then we have inserted our data
12:51:4512 hours, 51 minutes, 45 secondsinside this table. So we have inserted five customers. So those informations are my data. I have created those
12:51:5212 hours, 51 minutes, 52 secondsinformations and stored it inside the database. That's why we call it the user data. So nothing so far is new. So now
12:51:5912 hours, 51 minutes, 59 secondswhat happens behind the scenes is that the database server will not only store the user data that you have provided but
12:52:0612 hours, 52 minutes, 6 secondsalso it's going to go and store a different type of data inside the database and this data is the metadata.
12:52:1312 hours, 52 minutes, 13 secondsSo the database server going to store the metadata of the customer's table and it going to look like this. There is like a table name, there is a column
12:52:2212 hours, 52 minutes, 22 secondsnames and those are the column names that you have defined inside your database and those are the column names that you have defined as you are
12:52:3112 hours, 52 minutes, 31 secondscreating the table customers and it's going to store as well additional informations like which data type like the customer ID is int and the last name
12:52:3912 hours, 52 minutes, 39 secondsis v charts and many other informations like the length of the column and whether the column is nullable or not.

12:52:5412 hours, 52 minutes, 54 secondsmetadata we can find a lot of informations about not only the tables and columns but as well about the schemas and the database. So you can
12:53:0212 hours, 53 minutes, 2 secondsfind a full catalog about the structure of your database. Basic table the customers table it contains data about
12:53:0912 hours, 53 minutes, 9 secondsthe actual data. So it stores data about the customers. But the metadata of the customers table contains data about
12:53:1712 hours, 53 minutes, 17 secondsdata. So in the databases each table that you are using in order to store your data has a table twin that
12:53:2412 hours, 53 minutes, 24 secondsdescribes the structure of your data. So this is what we mean with a system catalog or a metadata. And now you might
12:53:3112 hours, 53 minutes, 31 secondsask where I can find all those system catalog and metadata inside our client here. Well, you cannot navigate through those informations in the object
12:53:4012 hours, 53 minutes, 40 secondsexplorer like we used to do for the user data. But you can find those informations in a special hidden schema

12:55:0712 hours, 55 minutes, 7 secondstwo. So maybe I was trying to test something. I can go now and clean up stuff right and this is exactly why the database maintain such a catalog. It
12:55:1612 hours, 55 minutes, 16 secondshelps the database to quickly find the structure of each table and of each column. and it helps me as well as a user to browse the catalog of the
12:55:2412 hours, 55 minutes, 24 secondsdatabase. So for example I can go over here and say okay let's get a distinct table name. So with that I will get a

12:55:3912 hours, 55 minutes, 39 secondsdone. So metadata are awesome. Now we come to the third
12:55:4512 hours, 55 minutes, 45 secondsstorage that temporary data storage. It is a temporary space used by the database for short-term task like
12:55:5312 hours, 55 minutes, 53 secondsprocessing a query or sorting data. And once these tasks are done, what going to happen? The database going to go and clean up the storage. And now of course
12:56:0112 hours, 56 minutes, 1 secondthe question is where we can find these temporary tables that is using the temporary storage in the disk. Well actually if you go to the object
12:56:0812 hours, 56 minutes, 8 secondsexplorer you will not find it inside our database sales DB but you will find it inside the system databases. Now since
12:56:1612 hours, 56 minutes, 16 secondswe are working locally we have the full access to everything inside the SQL server. But in real projects if you are just a user or let's say developer you
12:56:2512 hours, 56 minutes, 25 secondswill not have access to the system databases only for the database administrators. But now we are working on the local copy. So let's go to the
12:56:3312 hours, 56 minutes, 33 secondssystem database and here you have a special database from the SQL server called temp DB. And if you go inside it
