12:56:4912 hours, 56 minutes, 49 secondscreate any temporary tables that's why it's empty. But once you start creating temporary tables you will find those tables underneath this folder. We will
12:56:5812 hours, 56 minutes, 58 secondslearn about the temporary tables in the next sections. So these are the main component of the database architecture.
12:57:0912 hours, 57 minutes, 9 secondsSo now let's have an example. Now we have a table called orders that is stored inside the user storage and the metadata of this table is stored in the
12:57:1612 hours, 57 minutes, 16 secondscatalog. So now let's say that you are at the client side and you write a simple select query in order to select the data of the orders. So now that
12:57:2412 hours, 57 minutes, 24 secondsquery is sent to the server in order to be executed and the database engine going to take the query in order to process it. So first the database engine
12:57:3212 hours, 57 minutes, 32 secondsgoing to check whether we have the data in the cache because if the data is stored in the cache then things going to be really fast and the database engine can solve the task quickly but in this
12:57:4012 hours, 57 minutes, 40 secondsscenario we don't have the orders informations in the cache that's why the database engine going to say okay it's not in the cache let's check the disk so
12:57:4812 hours, 57 minutes, 48 secondsit will find the orders information in the disk and the query going to be executed then the result of this query going to be sent back to the client side
12:57:5612 hours, 57 minutes, 56 secondswhere at the end in return you will see in the output the result of the table orders. So this is how the SQL database
12:58:0312 hours, 58 minutes, 3 secondsexecute very simple select query query is a query inside another
12:58:1312 hours, 58 minutes, 13 secondsquery. So what this means let's have a sketch to understand it. So so far what we have learned we have different database tables like the orders
12:58:2112 hours, 58 minutes, 21 secondscustomers and so on and we write a simple SQL queries like select from where. So the SQL going to retrieve data from the database tables and in the output we will get some kind of results.
12:58:3212 hours, 58 minutes, 32 secondsSo this is so far what you have done. We have done very simple queries. Now in our query we can have things little bit different. So we could have another
12:58:4012 hours, 58 minutes, 40 secondsquery that is inside our query where we do the same things like select from where. So we have now a query inside our
12:58:4712 hours, 58 minutes, 47 secondsquery and we call this embedded query we call it a sub query and the original query the first one where we have select from we call it main query. So now if
12:58:5612 hours, 58 minutes, 56 secondsyou execute the whole query what going to happen SQL first going to go and select the subquery and then it's going to execute it. So it's going to go and select and retrieve data from our
12:59:0512 hours, 59 minutes, 5 secondsdatabase tables and the result of the subquery will not be sent to the users to us. So we cannot see it. What can happen? the result can stay inside the
12:59:1412 hours, 59 minutes, 14 secondsquery as an intermediate results and then now our main query can go and start interacting with this intermediate
12:59:2212 hours, 59 minutes, 22 secondsresult from the subquery. So the main query going to do some kind of operations on top of this intermediate results and use it for filtering or
12:59:2912 hours, 59 minutes, 29 secondsjoining or any purpose and still the main query can go and query the original database tables. So now the main query
12:59:3612 hours, 59 minutes, 36 secondshas two sources for data. The original database tables and as well the result from another query. So now by looking to
12:59:4412 hours, 59 minutes, 44 secondsthis you can see the subquery is a query inside the main query and it play a role of supporter. So it supports the main
12:59:5212 hours, 59 minutes, 52 secondsquery with data and the main job of the main query is of course to get all those data and to show us at the end the final
13:00:0013 hoursresults. Now there is now two things about this intermediate results that we got as a result from the subquery. Once the execution of the query is completely
13:00:0913 hours, 9 secondsdone, what can happen is going to go and destroy this intermediate result. So it's going to totally drop it. So we will not find it anywhere. It's
13:00:1713 hours, 17 secondscompletely lost. Now the other thing about the intermediate results is that imagine you are making another query that is completely outside of the first
13:00:2513 hours, 25 secondsquery. We are selecting few tables from our database. Now you might say you know what is it possible to access the
13:00:3213 hours, 32 secondsintermediate results from the first query. So now we are talking about completely external query you cannot do that. The intermediate result of the
13:00:4013 hours, 40 secondssubquery is only locally known from the main query itself and it is not globally available for any other query. So the
13:00:4813 hours, 48 secondssubquery can be used only from the main query.
13:00:5513 hours, 55 secondsSo with that we have understood what are subqueries and now you might ask me why do we need them in the first place? Why sub queries are important? Let's have
13:01:0413 hours, 1 minute, 4 secondsthe following sketch. Now in our complex task we might have to do several stuffs in our query. Like for example the first step we have to go and join tables in
13:01:1213 hours, 1 minute, 12 secondsorder to prepare the data and then the outcome of the joins should be filtered.
13:01:1613 hours, 1 minute, 16 secondsSo this going to be our step two. And then on top of that in the step three we have to go and do transformations like maybe handling the nulls or creating new
13:01:2513 hours, 1 minute, 25 secondscolumns and many other stuff. And the last step we want to go and do data aggregations like summarizing the data or finding average. Now if you go
13:01:3213 hours, 1 minute, 32 secondsimmediately and start writing the SQL query without having a plan what can happen you're going to end up having a long complex SQL query and it's going to
13:01:4113 hours, 1 minute, 41 secondsbe really hard to write and as well to understand and read. And now what we can do instead of that we're going to go and divide our task based on those steps. So
13:01:5013 hours, 1 minute, 50 secondswe're going to write one query section for each step. For example, for joining tables we're going to have one query for filtering another one transformation
13:01:5713 hours, 1 minute, 57 secondsanother one and for the aggregation we're going to have the last query. So now since each step is like a preparation for the next step we can go
13:02:0513 hours, 2 minutes, 5 secondsand say each of those queries is a subquery. So for step one, step two,
13:02:1013 hours, 2 minutes, 10 secondsstep three, we have sub queries and they are all doing like calculations and preparations for the last step to the
13:02:1713 hours, 2 minutes, 17 secondsaggregations and we call the last step the main query and of course the whole thing can exist in one single query. So if you want to visual this like you have
13:02:2513 hours, 2 minutes, 25 secondsa subquery in circle and then this circle belongs to a bigger circle called the main query. By the way, sometimes we
13:02:3213 hours, 2 minutes, 32 secondscall the main query as the outer query and the subquery we can call it an inner query. And of course, we can have many
13:02:4013 hours, 2 minutes, 40 secondssubqueries and many small circles inside each others to form something called nested queries. So this is the main
13:02:4613 hours, 2 minutes, 46 secondspurpose of using subqueries in our scripts and queries. It's going to help us to reduce the complexity and going to make it easier to read and we can have
13:02:5613 hours, 2 minutes, 56 secondslike a flow logical flow inside our queries.
13:03:0313 hours, 3 minutes, 3 secondsNow for the sub queries there are many different types and categories. So now what we're going to do I'm going to show you an overview of all those types and
13:03:1013 hours, 3 minutes, 10 secondscategories and then later we're going to deep dive into each of those types. So first of all if you are thinking about the dependencies between the subquery
13:03:1813 hours, 3 minutes, 18 secondsand the main query. There is mainly two types of subqueries. We have the non-correlated subquery. That means the
13:03:2613 hours, 3 minutes, 26 secondssubquery is independent from the main query. And the second type is the correlated subquery. It's exactly the
13:03:3313 hours, 3 minutes, 33 secondsopposite. The subquery gonna depend on the main query. Of course, we can explain all those stuff in details.
13:03:3913 hours, 3 minutes, 39 secondsDon't worry about it. So, this is the first group. Now, there is another group on how to group up the subqueries depending on the result type. So, I mean
13:03:4813 hours, 3 minutes, 48 secondswith this that the subquery has different output and results. For example, we have scalar subquery. It returns only one single value. or
13:03:5613 hours, 3 minutes, 56 secondsanother type it's called the row subquery. It's going to return multiple rows and the final type called the table
13:04:0313 hours, 4 minutes, 3 secondssubquery. It is a subquery that returns multiple rows and as well multiple columns. Now we come to the third way and the last way on how to categorize
13:04:1213 hours, 4 minutes, 12 secondsthe subqueries and this time based on the location and the clauses. So we are describing here where the subquery going
13:04:1913 hours, 4 minutes, 19 secondsto be used within the main query. So we can use it in different locations and clauses like the select clause or we can
13:04:2713 hours, 4 minutes, 27 secondsuse it in the from clause and this is the most common type for the subqueries or we can use it before joining tables and we can use it in order to filter the
13:04:3513 hours, 4 minutes, 35 secondsdata in the work clause and in the work clause as we learned there are two different sets of operators. We can use the subgrade together with the comparison operators the less, greater,
13:04:4613 hours, 4 minutes, 46 secondsequal and so on. Or we can use it with the logical operators like the in, any,
13:04:5213 hours, 4 minutes, 52 secondsall and exists. So now those are the different types and categories for the subqueries and we're going to now deep
13:04:5913 hours, 4 minutes, 59 secondsdive into all of them. So now let's go and start with the easiest category, the result types of the subqueries.
13:05:1013 hours, 5 minutes, 10 secondsNow we have different types of subqueries based on the results. So this means the amount of data that the subquery going to return. So the first
13:05:1813 hours, 5 minutes, 18 secondstype is the scalar subquery. So it is a subquery that it's going to return only one single value like for example the
13:05:2613 hours, 5 minutes, 26 secondsvalue three. Let's have an example for the scalar subquery. So in this query for example if you are saying select star you will get all columns all the
13:05:3413 hours, 5 minutes, 34 secondsrows from one table. But for the scalar subquery we need only one value. So how we usually get it is by doing some aggregations. For example, if you go and
13:05:4313 hours, 5 minutes, 43 secondssay let's get the average of sales. So let's execute it. And with that in the output we have only one value with a 38.
13:05:5113 hours, 5 minutes, 51 secondsWe call such a query as a scalar query.
13:05:5413 hours, 5 minutes, 54 secondsSo it has only one row and only one column. So this is a scalar query. All right. So now to the second type we have
13:06:0213 hours, 6 minutes, 2 secondsthe row subquery. So it is a subquery that going to return multiple rows and a single column. So we're going to have
13:06:0913 hours, 6 minutes, 9 secondslike values 1 2 3. So it is only one column with multiple rows. Let's have an example for the row query. As you can

13:06:4513 hours, 6 minutes, 45 secondsgoing to go and return multiple rows and as well multiple columns like any regular tables. So this subquery going
13:06:5213 hours, 6 minutes, 52 secondsto return a lot of values. Okay. So let's see an example of that table query. So if you check our example here,
13:06:5813 hours, 6 minutes, 58 secondsselect star from orders, we got here multiple rows and as well multiple columns and of course we can go and
13:07:0613 hours, 7 minutes, 6 secondsselect multiple columns like for example the order ID and the order dates. So if we execute it here in the output we have
13:07:1513 hours, 7 minutes, 15 secondsmultiple columns we have two columns and as well multiple rows that's why this kind of query is as well a table query.
13:07:2213 hours, 7 minutes, 22 secondsAll right. So with that we have learned the different types of subqueries based on the result type. Now we're going to go and learn how to use the subqueries

13:07:3913 hours, 7 minutes, 39 secondsclause. Okay. So we typically use the subqueries in the from clause in order to create temporary result sets that act

13:18:5513 hours, 18 minutes, 55 secondsfirst the subquery. So here subquery is like selecting and retrieving data from the table orders. So that means the database has to retrieve the data from
13:19:0413 hours, 19 minutes, 4 secondsthe disk storage from the user data. So now once the subquery is executed the result the intermediate results going to
13:19:1113 hours, 19 minutes, 11 secondsbe stored in the cache. So this means the result of the subquery is temporary and as well very fast to retrieve. And
13:19:1913 hours, 19 minutes, 19 secondsnow once the database engine is done with the subquery it going to go and start executing the main query. So let's see in this scenario it's completely depending on the result of the subquery.
13:19:2913 hours, 19 minutes, 29 secondsSo that means the main query going to go and interact with the cache storage. So this means now the data going to be retrieved very fast from the result of
13:19:3713 hours, 19 minutes, 37 secondsthe subquery. Once it's done, it's going to forward the result to the database engine and the database engine going to forward the results to the client side.
13:19:4513 hours, 19 minutes, 45 secondsAnd at your side, you will find the final result. And of course, once everything is executed, the database engine going to go and clean up the
13:19:5313 hours, 19 minutes, 53 secondscache. So the subquery results going to be destroyed and removed completely from the cache in order to have a free space for other queries. So this is how the

13:40:0613 hours, 40 minutes, 6 secondsAnd now the result of this subquery going to be used in order to filter the data using the in operator. And now the
13:40:1313 hours, 40 minutes, 13 secondsbig difference between the in operator and the comparison operators that the subquery is allowed to have multiple
13:40:2013 hours, 40 minutes, 20 secondsrows. So there is no rule about having like one single value scalar subquery.
13:40:2513 hours, 40 minutes, 25 secondsWe can have in the result a list of multiple values. So this is the syntax of the subquery using the in operator.

13:53:1713 hours, 53 minutes, 17 secondsabout how to use the subqueries in different locations and clauses. But we didn't talk about the exist operator and that's because I would like you to
13:53:2513 hours, 53 minutes, 25 secondsunderstand a very important concept in the subqueries where we have two different types of the subqueries based on the dependencies the non-correlated
13:53:3413 hours, 53 minutes, 34 secondsand correlated subqueries. And after that we're going to go back to the exist operator. All right friends. So now we
13:53:4413 hours, 53 minutes, 44 secondscome to the part where it is a little bit complicated about the subqueries.
13:53:4713 hours, 53 minutes, 47 secondsNow we're going to talk about the dependencies between the subquery and the main query. So far all the examples and the subqueries that we have learned
13:53:5613 hours, 53 minutes, 56 secondswhere a noncorrelated subquery. A non-correlated subquery means a subquery that can run independently from the main
13:54:0513 hours, 54 minutes, 5 secondsquery. So that means the subquery is like standalone query. But in the other hand we have the exact opposite type of
13:54:1213 hours, 54 minutes, 12 secondsthe subquery. We have the correlated subquery. A correlated subquery is a subquery that relies on values from the
13:54:2013 hours, 54 minutes, 20 secondsmain query for each row it processes. So that means the subquery here is completely depending on the main query.
13:54:2913 hours, 54 minutes, 29 secondsSo I know this might be a little bit confusing. That's why we can have the following very simple sketch in order to exactly understand how this works. So as
13:54:3713 hours, 54 minutes, 37 secondsusual we have a database tables and now this time going to go and start executing the main query first. This is
13:54:4513 hours, 54 minutes, 45 secondsthe first thing happens. So the main query going to go and query the database in order to get results and SQL going to
13:54:5313 hours, 54 minutes, 53 secondsprocess the results row by row. So now what going to happen? The main query going to go and pass the first row informations to the sub query. So now
13:55:0213 hours, 55 minutes, 2 secondsthe subquery going to get the data from the main query. So SQL going to execute the subquery. So here the subquery going to return a value like for example one.
13:55:1113 hours, 55 minutes, 11 secondsSo here it's very important to understand that now the SQL or the main query going to check is there a result from the sub query in this example yes
13:55:2013 hours, 55 minutes, 20 secondswe have a results. So here SQL is checking the output for the subquery for the first row. So if there is a result
13:55:2813 hours, 55 minutes, 28 secondsSQL going to go and return the row in the final result. So this is the whole iteration happened only for the first
13:55:3613 hours, 55 minutes, 36 secondsrow. So we're going to process the whole thing again from the start for the second row. So the main query going to get the second row from the database and it going to pass it to the subquery.
13:55:4713 hours, 55 minutes, 47 secondsOnce the subquery gets this new informations, SQL going to go and execute the subquery once again. So now let's say that after executing the
13:55:5613 hours, 55 minutes, 56 secondssubquery, there were no results. So the subquery is not returning anything after the execution. So now what can happen?
13:56:0413 hours, 56 minutes, 4 secondsSQL and the main query going to check okay there is no result from the sub query and this means this row should be
13:56:1113 hours, 56 minutes, 11 secondsexcluded and not presented in the output. So we will not see this row at the output. So as you can see SQL is
13:56:1813 hours, 56 minutes, 18 secondsexecuting the subquery once again for the second row. So this will keep happening as long as we have row. For
13:56:2513 hours, 56 minutes, 25 secondsexample, we have another row. The main query going to pass it to the subquery.
13:56:2913 hours, 56 minutes, 29 secondsThe subquery going to be executed for the third time and the result of the subquery is going to be one. So the same thing going to happen. SQL going to
13:56:3713 hours, 56 minutes, 37 secondscheck it. Okay, we have a value. So this row is allowed to be in the final results and so on. The cycle going to keep repeating for each row that's going
13:56:4613 hours, 56 minutes, 46 secondsto be retrieved from the main query and once we have processed all the rows, the final result going to be presented in the output. So what we have understood
13:56:5413 hours, 56 minutes, 54 secondsso far the correlated subqueries is always depending on the main query and the subquery going to be executed for
13:57:0313 hours, 57 minutes, 3 secondseach row that we're going to get from the main query. So in this example we have four rows and the subquery is executed four times. So this is how the
13:57:1213 hours, 57 minutes, 12 secondscorrelated subquery works. It's a little bit more complicated than the non-correlated subquery. The non-correlated subqueries are really
13:57:2013 hours, 57 minutes, 20 secondsstraightforward. So first the subquery going to go and execute the database only once and the output of the subquery
13:57:2713 hours, 57 minutes, 27 secondsgoing to be like an intermediate results that going to be used from the main query. So the main query going to go and query the intermediate results and in
13:57:3613 hours, 57 minutes, 36 secondsthe output we're going to get the final results. So as you can see in the execution of the non-correlated subquery it is straightforward. There's no
13:57:4413 hours, 57 minutes, 44 secondsiterations everything going to be executed only once. So now if you compare them side by side you can see that with the non-correlated subquery it
13:57:5213 hours, 57 minutes, 52 secondsis completely independent from the main query. So that means the subquery going to be executed only once and after that
13:58:0013 hours, 58 minutesSQL going to go and as well execute the main query only once using the result from the subquery. But on the left side
13:58:0713 hours, 58 minutes, 7 secondsthe subquery is going to be executed multiple times and it is completely depending on the main query and there is like an iteration for each row that's
13:58:1613 hours, 58 minutes, 16 secondsgoing to be retrieved from the main query. So the process going to be cycling until all the rows are processed and this is exactly how the correlated
13:58:2513 hours, 58 minutes, 25 secondsand the non-correlated subqueries work in SQL. All right. So now let's have the following task and it says show all customer details and find the total
13:58:3413 hours, 58 minutes, 34 secondsorders of each customer. We have already solved this task and you know in scale we don't have only one query in order to solve something. We have multiple ways
13:58:4213 hours, 58 minutes, 42 secondsin order to do it. So we solved this task before using the subqueries and the joins. Now we're going to go and solve this task using subquery in the select
13:58:5113 hours, 58 minutes, 51 secondsclause and as well using the correlated subqueries. So again let's do it step by step. It's very simple. First we need

13:59:4513 hours, 59 minutes, 45 secondsSo let's just put it over here. And this is the main query. And in order to make this as a subquery, what we're going to
13:59:5213 hours, 59 minutes, 52 secondsdo, we're going to have the parenthesis and we're going to say the total sales.
13:59:5613 hours, 59 minutes, 56 secondsSo now let's go and execute it. So now as you can see, we have here all the details about the customers and we have the total sales. But we have one issue.
14:00:0314 hours, 3 secondsWe don't need just the total order. We need the total orders for each customer.
14:00:0814 hours, 8 secondsSo each customer has different total orders. So we cannot have like the following setup. We cannot say group by
14:00:1514 hours, 15 secondscustomer ID. And then you have like here the customer ID and so on. So if you go and execute it, you will get a problem.
14:00:2314 hours, 23 secondsAnd that's because if you go and execute this subquery over here, you will get like multiple rows and multiple columns.
14:00:3014 hours, 30 secondsSo you have like a table query. And this type of subquery is not allowed to be used in the select clause, right? We have to have only scalar
14:00:3814 hours, 38 secondssubquery. So that's why we cannot do that. So we have to go and remove all those stuff.
14:00:4514 hours, 45 secondsBut we can go and solve it using the correlated subqueries. So now the subquery is completely independent from
14:00:5214 hours, 52 secondsthe main query. So in order to correlate it, what we're going to do, we're going to go and connect it. So I'm going to give aliases for the tables and I'm
14:01:0014 hours, 1 minutegoing to say where the customer ID equal to the customer ID from the main query
14:01:0714 hours, 1 minute, 7 secondsfrom the customers. So again we are connecting the customer ID from the orders in the subquery with the customer
14:01:1414 hours, 1 minute, 14 secondsID from the table customers that comes from the main query. So now we are saying okay execute this only for a
14:01:2214 hours, 1 minute, 22 secondsspecific customer not for the whole table. So let's go and execute it. So now in the output we have the total
14:01:2914 hours, 1 minute, 29 secondssales for each customer and we don't have here like the total sales in the whole table orders and that's because what is happening for each row the
14:01:3714 hours, 1 minute, 37 secondssubquery going to be executed. So for the customer number one this query going to be executed like this count the total
14:01:4414 hours, 1 minute, 44 secondsnumber of orders where the customer ID equal to the one. So let me just show you what this means. If I go and remove
14:01:5214 hours, 1 minute, 52 secondsthis from here and just put the number one. So if I go and execute this, you will see the customer ID one has three
14:02:0014 hours, 2 minutesorders. And let's just put it back and execute. And the same thing going to happen for each customer. So for each customer, for each row, this subquery
14:02:0914 hours, 2 minutes, 9 secondsgoing to be executed and it can be filtered with the customer ID that comes from the main query. So this is another way in how to solve this task using the
14:02:1714 hours, 2 minutes, 17 secondscorrelated subqueries. So now let's summarize and understand quickly what are the differences between the non-correlated and the correlated subqueries. So now if you are talking
14:02:2514 hours, 2 minutes, 25 secondsabout the definition the non-correlated subquery are subqueries that are independent of the main query but in the
14:02:3314 hours, 2 minutes, 33 secondsother hand the correlated subqueries are dependent of the main query. And now if you're talking about the execution the non-correlated subquery is going to be
14:02:4114 hours, 2 minutes, 41 secondsexecuted only once and then the results going to be used by the main query but by the correlated subqueries the subquery going to be executed for each row that we have from the main query.
14:02:5214 hours, 2 minutes, 52 secondsAnd as we learned for the non-correlated subqueries we can execute it on its own.
14:02:5714 hours, 2 minutes, 57 secondsSo we can go and select it and execute it. But the correlated subqueries we cannot execute it on its own. So we have to execute always the whole thing. And
14:03:0614 hours, 3 minutes, 6 secondsif you are talking about which one is easier, I think it's clear that the noncorrelated subqueries are easier to write and to read. And in the other
14:03:1314 hours, 3 minutes, 13 secondshand, the correlated subqueries are harder to read and as well it's complex.
14:03:1814 hours, 3 minutes, 18 secondsNow, if you're talking about the performance of the database since the correlated subqueries can be executed only once, this of course going to lead
14:03:2514 hours, 3 minutes, 25 secondsyou to have better performance because things are really straightforward and not complicated. But in the other hand with the correlated subqueries there is
14:03:3214 hours, 3 minutes, 32 secondsmore effort because SQL has to check a lot of stuff and the subquery going to be executed many times. So the noncorrelated subqueries are faster. We
14:03:4014 hours, 3 minutes, 40 secondsuse the noncorrelated subqueries in order to do static comparison. So the value that we are getting from the subquery is executed only once and we
14:03:4914 hours, 3 minutes, 49 secondswill get only one static value in order to use it for filtering and so on. But in the other hand we use correlated subqueries in order to do rowby row
14:03:5714 hours, 3 minutes, 57 secondscomparison. And since we don't have here a static value each time the subquery going to run we're going to have different results. This going to add more dynamic to the filters and we don't
14:04:0614 hours, 4 minutes, 6 secondshave a static value. So those are the big differences between the non-correlated and the correlated subqueries. All right. So now after we
14:04:1314 hours, 4 minutes, 13 secondsunderstood the concept of the two types correlated and non-correlated subqueries we're going to go now and cover the last
14:04:2014 hours, 4 minutes, 20 secondsoperator for the subqueries. We have the exists. So what is exist
14:04:2714 hours, 4 minutes, 27 secondsoperator? All right. So now we're going to talk about a very interesting operator function in SQL the exists. So now in some scenarios as you are
14:04:3514 hours, 4 minutes, 35 secondsquerying the data from one table you would need to go and check whether the rows of this table exist in another table. So that means you are checking
14:04:4414 hours, 4 minutes, 44 secondslike the existence of your rows in different table. And exactly in this scenario we go and use subqueries together with the operator exists. So
14:04:5314 hours, 4 minutes, 53 secondsthe exist operator is very simple. It just simply check whether the subquery returns any results any rows. All right.
14:05:0014 hours, 5 minutesSo now let's understand the syntax of the correlated subqueries using the exist operator. This can be a little bit complicated but we're going to do it
14:05:0814 hours, 5 minutes, 8 secondsstep by step. Don't worry about it. So let's start with the easy stuff. In the main query we're going to go and write a simple select. We are selecting few
14:05:1614 hours, 5 minutes, 16 secondscolumns from the table two. And now we don't need all the data from table two.

14:05:2714 hours, 5 minutes, 27 secondsexists. So we don't specify any column before the exist like we have done in the comparison operator or the in operator. We don't need that because we
14:05:3514 hours, 5 minutes, 35 secondsare not filtering based on a value. We are filtering based on the logic. That's why we have the word exist immediately.
14:05:4214 hours, 5 minutes, 42 secondsAnd now directly after they exist, we're going to go and define the subquery like this. So we're going to start saying select one from the table number one.
14:05:5014 hours, 5 minutes, 50 secondsWell, it is not like a must or something. But it is very commonly used to specify here a one. We are not using the subquery in order to retrieve
14:05:5814 hours, 5 minutes, 58 secondsinformations from the table one. We are just testing whether the subquery going to return a value or not. And we don't care about the returned value. It could
14:06:0714 hours, 6 minutes, 7 secondsbe one, it could be column, it could be anything. So we don't care about the data that is retrieved. We are just care whether the subquery is returning

14:06:3514 hours, 6 minutes, 35 secondsfrom the outer query from the main query. And with that we are building like a relationship between the subquery

14:06:5014 hours, 6 minutes, 50 secondsthe table 2 id. So the ids from the main query going to filter the subquery. So this is the syntax of correlated sub
14:06:5914 hours, 6 minutes, 59 secondsqueries using the exist where we are making the subquery depending totally on the main query. So let's understand how
14:07:0614 hours, 7 minutes, 6 secondsexist works. So now for each row that we have from the main query, it's going to trigger and cause an execution of the
14:07:1414 hours, 7 minutes, 14 secondssubquery. This subquery going to help us to evaluate this row. So we are testing this row. Now if the subquery doesn't return anything, so there is no results,
14:07:2314 hours, 7 minutes, 23 secondswhat can happen? The row that we are evaluating from the main query will be excluded from the final results. But now
14:07:3014 hours, 7 minutes, 30 secondsin the other hand if the subquery is returning a value so we have like some kind of results then this row that we
14:07:3714 hours, 7 minutes, 37 secondsare evaluating going to be included in the final results. So the subquery is used in order to do a test. Do we have a
14:07:4514 hours, 7 minutes, 45 secondsresults or we don't and based on this SQL either going to include or exclude the row from the final results. So this is the logic behind the exist in SQL.

14:18:2114 hours, 18 minutes, 21 secondsmultiple times within your query to simplify and organize complex query. So let's understand what this means using
14:18:2914 hours, 18 minutes, 29 secondsthe following sketch. So we have our database tables like orders, customers and so on. And in very simple scenario we write a simple SQL in order to query

14:19:1714 hours, 19 minutes, 17 secondsyou check this we have like a query inside another query. So now let's see what is going to do with this. The first thing is going to go and execute the
14:19:2514 hours, 19 minutes, 25 secondscity query. So the city query going to be executed and we're going to go and retrieve few informations from our database tables. Now the output going to
14:19:3414 hours, 19 minutes, 34 secondsbe available only in the query and the output going to have the shape of like a table like for example the sales. So now the sales table and the orders tables
14:19:4314 hours, 19 minutes, 43 secondsboth of them are tables but one is stored in the database and the other one is an intermediate virtual table. So now
14:19:5114 hours, 19 minutes, 51 secondswhat can happen in the main query we can go and start querying the sales table the result from the CTE as any other
14:19:5914 hours, 19 minutes, 59 secondsnormal table like we do to the database tables. So the main query going to go and retrieve few informations and maybe do some manipulations on top of the

14:20:1614 hours, 20 minutes, 16 secondsdatabase. So the main query has two sources of tables. Either get it directly from the database or get it from the table that is created inside
14:20:2514 hours, 20 minutes, 25 secondsthe query and then once everything is done the final results of the main query going to be presented for the user as a

14:22:3514 hours, 22 minutes, 35 secondsCTE, I think from top to bottom. But still you say, you know what, I don't care how we write it. They are doing the same thing. The subquery is introducing
14:22:4414 hours, 22 minutes, 44 secondsan intermediate result that is used later from the main query. And the same thing for the CTE. It present like
14:22:5114 hours, 22 minutes, 51 secondsintermediate table that is used as well from the main query. Now let me tell you the big differences between them is that
14:22:5714 hours, 22 minutes, 57 secondsin the subquery the result can be used only once. So you cannot have another place in your main query where you go and reuse the result from the subquery.
14:23:0714 hours, 23 minutes, 7 secondsSo you can use it maximum only in one position and only once. But in the other hand with the city technique, you can

14:23:3914 hours, 23 minutes, 39 secondsis only available in our query. So this is the main and the most important difference between the subquery and the CTE. It's from the name common table
14:23:4614 hours, 23 minutes, 46 secondsexpression. We think about the result of the CTE as a table. So we can go and select it. We can go and join it with
14:23:5414 hours, 23 minutes, 54 secondsany other table. So it is like a hidden virtual table lives inside our query.
14:23:5914 hours, 23 minutes, 59 secondsBut the subqueries it's totally different. It's a result only for one position in the main query and it's used only once. So that means if you want the subquery in two three different places,
14:24:1014 hours, 24 minutes, 10 secondsyou have to go and write the subquery three different times. So now you understand why do we have CTE and why do we have
14:24:2114 hours, 24 minutes, 21 secondssubqueries. All right. So with that you have understood what is CTE. Now the question is why do we need CTE in the first place? What is the main purpose of the CTE? Let's go back to the sketch.
14:24:3214 hours, 24 minutes, 32 secondsNow let's say in our complex SQL task we have to do the following step. Step one we have to go and join the tables together in order to prepare all the
14:24:4014 hours, 24 minutes, 40 secondsdata that we need for the next step. And now in the second step we have to go and aggregate the data. Maybe we are doing summarizations. Now in our task we have
14:24:4814 hours, 24 minutes, 48 secondsto do as well different types of aggregations based on different data.
14:24:5214 hours, 24 minutes, 52 secondsAnd now what might happen is that we have to go and join again the same tables in order to prepare the data and perform different type of aggregations
14:25:0014 hours, 25 minuteslike for example the average which going to be in the last step. Now we have learned before we can go and use the subqueries in order to make this logical
14:25:0814 hours, 25 minutes, 8 secondsflow. So for step one, step two, step three, we will have subqueries and the final step going to be in the main query. But now if we keep doing this
14:25:1714 hours, 25 minutes, 17 secondswe're gonna have a problem and that is we are repeating the same step more than once. So we are joining the table twice
14:25:2414 hours, 25 minutes, 24 secondsin step number one and three for different purposes which cause us to have two different subqueries that looks
14:25:3114 hours, 25 minutes, 31 secondsexactly the same and this is exactly the weak point of the subqueries. It might introduce redundancies. So that means
14:25:3914 hours, 25 minutes, 39 secondsthe subqueries alone will not help you to eliminate all the duplicates in your code. But still we have different techniques in order to solve this issue.
14:25:4714 hours, 25 minutes, 47 secondsSo what we going to do? We're going to have only one step in order to join the tables. And then this data going to be used in the step two in order to
14:25:5514 hours, 25 minutes, 55 secondsaggregate the data. And then we don't need the step three of joining again the data. We're going to reuse the step one.
14:26:0214 hours, 26 minutes, 2 secondsAnd we're going to use the same data for the step four which is aggregating the data using average. And we can do this
14:26:0914 hours, 26 minutes, 9 secondswith the help of the amazing CTE. So now if you compare the steps in the subqueries with the steps with the CTE you can see with the CTE we are reducing
14:26:1814 hours, 26 minutes, 18 secondsthe number of steps which can lead to reduce the size of the query. So now again here in subquery we think about
14:26:2414 hours, 26 minutes, 24 secondsthe steps from bottom to top but in the city it's the way around we think from top to bottom. So that means the first step on the top it's going to be joining
14:26:3314 hours, 26 minutes, 33 secondsthe tables and then below it going to be step two and step three. And of course since we are repeating the join we're going to put it in CTE and then we can
14:26:4014 hours, 26 minutes, 40 secondsuse it twice in different places in the main query. So as you can see there are a lot of benefits of the CTE. It's like the subqueries. We are breaking down
14:26:4914 hours, 26 minutes, 49 secondscomplex queries into smaller pieces that are easier to write manage understand and as well we have like a logical flow
14:26:5714 hours, 26 minutes, 57 secondsfrom step one to three but with one more benefit that we reduce the redundancies of our code. So we don't have to join
14:27:0514 hours, 27 minutes, 5 secondsthe tables twice. Now I'm going to show you a simple example how the CTE makes our life easier in our query. We might have to do different stuff like for
14:27:1314 hours, 27 minutes, 13 secondsexample we have to go and find the top customers. So we can put this in one CTE and we might need as well to calculate
14:27:2014 hours, 27 minutes, 20 secondswhat are the top products and we can put as well this in another city. So you don't have to put everything in one big city. Then you can have the same issue
14:27:2814 hours, 27 minutes, 28 secondsof having complex query. And let's say that we have as well to find and calculate the daily revenue. And for this as well, we have to put it in one CTE. Now once we have all those parts,
14:27:3814 hours, 27 minutes, 38 secondswe can put everything together in the main query. So now if you look to this structure, you can see it's really easy to understand this code. It's easy to
14:27:4714 hours, 27 minutes, 47 secondsread. So CTE improves the readability of our queries. So that means your code is divided into clear sections making it
14:27:5514 hours, 27 minutes, 55 secondseasier to understand what each part does. Now if you keep looking to this we have another advantage of the CTE
14:28:0214 hours, 28 minutes, 2 secondsintroduces modularity. So that means it breaks your code into smaller manageable parts. So this means instead of writing
14:28:1014 hours, 28 minutes, 10 secondsone huge complex query you break it down into smaller chunks using CTE. Each city is like self-contained and handles
14:28:1914 hours, 28 minutes, 19 secondsspecific part of the problem and then you can combine them all together in the final query. It's like we are putting together a puzzle piece by piece. And
14:28:2714 hours, 28 minutes, 27 secondsnow one very important advantage of the CTE is the reusability. So that means we can have a result set that is used
14:28:3414 hours, 28 minutes, 34 secondsmultiple times inside our query. So that means you write the logic the code only once and then use it in different places inside your query. This is very
14:28:4314 hours, 28 minutes, 43 secondsimportant. Not only you are wasting time writing the same stuff over and over,
14:28:4714 hours, 28 minutes, 47 secondsbut also it reduces the errors and mistakes that you might do if you are repeating the same code. Especially if later you want to go and change the
14:28:5414 hours, 28 minutes, 54 secondslogic then you have to go and visit each time you have done this logic and then do the changes and you might forget some places. That's why the CTE is amazing.
14:29:0414 hours, 29 minutes, 4 secondsYou can write the logic once and then you go and reuse it in different places.
14:29:0814 hours, 29 minutes, 8 secondsSo these are the advantages of using this technique the CTE inside your
14:29:1314 hours, 29 minutes, 13 seconds[Music]
14:29:1614 hours, 29 minutes, 16 secondsqueries. So again you are at the client side and you are data analyst. You are writing a query where you are defining a CTE called details and inside it you
14:29:2514 hours, 29 minutes, 25 secondshave some logic and now in the main query you are selecting the data from the orders and as well you are joining it with the details with the CTE
14:29:3314 hours, 29 minutes, 33 secondsmultiple times using multiple conditions. Now once you go and execute this query the database engine going to read the query and say aha we have here
14:29:4114 hours, 29 minutes, 41 secondsa CTE and it has the main priority. So that means it going to go and execute the CTE first. And now let's say that in
14:29:4814 hours, 29 minutes, 48 secondsthe city you are retrieving data from the table orders and the table orders of course in the disk storage inside the user data. And now once the city is
14:29:5714 hours, 29 minutes, 57 secondscompletely executed the database engine going to go and place the results in the cache and it's going to name this result as details. It's like a table name. So
14:30:0614 hours, 30 minutes, 6 secondsthe database engine is done with the CTE. It's going to go now and grab the main query and it's going to start executing it step by step. So the first
14:30:1314 hours, 30 minutes, 13 secondsstep is that to get the data from the orders. So since the orders exist in the disk storage, it going to go and retrieve it from there. Now the database engine going to check the details. Okay,
14:30:2214 hours, 30 minutes, 22 secondswe have it in the cache. That means we don't have to search for it in the disk storage and it going to start retrieving the data from the details with high
14:30:3014 hours, 30 minutes, 30 secondsspeed. And now it's going to go to the second step as well joining the data with the details. So again the database engine going to go to the cache and going to see the table details and
14:30:3914 hours, 30 minutes, 39 secondsretrieve the data based maybe in different conditions. And then to the third time as well we are joining to the details and we're going to get the data from the cache. So as you can see from
14:30:4714 hours, 30 minutes, 47 secondsthe main query we are using the result from the CTE multiple times in different places and the retrieval of all those informations is happening in high speed.
14:30:5714 hours, 30 minutes, 57 secondsSo this is one big benefit of using the CTE is to utilize using the high-speed memory of the cache. So that means
14:31:0414 hours, 31 minutes, 4 secondsretrieving the data from the cache from the details is way faster than retrieving the data from the disk storage from the orders. Now once the
14:31:1214 hours, 31 minutes, 12 secondsmain query is completely executed the result going to be returned to the database engine and then it's going to send it back to the client side and we
14:31:2014 hours, 31 minutes, 20 secondswill see the results in the output. So that's it. It's amazing right? This is how the database server execute the
14:31:2614 hours, 31 minutes, 26 secondsamazing technique the CTE behind the scenes.
14:31:3314 hours, 31 minutes, 33 secondsAll right. So now for the CTE, we don't have only one CTE. We have different types of CTE. So mainly there are like two types of CTE. We have the
14:31:4214 hours, 31 minutes, 42 secondsnonrecursive CTE and recursive CTE. And we can say for the nonrecursive CTE, we
14:31:4814 hours, 31 minutes, 48 secondshave two subtypes. The first type is the standalone CTE and the second one is the nested CTE. And now what we're going to
14:31:5614 hours, 31 minutes, 56 secondsdo, we're going to deep dive into each type. And we will start with the easiest form of the CTE, the standalone CTE. It
14:32:0314 hours, 32 minutes, 3 secondsis the simplest form. So what is standalone CTE? It is a
14:32:1114 hours, 32 minutes, 11 secondsCTE query that is defined and used independently in the query. So that means it is self-contained and it
14:32:1814 hours, 32 minutes, 18 secondsdoesn't depend on anything. It doesn't depend on any other CTE or queries. So that means we can run the standalone
14:32:2614 hours, 32 minutes, 26 secondsquery independently from anything inside our query. So let's understand what this means. We have our CTE. It's going to go
14:32:3214 hours, 32 minutes, 32 secondsand query the database tables and in the output we will get an intermediate results and then the output can be used from the main query. So the main query
14:32:4114 hours, 32 minutes, 41 secondsgoing to query the intermediate results and present in the output the final results. So now if you check our CTE, it is completely independent from anything
14:32:4914 hours, 32 minutes, 49 secondselse. So it simply query the database and it has one output. So since this CTE is independent from anything else we
14:32:5714 hours, 32 minutes, 57 secondscall it a standalone CTE. Now if you compare this CT with the main query you can see that the main query cannot be executed alone. And that's because it needs the result from the first query.
14:33:0914 hours, 33 minutes, 9 secondsSo we cannot say the main query is independent cannot be executed alone. It always depend on the city query. So that
14:33:1714 hours, 33 minutes, 17 secondsmeans city first need to be executed then the main query can be executed. So this is what we mean with the standalone
14:33:2414 hours, 33 minutes, 24 secondscity. It doesn't depend on anything else. So now we can understand the syntax of the CTE. So we have a very simple query select from where. So it is
14:33:3214 hours, 33 minutes, 32 secondsa very simple select statement. Now in order to put it inside a CTE we can go and use the with clause. So it starts with the keyword with then the CTE name.
14:33:4414 hours, 33 minutes, 44 secondsIt's like a table name and then we have the keyword as in order to say this CTE is defined like the following. So this
14:33:5214 hours, 33 minutes, 52 secondsis the definition of the CTE and it has two parenthesis the starting and the ending. So with this you are telling a scale okay now we are talking about CTE
14:34:0114 hours, 34 minutes, 1 secondand it has a name. So if you are using a query inside with clause we call this a CTE query it is where you define the
14:34:0814 hours, 34 minutes, 8 secondsCTE. Now of course we don't want only to define a CTE. We want to use it. So outside of this definition we can go and use it like this. So we are saying
14:34:1714 hours, 34 minutes, 17 secondsselect from the CTE name. So that means we want to select the data from the result of the CTE. And here it's very
14:34:2514 hours, 34 minutes, 25 secondsimportant to use exactly the same name as you define it in the width clause. So if you leave it like this, we can call

14:49:0214 hours, 49 minutes, 2 secondsquery inside another query. So not only a main query can use the result of CTE another CTE can use the result from a
14:49:1014 hours, 49 minutes, 10 secondsCTE and of course the nested CTE is like a main query is depend on other query that means you cannot go and select it and run it independently from the query.
14:49:2014 hours, 49 minutes, 20 secondsSo always you have to run the CTE inside it first before seeing the result of the nested CTE. Okay. So now let's understand what this means. Again we
14:49:2814 hours, 49 minutes, 28 secondshave our database and we have a city query that goes directly to the database and queries the data from there and in
14:49:3614 hours, 49 minutes, 36 secondsthe output we will get the intermediate results. And now in this scenario this time we will not have only one intermediate results because we have
14:49:4314 hours, 49 minutes, 43 secondsmany different steps. We need another intermediate results before everything is prepared for the main query. So that
14:49:5014 hours, 49 minutes, 50 secondsmeans we have another step that's going to be built up on top of the first intermediate results. So that means we can have another CTE that's going to be
14:50:0014 hours, 50 minutesquering the results from the first CTE and build on top of it another intermediate result. So as you can see
14:50:0614 hours, 50 minutes, 6 secondshere we have CTE1 and CTE2 and that means now we have like two intermediate results. And now of course we can go and
14:50:1414 hours, 50 minutes, 14 secondsadd CTE 3 4 and so on. But now let's say that the CTE2 going to prepare the final intermediate result for the main query.
14:50:2214 hours, 50 minutes, 22 secondsSo now the main query going to go and query the second intermedator results and it's going to do the final step where the final result can be presented
14:50:3014 hours, 50 minutes, 30 secondsfor the user and of course if it is needed the main query can access not only the second intermediate result from
14:50:3714 hours, 50 minutes, 37 secondsthe second CTE but also the first intermediate result from the CTE1. Now we call the first CTE a standalone CTE
14:50:4514 hours, 50 minutes, 45 secondsbecause it doesn't depend on any intermediate results. It goes directly to the database and gets the data. But now since the second city is completely
14:50:5414 hours, 50 minutes, 54 secondsdepending on the city one. So this time we're going to call this CTE a nested CTE because we cannot go and execute it
14:51:0214 hours, 51 minutes, 2 secondson its own. It always depends on the city one. And of course the main city is depending on everything. So as you can see we're using the CTE we're going to
14:51:0914 hours, 51 minutes, 9 secondsgo and build like a chain. So this is what we mean with the standalone city and nested city. Okay. So now let's understand the syntax of the nested
14:51:1714 hours, 51 minutes, 17 secondscity. So we start as usual with the definition of the first city using the with clause and then the name of the city and the definition of the city. So
14:51:2614 hours, 51 minutes, 26 secondshere it's nothing new. Now we go and define the second city as we learned using the comma then the name of the CTE
14:51:3414 hours, 51 minutes, 34 secondsand the definition. So this is our CTE number two. So now the second CTE is depending on the results of the first
14:51:4114 hours, 51 minutes, 41 secondsCTE. So how we going to do it? It's very simple. Now for the CTE number two,
14:51:4614 hours, 51 minutes, 46 secondswe're going to select the data from the CTE number one. And with that, we are making the second city depending on the
14:51:5314 hours, 51 minutes, 53 secondsfirst one. So this means the second CTE is getting the data from the first one and it's querying the data in order to do the second step. And with that we are

15:03:0415 hours, 3 minutes, 4 secondsthey overuse it of course not all of them but a lot of SQL developers overuse using the CTE of course the CTE is very
15:03:1215 hours, 3 minutes, 12 secondspowerful but with power comes great responsibility remember with great power comes great
15:03:2015 hours, 3 minutes, 20 secondsresponsibility so my advice for you especially if you are new to the CTS try to not add a new CTE each time you are
15:03:2815 hours, 3 minutes, 28 secondsdoing something new and I saw it a lot like for each new calculation for each new column they jump immediately and create a new CT and what happens at the
15:03:3615 hours, 3 minutes, 36 secondsend we can have like massive number of CTE inside one query and the developer thinks now everything is organized and
15:03:4315 hours, 3 minutes, 43 secondseasy to read but believe me it's exactly the opposite if you open any code and you have a lot of CDEs and especially if
15:03:5115 hours, 3 minutes, 51 secondsthey are necessities it is impossible to understand what is going on even if the developer like describe each CTE and the
15:03:5815 hours, 3 minutes, 58 secondstask of the CTE, it's going to be really hard to understand and as well to read.
15:04:0215 hours, 4 minutes, 2 secondsIf everything is like nested and you have like I don't know 20 cities in one query. So it's going to be impossible to read and to understand and as well
15:04:1015 hours, 4 minutes, 10 secondsyou're going to be using a lot of memory and you might get bad performance. So my advice for you try always as you are creating new CDs to think about how
15:04:1915 hours, 4 minutes, 19 secondsabout to merge two CDEs in one. So it is really always important to rethink and refactor your CDEs in order to merge it
15:04:2715 hours, 4 minutes, 27 secondsinto one and to reduce the number of CTE. But now if you ask me how many CTEs are okay in one query, well I don't have
15:04:3415 hours, 4 minutes, 34 secondsa magic number for that. But normally I tend to say between three and five CTE it's fine. So it's going to be easy to
15:04:4115 hours, 4 minutes, 41 secondsunderstand and to read and so on. But once you get more than five CTE then you have to rethink your code. Maybe you
15:04:4815 hours, 4 minutes, 48 secondshave to create another complete query so you don't have to put everything in one query. So this is my advice for you. Try to not overuse the CTEs in your
15:04:5715 hours, 4 minutes, 57 secondsprojects. Not for each step always refactor the CTE, consolidate them and try to not have more than five CTEs in one query. So that's my advice for you.
15:05:0615 hours, 5 minutes, 6 secondsBe responsible using the CTE. And let's go back to our course.
15:05:1415 hours, 5 minutes, 14 secondsSo with that we have learned the standalone CTE and the NIST CDE and both of them belongs to a type called
15:05:2115 hours, 5 minutes, 21 secondsnonrecursive CTE. So what is a non-recursive CDE? It means it is a city that is executed only once. So there is no repetitions or looping or anything.
15:05:3115 hours, 5 minutes, 31 secondsSo the SQL going to execute it in one go and that's it. But in the other hand the recursive city is exactly the opposite.
15:05:3815 hours, 5 minutes, 38 secondsSo a recursive city it is a selfreferencering query that repeatedly processing the data until a certain
15:05:4615 hours, 5 minutes, 46 secondscondition is met and we usually use the recursive city if we have like hierarchical structure and we want to navigate and travel through the
15:05:5415 hours, 5 minutes, 54 secondshierarchy. I know this might be confusing but don't worry about it.
15:05:5715 hours, 5 minutes, 57 secondsWe're going to have very simple examples. Now again we have our tables in the database and we have a CTE. Now the query of the CTE going to be
15:06:0415 hours, 6 minutes, 4 secondsexecuted for the first time and in the results we're going to have the initial data from the CTE but it is not everything yet. Now this intermediate
15:06:1315 hours, 6 minutes, 13 secondsresult is not ready yet for the main query but instead of that it's going to go back to the CTE and CTE going to
15:06:2115 hours, 6 minutes, 21 secondscheck whether the current results is meeting a specific condition. So now if the check says no it's not meeting the
15:06:2915 hours, 6 minutes, 29 secondscondition what's going to happen the city query going to be executed for the second time. So as you can see we are looping through the CTE. Now the result
15:06:3815 hours, 6 minutes, 38 secondsof the second iteration the second execution will be added to the intermediate result. So now the
15:06:4415 hours, 6 minutes, 44 secondsintermediate result has more data and again before we can use it from the main query it going to be checked from the
15:06:5115 hours, 6 minutes, 51 secondsCTE. Does the result fulfill the condition? If it's still no, then go and execute the CTE again. So we're going to
15:06:5915 hours, 6 minutes, 59 secondshave a third iteration and a new data going to be added to the intermediate result. So this is our third iteration.
15:07:0615 hours, 7 minutes, 6 secondsNow it's going to be checked again from the CTE. Did we fulfill the condition?
15:07:1015 hours, 7 minutes, 10 secondsIf the answer is yes, then the loop going to break and everything else. So there will be no fourth iteration of the CTE. So with that, the CTE says okay,
15:07:1915 hours, 7 minutes, 19 secondsI'm done. This is the final result of the intermediate result. then the loop going to break and everything ends and
15:07:2715 hours, 7 minutes, 27 secondsthe city will not be executed for the first time and now the city going to say okay I'm done now my intermediate result
15:07:3415 hours, 7 minutes, 34 secondsis ready to be used from the main query and now nothing new happens the main query going to go and retrieve the data from the intermediate results and do
15:07:4215 hours, 7 minutes, 42 secondssome magic in order to prepare the final results so that means there will be no iterations or looping inside the main query the looping going to be happen
15:07:5015 hours, 7 minutes, 50 secondsonly in the CTE and that's why we call it recursive CTE. So now if you compare it with the other types, all other types
15:07:5715 hours, 7 minutes, 57 secondsare always in one direction and all the CTE is going to be executed only once but the recursive CTE going to be keep
15:08:0415 hours, 8 minutes, 4 secondslooping until the condition is met and only then it's going to forward the data to the main query. And normally we use
15:08:1115 hours, 8 minutes, 11 secondsthe recursive CTE if you are navigating through hierarchical structure. So if you have in your data like hierarchal structures, you can go and use the
15:08:1915 hours, 8 minutes, 19 secondsrecursive CTE in order to navigate through it. So this is the recursive city. Okay. So now let's check the syntax of the recursive CTE. It is a
15:08:2715 hours, 8 minutes, 27 secondslittle bit complicated but we're going to do it step by step. So what do we have? We have a query and we would like to put it in a city. So we're going to
15:08:3415 hours, 8 minutes, 34 secondshave the usual stuff with clause the name of the city and as and then the query. So this is the definition of our
15:08:4215 hours, 8 minutes, 42 secondscity. But now if you leave it like this SQL going to execute it only once. But we would like to make a loop iteration.
15:08:4815 hours, 8 minutes, 48 secondsSo in order to do that we have to go and define a second select statement inside our CTE like this. So we are selecting
15:08:5615 hours, 8 minutes, 56 secondsthe data and here we have to define a breaking condition. So here in the second query we are defining a condition in order to break the loop otherwise
15:09:0515 hours, 9 minutes, 5 secondsit's going to loop for infinite or the system going to break. You could use it in the wear clause or you can use it even in an inner join because both of
15:09:1215 hours, 9 minutes, 12 secondsthem are filtering the data and you can use it in order to break the condition.
15:09:1615 hours, 9 minutes, 16 secondsAll right. So now still there is something missing. How we going to make like things looping? Well, we have to reference this CTE to itself. So what we
15:09:2415 hours, 9 minutes, 24 secondsgoing to do? We're going to say the second query going to select the data from the same CTE. So that means we have now a query that is quering itself. And
15:09:3315 hours, 9 minutes, 33 secondsthis is of course what we want. We want to make iterations and we want to make a loop. That's why we have to go and reference it to itself. And now in SQL
15:09:4115 hours, 9 minutes, 41 secondsyou cannot have it like this. You cannot have like two select statements in one query. you have to connect it somehow.
15:09:4715 hours, 9 minutes, 47 secondsThat's why we can go and use the union all or union depend if you want to have duplicates or not. So now we call the first query the anchor query. The anchor
15:09:5615 hours, 9 minutes, 56 secondsquery going to be the first query that interacts with the database and provide us the initial intermediate results. So
15:10:0315 hours, 10 minutes, 3 secondsit is the starting point of the iteration and we can say it is the first step in the process. So this going to be executed only once and it going to
15:10:1215 hours, 10 minutes, 12 secondsprovide us the initial step the first step in the process. Now we call the second step as a recursive query and we call it like this because this query
15:10:2115 hours, 10 minutes, 21 secondsgoing to be executed multiple times and it will keep repeating and add data to the intermediate results until the
15:10:2915 hours, 10 minutes, 29 secondscondition is met or let's say there will be no more data that is available to be processed. So this is the syntax of the
15:10:3615 hours, 10 minutes, 36 secondscity query for the main query nothing is changed. So we have to go and use the city name in the main query. So this is
15:10:4415 hours, 10 minutes, 44 secondsthe syntax of the recursive city. So think about it like this. SQL going to go and execute the anchor query only
15:10:5115 hours, 10 minutes, 51 secondsonce and then after that going to go through the recursive query and keep looping and looping and iterating until
15:10:5915 hours, 10 minutes, 59 secondsa certain condition is met and then SQL going to go out from the CTE. So this is actually what we mean with the anchor and recursive queries. All right. Right.

15:13:5615 hours, 13 minutes, 56 secondstake the my number the current value and we're going to add to it plus one in order to generate the second value in
15:14:0315 hours, 14 minutes, 3 secondsthe sequence. So that means my number always holds the current value and we do the operation + one in order to generate
15:14:1115 hours, 14 minutes, 11 secondsthe next sequence. So having it like this what we are doing we are generating the sequence of numbers. Now if you go and execute it like this let me just

15:14:4415 hours, 14 minutes, 44 secondscondition. For example, we can go and use the wear clause and we can say okay keep looping and keep generating but

15:15:1315 hours, 15 minutes, 13 secondsthat's why we are making it with 20. So now let's go and execute it and let's check the sequence. It start with 1 2 3
15:15:2015 hours, 15 minutes, 20 seconds4 5 and until we reach the 20. So with that we have solved the task. Again here it's not that hard right? We are just
15:15:2815 hours, 15 minutes, 28 secondsproviding the initial step and then we are providing the loop where we are defining inside it how the loop going to ends. Now there is one more thing that
15:15:3615 hours, 15 minutes, 36 secondsyou can do with the recursive CTE is to define the limit of iterations. So for example in your code if you say okay if
15:15:4315 hours, 15 minutes, 43 secondsthis iterates more than 10 times then the SQL should breaks and stops. So you can define for the SQL the maximum
15:15:5115 hours, 15 minutes, 51 secondsnumber of recursions. So how we can do that? We can do that in the main query.
15:15:5615 hours, 15 minutes, 56 secondsSo if you go over here and say option then two parenthesis and then max recursion and after that you can define
15:16:0515 hours, 16 minutes, 5 secondsthe limit. So for example let's go with the 10. Now of course we are iterating in our code now more than 20 but here we are making the rule it should not
15:16:1315 hours, 16 minutes, 13 secondsiterate more than 10. So let's go and execute it. So now we can see that our SQL breaks and it says the maximum recursion is 10. So as you can see now
15:16:2215 hours, 16 minutes, 22 secondsin the output we are getting the error of having more than 10 iterations which is not allowed. So with that you can control how many recursions you can
15:16:3015 hours, 16 minutes, 30 secondshave. Let's say that you would like to have like thousand iteration. So if you go over here and say you know what I would like to have a sequence of 1,000.
15:16:3715 hours, 16 minutes, 37 secondsIf you let me just comment this out. So if you execute it you will get an error because the default is 100. But of
15:16:4515 hours, 16 minutes, 45 secondscourse you can go and increase the maximum recursion. For example let's go with 5,000s. in the output it will work
15:16:5215 hours, 16 minutes, 52 secondsand you will get a sequence of 1,000. So with this you can control how many iterations are allowed in your query. So that you have like a control on it.
15:17:0015 hours, 17 minutesOkay. So now we can understand step by step how SQL executed the recursive query. And here we have like flow diagram in order to understand the
15:17:0815 hours, 17 minutes, 8 secondsprocess the steps of executing the recursive query. So let's go and do it.
15:17:1215 hours, 17 minutes, 12 secondsNow in the start we have the first step is to run the anchor query. So our anchor query is just a select for the
15:17:1915 hours, 17 minutes, 19 secondsvalue one. So in the output we will get the value one in my number and as you can see the anchor query going to be
15:17:2615 hours, 17 minutes, 26 secondsexecuted only once. So there is no iterations or anything. SQL executed once and then goes to the next step. So
15:17:3415 hours, 17 minutes, 34 secondswhat is the next step? It's going to execute the recursive query. So it's going to go over here and now what going to happen? We will get the current value of my number. The current value is one.
15:17:4415 hours, 17 minutes, 44 secondsand then we're going to add to it a one.
15:17:4615 hours, 17 minutes, 46 secondsSo 1 + 1 we will get from the recursive query the two which is added to our results. Now it's going to check the
15:17:5415 hours, 17 minutes, 54 secondscondition is my number now smaller than 20. Well yes it's smaller than 20 and what's going to happen since it's true
15:18:0315 hours, 18 minutes, 3 secondsis going to go and reexecute the recursive query. So now we are doing the second iteration. So again it's going to
15:18:1015 hours, 18 minutes, 10 secondsgo to the recursive query and going to say okay what is the current value of my number? It is two. So 2 + 1 the second iteration will give us the value three.
15:18:2115 hours, 18 minutes, 21 secondsSo as you can see each time the recursive query is executed it is adding more values to our result. So the same
15:18:2815 hours, 18 minutes, 28 secondsquestion can be asked is now my number smaller than 20. Well yes it is smaller.
15:18:3415 hours, 18 minutes, 34 secondsWell what can happen is still going to reexecute the recursive query. So SQL going to keep looping and iterating and
15:18:4115 hours, 18 minutes, 41 secondsadding values to the output until we reach the value 20. So now SQL going to ask is 20 my number now smaller than 20.
15:18:5215 hours, 18 minutes, 52 secondsWell no. So it is false and what's going to happen the chain will break and we will not loop anymore. So it's going to
15:19:0015 hours, 19 minutesbe the end of the city and this going to be the final results that's going to be used from the main query. So this is how

15:32:4215 hours, 32 minutes, 42 secondsSQL. So now let's have a quick recap. So we have learned that the CTE the common table expression is a temporary named
15:32:5015 hours, 32 minutes, 50 secondsresult like a virtual table that could be used from different places in the query and we have a lot of advantages for the CTE. The main one is it breaks
15:32:5915 hours, 32 minutes, 59 secondsthe complexity of query into small multiple pieces which makes our query much easier to read and as well to understand. So it improves readability.
15:33:0815 hours, 33 minutes, 8 secondsAnother advantage of the city is that those small multiple pieces they are really easy to manage and to develop. So those pieces are like self-contained
15:33:1715 hours, 33 minutes, 17 secondswhich makes our queries more modular. So it introduces modularity inside our queries. And we also learned that the
15:33:2415 hours, 33 minutes, 24 secondsCTE help us to reduce the redundancy inside our queries where it makes the result of one query usable in multiple
15:33:3315 hours, 33 minutes, 33 secondsplaces inside our query. So it makes our code smaller and reduce redundancy. And one more advantage of the city is that
15:33:4015 hours, 33 minutes, 40 secondsit help us to do looping and iterating in SQL by using the recursive CTE. And we have understood as well that we can
15:33:4815 hours, 33 minutes, 48 secondstreat the CTE result as any other physical table inside our database. So we can treat it and handle it like any
15:33:5515 hours, 33 minutes, 55 secondsother tables. Only one exception that this table lives only in one query. So we cannot query the CTE from an external
15:34:0315 hours, 34 minutes, 3 secondsquery. Now we have learned that the result of the CTE could be used from the main query. This is the classical one.
15:34:1015 hours, 34 minutes, 10 secondsBut not only we can use it in the main query but also we can use it in another CTE query which leads to having nested
15:34:1815 hours, 34 minutes, 18 secondscities. And of course we have learned as well we can use the result of the CTE within itself which makes the CTE
15:34:2515 hours, 34 minutes, 25 secondsrecursive and allows for looping and iterating. And I can only keep recommending to not use more than five CTEs in one query. Otherwise you're
15:34:3415 hours, 34 minutes, 34 secondsgoing to get the exact opposite and benefits from cdes where your code going to be really hard to understand and to read and even to extend. Okay my friends
15:34:4215 hours, 34 minutes, 42 secondswith that we have covered this amazing and very important technique in SQL the common table expressions the city. Now in the next step we're going to talk
15:34:5015 hours, 34 minutes, 50 secondsabout a new type of objects that you can use in databases. We don't have only tables we have as well views. And views are amazing in order to give you dynamic
15:34:5915 hours, 34 minutes, 59 secondsand flexibility in your project. So let's talk about views. Now a view is not like a query

15:35:1515 hours, 35 minutes, 15 secondsI would like to give you the big picture, the whole structure of the database. So let's go. We have like hierarchy structure and the highest
15:35:2415 hours, 35 minutes, 24 secondslevel of this hierarchy is the SQL server. The SQL server manages multiple databases. It's like the control center
15:35:3115 hours, 35 minutes, 31 secondsthat keep everything running and accessible. Now inside the SQL server,
15:35:3615 hours, 35 minutes, 36 secondswe have multiple databases. So a database is collection of informations that are stored in structured way. It's
15:35:4315 hours, 35 minutes, 43 secondswhere all your data is kept and organized in different tables and objects. And each database is separated from others and it has its own data. Now
15:35:5215 hours, 35 minutes, 52 secondsinside each database we can find multiple schemas. A schema is like a logical way on how you group up related
15:35:5915 hours, 35 minutes, 59 secondsobjects like tables and views together within a database. Like for example, if you have a database called sales, we can group up different tables about the
15:36:0815 hours, 36 minutes, 8 secondsorders underneath the schema orders. And maybe we have like multiple views and tables about the customers where we can put it in the schema customers. So if
15:36:1715 hours, 36 minutes, 17 secondsyou find like multiple tables and views that are describing the same object, the same topic, we put them all together
15:36:2415 hours, 36 minutes, 24 secondsunderneath one schema. So again, a database could be like the sales database and the HR database. They are completely different types of data. And
15:36:3315 hours, 36 minutes, 33 secondsunderneath the sales, we can have like different sections. We have the sections about the orders and sections about the customers. And now moving on, what we
15:36:4015 hours, 36 minutes, 40 secondscan find inside the schema, we can find tables. A table is where actually your data is stored. It contains multiple
15:36:4715 hours, 36 minutes, 47 secondscolumns and rows. So it is where the data physically lives. And now inside the schemas, we have another type of object. We call it view. And of course
15:36:5615 hours, 36 minutes, 56 secondsin this section, we are focusing on the views. So a view is like a virtual table that has a structure and everything but
15:37:0315 hours, 37 minutes, 3 secondsinside it we don't have any data. So the view does not store any data and in order to see the data we have to execute
15:37:1015 hours, 37 minutes, 10 secondsthe query behind the view and only after that we're going to see some data but it is not like the tables it doesn't store the data permanently. Now inside the
15:37:1815 hours, 37 minutes, 18 secondstables we can define multiple stuff like columns and as well keys and the same thing for the views. Inside the views we can define multiple columns and one last
15:37:2815 hours, 37 minutes, 28 secondslevel for each column we have like a name and a data type. So as you can see the databases are really organized and we have like hierarchy where the top
15:37:3615 hours, 37 minutes, 36 secondsnode is the SQL server and the lowest node is the columns and rows. So this is what we call the database structure. Now
15:37:4415 hours, 37 minutes, 44 secondsin order for you to build and manage this structure we have set of commands we call it DDL the shortcut of data
15:37:5215 hours, 37 minutes, 52 secondsdefinition language. So the detail is a set of commands that allow us to define and manage the structure of the
15:38:0015 hours, 38 minutesdatabase. So we have commands like create where it help us to create databases, schemas, tables, views.

15:38:1415 hours, 38 minutes, 14 secondsin order to remove any database object like dropping a schema, dropping a database, tables, views. So as you can see the DDL commands can help us to
