15:40:1915 hours, 40 minutes, 19 secondsAnd if you go to the columns you will end up at the lowest level of the hierarchy. And here we have the columns like the customer ID and we have some
15:40:2715 hours, 40 minutes, 27 secondsextra informations like the data type length and so on. So this is the structure and the hierarchy of databases.
15:40:3715 hours, 40 minutes, 37 secondsNow I would like you to understand a fundamental concept on the database in order to understand the views the three-level architecture of the
15:40:4415 hours, 40 minutes, 44 secondsdatabase. This architecture can describe the different levels of data abstractions in a database. So let's see
15:40:5215 hours, 40 minutes, 52 secondswhat this means. So the architecture is divided into three levels. The first level is the physical level. Then we have the logical level and the third one
15:41:0015 hours, 41 minutesis the view level. Now let's understand each level what it means. So now the physical level it is the lowest level of
15:41:0715 hours, 41 minutes, 7 secondsthe database where the actual data is stored in a physical storage and usually who has access to this layer are the
15:41:1515 hours, 41 minutes, 15 secondsdatabase administrators because they are the experts and they have to manage the access and the security of this layer because they are the expert that have to
15:41:2415 hours, 41 minutes, 24 secondsmanage a lot of stuff like optimizing the performance making sure that everything is secure and managing the backup and recovery and to do all the
15:41:3215 hours, 41 minutes, 32 secondsconfigurations and many other tasks. So at the physical layer we have to deal with a lot of stuff like the data files,
15:41:3915 hours, 41 minutes, 39 secondspartitions, logs, cataloges, blocks and caches and many other stuff that each database needs in order to store your
15:41:4615 hours, 41 minutes, 46 secondsdata. So as you can see this layer is very complicated and you need to be really an expert of databases in order
15:41:5415 hours, 41 minutes, 54 secondsto be able to manage all those stuff. So we call this layer a physical layer or sometimes we call it an internal layer.
15:42:0115 hours, 42 minutes, 1 secondSo now let's move to the next level. we have the logical level. So the logical layer it is less complicated than the physical layer. Here at this level you
15:42:0915 hours, 42 minutes, 9 secondshave to deal on how to organize your data and normally we have here like an application developer or we have like
15:42:1615 hours, 42 minutes, 16 secondsdata engineers that access the logical level in order to define the structure of your data. So those developers can
15:42:2315 hours, 42 minutes, 23 secondsfocus on how to structure your data rather than how the data is exactly storing the data physically at the storage. So they don't have to deal with
15:42:3115 hours, 42 minutes, 31 secondsall those details. they leave it for the database administrator and they can focus only on how to structure the data.
15:42:3815 hours, 42 minutes, 38 secondsThat's why we need for this kind of role an abstraction level for them which is the logical level. So now what actually the developers are doing at this level?
15:42:4815 hours, 42 minutes, 48 secondsWell, they are like creating tables and defining the relationships between those tables or they can go and define views.
15:42:5615 hours, 42 minutes, 56 secondsthey can create indexes on the tables in order to optimize the performance of the tables or maybe they are creating stored
15:43:0315 hours, 43 minutes, 3 secondsprocedures and functions and some other codes in order to manage those tables.
15:43:0815 hours, 43 minutes, 8 secondsSo as you can see they are building the data model they are structuring your data but they don't care at all where are those data stored physically in the
15:43:1715 hours, 43 minutes, 17 secondsdatabase. So as you can see here things are less complicated than the physical layer and it is perfect abstraction for developers to build projects. So we call
15:43:2515 hours, 43 minutes, 25 secondsthis the logical layer or sometimes we call it the conceptual layer. Okay. So now moving on to another level of
15:43:3215 hours, 43 minutes, 32 secondsabstraction. We have the view level. So the view level is the highest level of abstraction in the database and it is
15:43:4015 hours, 43 minutes, 40 secondswhat the end users and applications can access and can see. So for example, you could have like one view for business
15:43:4815 hours, 43 minutes, 48 secondsanalyst. So you prepare and customize a views that are suitable only for the business analyst and you might say you
15:43:5515 hours, 43 minutes, 55 secondsknow what let's prepare another set of views that are suitable for data visualizations and reporting like you
15:44:0315 hours, 44 minutes, 3 secondscan go and connect for example a PowerBI in order to create dashboards. So they are fully customized and prepared views
15:44:1015 hours, 44 minutes, 10 secondsin order to be connected with the PowerBI reports and you can keep doing that by creating multiple set of views
15:44:1815 hours, 44 minutes, 18 secondsthat are suitable for specific purpose and use case. So as you can see at this level we are exposing our data for multiple users and multiple
15:44:2715 hours, 44 minutes, 27 secondsapplications. So now the question is what do we have to deal at the view level? Well, you have their only views that holds only the relevant informations for the use case or users.
15:44:3815 hours, 44 minutes, 38 secondsSo the users at this level have only views. They don't have to deal with the tables, indexes, store procedures, any files, logs, partitions or anything.
15:44:4815 hours, 44 minutes, 48 secondsThis is the highest level of abstraction because the focus of this layer is to make it friendly for the end users and
15:44:5515 hours, 44 minutes, 55 secondseasy to consume. So we call this layer the view layer or sometimes we call it an external layer. So this is the three-level architecture of the
15:45:0415 hours, 45 minutes, 4 secondsdatabases or we call it the three abstraction levels of the database. So the physical layer has the highest complexity, the lowest abstraction and
15:45:1315 hours, 45 minutes, 13 secondsthe view layer has the highest abstraction. So this is one more reason why the views are very important concept in SQL
15:45:2115 hours, 45 minutes, 21 seconds[Music]
15:45:2415 hours, 45 minutes, 24 secondsdatabases. Okay. So with that we have enough fundamentals in order to start talking about the views. So the question is what are views? A view is a virtual
15:45:3315 hours, 45 minutes, 33 secondstable in SQL that is based on the result of a query without actually storing the data in the database. So in short this
15:45:4115 hours, 45 minutes, 41 secondsmeans views are stored or persisted SQL query in the database. So let's understand what this exactly means. Now
15:45:5015 hours, 45 minutes, 50 secondsso far what you have learned we have like database table and all what you have done we create a select query in order to retrieve the data from this
15:45:5715 hours, 45 minutes, 57 secondstable. So once we execute our query we will get the result back. Now if you are talking about views they have as well like the structure of the table but
15:46:0615 hours, 46 minutes, 6 secondswithout any data inside it. And for each view there is like a query attached to it. So there is no data but we have like
15:46:1315 hours, 46 minutes, 13 secondsa query in order to get data. We call the normal table as a physical table and the view we call it a virtual table. So
15:46:2115 hours, 46 minutes, 21 secondsnow how exactly we're going to get the data. So now if you go and write query by selecting data from the view not from the table from the view what going to
15:46:2915 hours, 46 minutes, 29 secondshappen SQL going to go and trigger the queue that is attached to the view and this query is responsible to query the
15:46:3715 hours, 46 minutes, 37 secondsphysical table and then the result going to fill the structure of the view and we will get back of course the results. So we are directly querying a view but
15:46:4615 hours, 46 minutes, 46 secondsactually we are indirectly querying a physical table. So the view is like between us and the data. So that means
15:46:5315 hours, 46 minutes, 53 secondsmy real data is stored inside the database tables and the views are like an abstraction layer between me and my
15:47:0015 hours, 47 minutesreal data. And of course the data will not be stored inside the view. Each time I'm querying the view what's going to happen the SQL query behind the view
15:47:0915 hours, 47 minutes, 9 secondsgoing to be executed again. So it's going to go and retrieve the data and get it back to the view and then I will see it in the output. So this is what we
15:47:1715 hours, 47 minutes, 17 secondsmean with SQL view. So now let's have a quick comparison between tables and views.
15:47:2515 hours, 47 minutes, 25 secondsTables stores the actual data physically at a database. So the tables where the data is persisted with in the other hand
15:47:3215 hours, 47 minutes, 32 secondsthe views they are virtual tables and they do not store any data inside the database but they present the data from the underlying tables. So that means views don't persist any data physically.
15:47:4415 hours, 47 minutes, 44 secondsNow the tables are hard to maintain and as well hard to change. So it needs a lot of efforts in order to do any change like adding columns and moving columns
15:47:5315 hours, 47 minutes, 53 secondsalways requires a lot of efforts for the migration especially if you have large tables. But in the other hand the views
15:48:0015 hours, 48 minutesare way easier to maintain and very flexible to change. All what you have to do is only to change the query of the view. So that means you can very quickly
15:48:0815 hours, 48 minutes, 8 secondschange stuff in the views compared to the tables. But if you are talking about performance, tables are faster than views. For example, if you go and do a
15:48:1615 hours, 48 minutes, 16 secondssimple select on the table, you will get the data back as soon as the database fetches the data. But if you are selecting something from the view, it is
15:48:2415 hours, 48 minutes, 24 secondsactually two queries. The query that comes from the user and as well the second query is the view query. and the query of the view could be very
15:48:3315 hours, 48 minutes, 33 secondscomplicated in order to extract the data from the underlying table. So selecting something from the view is always slower than selecting something from a table.
15:48:4215 hours, 48 minutes, 42 secondsNow if you have a table you can read from the table and as well you can write to a table but the views are read only as the name says it is only a view. You
15:48:5115 hours, 48 minutes, 51 secondscannot go and write something to the database using the view. Okay. So those are the big differences between views and tables.
15:49:0215 hours, 49 minutes, 2 secondsAll right. So with that we have a clear understanding what are views. But now you might ask me why do we need views?
15:49:0815 hours, 49 minutes, 8 secondsThat's why now what we're going to do we're going to deep dive into multiple scenarios and use cases that you might encounter in your SQL projects. So let's
15:49:1615 hours, 49 minutes, 16 secondsstart with the first use case. The first use case and the core reason why we use views in our data projects is to store
15:49:2315 hours, 49 minutes, 23 secondscentral logic from a complex query in the database so that everyone can access it and with that we improve reusability
15:49:3115 hours, 49 minutes, 31 secondsbetween multiple queries and we reduce as well the complexity of the overall projects. So let's understand what this means. So now in our project we have
15:49:3915 hours, 49 minutes, 39 secondslike two tables in the database orders and customers and we have learned previously that if we have like a complex query we can go and use the
15:49:4715 hours, 49 minutes, 47 secondscity. So for example in our city we are joining tables and doing some aggregations using the sum and the city going to store the data in an
15:49:5415 hours, 49 minutes, 54 secondsintermediate results and then we have the main query. For example we are doing the step two where we are ranking the data. So the whole thing is in one query
15:50:0315 hours, 50 minutes, 3 secondsand let's say that a financial analyst was doing this type of analyszis. Now what could happen is that you might have
15:50:1015 hours, 50 minutes, 10 secondsanother user for example a budget analyst where he is doing exactly the same first step. So he has as well a
15:50:1715 hours, 50 minutes, 17 secondscity query where first the data are joined and then aggregated using the sum. But the last step in the main query he's not doing ranking he's just doing
15:50:2515 hours, 50 minutes, 25 secondslike max and min. And not only that, we have a third user, the risk analyst,
15:50:3015 hours, 50 minutes, 30 secondswhere as well doing the same initial step using the CTE, joining the tables and doing the summarization. But here
15:50:3715 hours, 50 minutes, 37 secondsthe risk analyst in this scenario, he's just comparing the data at the last step in the main query. So now if you sit back and look to this, you can see all
15:50:4515 hours, 50 minutes, 45 secondsthose three data workers, all of them are doing the same first step. So all of them are doing the same CTE. They are joining the data and then doing
15:50:5315 hours, 50 minutes, 53 secondssummarization. And of course this is a complete waste of time that each one of them has to create first the city from the scratch in order to do some
15:51:0115 hours, 51 minutes, 1 secondanalyszis. So it is complete redundancy and makes no sense. So this is exactly the disadvantage of only using cities in
15:51:0915 hours, 51 minutes, 9 secondsthe projects. Now what we can do instead of that those three data workers going to decide to say you know what let's put the first step as view in the database.
15:51:1815 hours, 51 minutes, 18 secondsSo instead of using CTE each time we're going to take this script and put it in the database. So we have now a central
15:51:2615 hours, 51 minutes, 26 secondslogic that is stored in the database where everyone can use it. So we have this query this logic only once and
15:51:3315 hours, 51 minutes, 33 secondseveryone can benefit from it. So now the financial analyst instead of going directly to the physical tables they can
15:51:4015 hours, 51 minutes, 40 secondsgo to the view. So thus means she needs only to write one script the rank script. Same thing goes for the budget analyst. he has only to write the query
15:51:4915 hours, 51 minutes, 49 secondsfor the max and min and as well for the risk analyst he just need to compare the data. So as you can see all those queries are reduced and they can only
15:51:5715 hours, 51 minutes, 57 secondsfocus on the analyzes. So this is exactly the magic of views in data analytics. This logic this knowledge can
15:52:0415 hours, 52 minutes, 4 secondsbe centralized in the database and this is way faster and better than having this logic written each time someone
15:52:1115 hours, 52 minutes, 11 secondswant to do any analyzes. So this is why we need views in data projects.
15:52:2015 hours, 52 minutes, 20 secondsSo now if you compare views with CTE,
15:52:2215 hours, 52 minutes, 22 secondsthe CTE are used in order to reduce the redundancy within one single query. So it improves the reusability within one
15:52:3115 hours, 52 minutes, 31 secondsquery. Where in the other hand in the views we are reducing the redundancies from multiple queries. So we are
15:52:3815 hours, 52 minutes, 38 secondsreducing the complexity of the whole project. So the views are improving the reusability in multiple queries. Now
15:52:4615 hours, 52 minutes, 46 secondsthink about it like this. We use views in order to persist a logic in the database. So the logic is so important
15:52:5415 hours, 52 minutes, 54 secondsthat we want to persist it in the database. It's like in the tables we persist data but with the views we are persisting logic. But in the other hand in the CTE the logic is not persisted.
15:53:0515 hours, 53 minutes, 5 secondsIt is temporary and going to be calculated only on the fly within the scope of one query. So this logic is
15:53:1215 hours, 53 minutes, 12 secondsimportant only in this scenario and it is not important for any other queries.
15:53:1715 hours, 53 minutes, 17 secondsThat's why it makes no sense to persist it using the views. So you have to decide is this logic is very important
15:53:2415 hours, 53 minutes, 24 secondsthen take it away from the city and put it in the view. But if you think you know what this logic is not really important and only important in this one
15:53:3215 hours, 53 minutes, 32 secondsquery then stay with the city because creating views always needs some extra steps in order to maintain the view. You
15:53:4015 hours, 53 minutes, 40 secondshave to create the view. You have to drop the view if you don't need it. But the CTE, there is almost no maintenance for it. The database going to do
15:53:4715 hours, 53 minutes, 47 secondsautomatically the cleanup once the query is done. So there is no extra activity to drop a city or something. That's why
15:53:5515 hours, 53 minutes, 55 secondsCTE is easier to use than views. So those are the big difference between the views and
15:54:0315 hours, 54 minutes, 3 secondscities. Okay. So now let's check quickly the syntax of a view. So now we have a query like select from where. So this is
15:54:1015 hours, 54 minutes, 10 secondsa query a simple select statement. But now in order to create a view an object in database we have to go and use a DDL
15:54:1815 hours, 54 minutes, 18 secondscommand create. So we're going to say create view cuz we want to create a view then the name of the view and then it's
15:54:2515 hours, 54 minutes, 25 secondslike the CTE we say as and then double parenthesis. So as you can see it's very simple and we call this a DDL command
15:54:3315 hours, 54 minutes, 33 secondswhere we are telling the database go and create a view and the logic of the view comes from this query. So it's very simple. This is how you can create views

16:04:3116 hours, 4 minutes, 31 secondsthe TSQL the transacts SQL it is like an extension for SQL only in SQL server well it's like programming where you can
16:04:3916 hours, 4 minutes, 39 secondsgo and add variables or you can all go and add checks we will not do a deep dive in this language but I would like to show you how to do it for the views
16:04:4616 hours, 4 minutes, 46 secondsso just follow me with that I'm going to go and replace the whole thing and then we're going to say if and now we are checking the system catalog if the
16:04:5516 hours, 4 minutes, 55 secondsobject ID And now we go and specify the view name.
16:05:0016 hours, 5 minutesSo let's go and copy the whole thing with the schema as well. And then we're going to say for SQL this is a view. So
16:05:0716 hours, 5 minutes, 7 secondsif this object exists so we are saying is not null. So that means it exist in the catalog then what SQL should do?
16:05:1516 hours, 5 minutes, 15 secondsShould drop this view. So we're going to say drop view and it's like we have done it first and then semicolon and then we
16:05:2316 hours, 5 minutes, 23 secondssay for scale go and with that we are saying for SQL the tscale is done. So the logic is done and after that we have
16:05:3116 hours, 5 minutes, 31 secondsthe DDL for our view. So again what we are doing we are checking before creating the view whether the view exist. If it exist then we are telling
16:05:4016 hours, 5 minutes, 40 secondsthe scale go and drop it and if it doesn't exist that means we haven't created this view yet. it is completely brand new view then this step going to
16:05:4916 hours, 5 minutes, 49 secondsbe skipped so that there is nothing to drop. So now if you go and execute the whole thing it will work and of course if you go and refresh over here you
16:05:5616 hours, 5 minutes, 56 secondsstill see the view. So SQL did destroy the table first and then recreated. So if you execute it again. So this is how
16:06:0416 hours, 6 minutes, 4 secondsyou replace your logic in view in SQL server. And with that we have learned all possible scenarios. How to create a
16:06:1116 hours, 6 minutes, 11 secondsview, how to drop a view and how to update the logic of a
16:06:1916 hours, 6 minutes, 19 secondsview. Now back to our database architecture and let's understand how the database executes views. So now
16:06:2616 hours, 6 minutes, 26 secondslet's say that the data engineer is creating view called top end. So the query going to be sent to the database engine and once the database engine
16:06:3416 hours, 6 minutes, 34 secondsunderstand this is a view this is not a table. So now the database engine going to go to the disk storage and to the catalog and it will stores not only the
16:06:4316 hours, 6 minutes, 43 secondsmetadata about the view also the SQL that is responsible for the view. So it's going to take the SQL statements
16:06:5016 hours, 6 minutes, 50 secondsthat you have defined in the create view and place it as well in the catalog. So if you compare to the tables we have in tables only metadata but in the views we
16:07:0016 hours, 7 minuteshave both the metadata and as well the query of the view and as well you can see that the database engine will not
16:07:0716 hours, 7 minutes, 7 secondscreate a table in the user's data. So there is nowhere data stored inside the disk or the cache. So the actual data
16:07:1516 hours, 7 minutes, 15 secondsthe physical data will not be stored anywhere. We are storing only metadata and the query inside the system catalog.
16:07:2216 hours, 7 minutes, 22 secondsSo now we tell our data analyst okay we have a new view and the data analyst can go and write a query in order to retrieve the data from the view. So he
16:07:3016 hours, 7 minutes, 30 secondsgoing to say and say select from the view and execute it. The database engine going to take it and understand okay now we are talking about view. So the
16:07:3716 hours, 7 minutes, 37 secondsdatabase first has to retrieve not the data going to retrieve the query from the catalog in order to understand what
16:07:4516 hours, 7 minutes, 45 secondsdo we have now to execute. Then the database going to execute the query of the view first and the data for this query comes from a physical table called
16:07:5416 hours, 7 minutes, 54 secondsorders. So now the database engine is querying the order to retrieve the data so that we have a data for the end user and then it's going to be executed and
16:08:0216 hours, 8 minutes, 2 secondsthe result going to be sent back to the data analyst. So as you can see there is like two queries. The SQL engine first
16:08:0916 hours, 8 minutes, 9 secondshas to execute the query from the view and only after that the database engine can execute the query that comes from
16:08:1716 hours, 8 minutes, 17 secondsthe user. So actually the data comes always from a physical table but we are not providing the data analyst an access to the table. We are just providing an
16:08:2616 hours, 8 minutes, 26 secondsaccess to the view. So this can happen each time an end user selecting data from the view. Always the database engine going to grab the query from the
16:08:3416 hours, 8 minutes, 34 secondscatalog, execute it first in order to get the data and then execute what the end user wants. And now if the data engineer says no, let's go and drop the
16:08:4216 hours, 8 minutes, 42 secondsview. So she writes a query in order to drop the view. And the database engine going to go to the system catalog and delete both the metadata and the query.
16:08:5116 hours, 8 minutes, 51 secondsSo as you can see, if you are dropping a view, you are not losing the actual data. So there will be no user data lost at all. So don't worry about it. What
16:09:0016 hours, 9 minutesyou are losing is only the query and the metadata about your view. It's only if you drop a physical table like the
16:09:0716 hours, 9 minutes, 7 secondsorders, you will lose your data. So dropping views is not that bad like dropping a database table. So this is how the database works with the views behind the scenes.
16:09:2016 hours, 9 minutes, 20 secondsNow moving on to the second scenario to the next use case of using views in projects is that we use views in order to hide complexity and to improve
16:09:2916 hours, 9 minutes, 29 secondsabstraction. In many scenarios we work with a very large and complex databases and we can use views in order to reduce
16:09:3716 hours, 9 minutes, 37 secondsthe complexity and make things easier for the users. So let's understand what this means. Now I'm going to explain for you a scenario that happens almost in
16:09:4516 hours, 9 minutes, 45 secondseach project. Like if you get an access to a database where you want to do analyzes, you will be in scenario and this can happen a lot where you're going
16:09:5216 hours, 9 minutes, 52 secondsto find a large database where the tables are very complex to understand.
16:09:5616 hours, 9 minutes, 56 secondsThey have a lot of columns. They have like technical and cryptical names and how tables are connected to each others and relationship between them. It's
16:10:0416 hours, 10 minutes, 4 secondsalmost impossible to understand. then you have to be deeply involved with the data models with documentations and with
16:10:1116 hours, 10 minutes, 11 secondsexperts until you understand how to query this database. So if you are not a developer and from end user perspective
16:10:1916 hours, 10 minutes, 19 secondsit can be nightmare where you are trying to do multiple joins in order to make simple analyzes and of course from the database perspective this data model is
16:10:2816 hours, 10 minutes, 28 secondsgood enough for one application but if you are opening your database for multiple data analyszis projects this can be a nightmare because you have to
16:10:3616 hours, 10 minutes, 36 secondsgo and explain for each user how to query the data. So what we usually do instead of giving a direct access to
16:10:4316 hours, 10 minutes, 43 secondssuch technical and hard to understand data model we go as developers creating multiple views since we are the expert
16:10:5116 hours, 10 minutes, 51 secondsof the data model and these new views going to be an abstraction of the complexity that I have in my database
16:10:5816 hours, 10 minutes, 58 secondsand we have to make sure that those views are providing objects that are friendly. So they have like a full
16:11:0516 hours, 11 minutes, 5 secondsEnglish name that makes sense and as well the columns are friendly and we try to not offer a lot of views so the user don't have to do all the joins. So we
16:11:1416 hours, 11 minutes, 14 secondsprovide like few views that are friendly and has a lot of informations that the users needs for the analyzes. So with
16:11:2216 hours, 11 minutes, 22 secondsthat the users can have an access to something more friendly and easy to consume and then they can write simple queries in order to do analyzes on top
16:11:3016 hours, 11 minutes, 30 secondsof these friendly views. And this is what we can give a name like we are providing a data product from my complex
16:11:3916 hours, 11 minutes, 39 secondsphysical database. So here again how important are the views to provide an abstraction and easy to consume objects
16:11:4616 hours, 11 minutes, 46 secondsfor the users and with that I can hide all my complexity and the script of the view going to be developed from the
16:11:5416 hours, 11 minutes, 54 secondsexperts and only once so that the users don't have to understand or to write these complex SQL joins and with that
16:12:0216 hours, 12 minutes, 2 secondsyou can make your data projects way easier than before. So this is another important use case for the views where we can use it in order to provide
16:12:1016 hours, 12 minutes, 10 secondsabstraction and as well easy and friendly objects for the end users.

16:19:5416 hours, 19 minutes, 54 secondsviews. Okay. Moving on to the next scenario to the next use case. We use SQL views in order to implement security and to protect our data in the database.
16:20:0316 hours, 20 minutes, 3 secondsIn many scenarios, we have sensitive informations in our data and we cannot go and share it with everyone. So one of the best practices is to create views in
16:20:1216 hours, 20 minutes, 12 secondsorder to protect your data before sharing it with the users. So let's understand what this means. So now let's understand first the scenario without
16:20:2016 hours, 20 minutes, 20 secondsviews only tables. So now let's say that you have the table orders four columns and three rows and then you have like for example a manager that has an access
16:20:2816 hours, 20 minutes, 28 secondsdirectly to the database and start writing some queries in order to retrieve data. But in your project you have multiple people that has an access
16:20:3616 hours, 20 minutes, 36 secondsto your database like for example a data analyst and as well she is writing a script in order to retrieve data from the orders and as well you have maybe a
16:20:4416 hours, 20 minutes, 44 secondsstudents that has an access to your database and querying the data like any other role like a manager and data analyst. So as you can see you have now
16:20:5116 hours, 20 minutes, 51 secondsdifferent rules in your project and all of them having the same rights by accessing directly your table. So a
16:20:5816 hours, 20 minutes, 58 secondsmanager or data analyst or a student they are seeing the whole table all rows and all columns. And of course in the real projects this is a big problem.
16:21:0816 hours, 21 minutes, 8 secondsSometimes the data are sensitive and you cannot give an access for everyone. And of course if you are using only tables this going to be a nightmare because you
16:21:1516 hours, 21 minutes, 15 secondscan go and create multiple tables but it's going to be really hard to make all those tables in sync. But instead of that we have views. So what you can do
16:21:2316 hours, 21 minutes, 23 secondsyou can go and remove all accesses to the physical table but instead you can go and create multiple views for each
16:21:3116 hours, 21 minutes, 31 secondsrole. For example you can go and create a view called orders managers and maybe you can give all the data and all the columns because the managers are allowed
16:21:4016 hours, 21 minutes, 40 secondsto see let's say sensitive data but still it's nice to create a view maybe you change your mind later and you go and remove something. Now let's say that
16:21:4716 hours, 21 minutes, 47 secondsfor the data analyst you want to offer all the data but there is only one column that is very sensitive. So what you can do you can go and create another
16:21:5516 hours, 21 minutes, 55 secondsview called orders analyst. So in the view only three columns are available ABC and then you give access to all data
16:22:0216 hours, 22 minutes, 2 secondsanalyst and with that you have protected this sensitive information. So we call this column level security. And now we come to our poor students. And here we
16:22:1116 hours, 22 minutes, 11 secondscreate another view where we are not only protecting the column D but also we are protecting few rows like for example
16:22:1916 hours, 22 minutes, 19 secondsthe row number three because we want to offer only few informations to the students. So we are protecting the columns and as well the rows and for
16:22:2616 hours, 22 minutes, 26 secondsthat we can create another dedicated view called for example orders students and we can offer it to the students and with that we are doing column level security and as well row level security.
16:22:3816 hours, 22 minutes, 38 secondsSo we are offering multiple views very easily without having to worry how to load the data from one table to another.
16:22:4516 hours, 22 minutes, 45 secondsSo creating those views are really easy and provide us a perfect tool in order to manage the security of our data. So
16:22:5316 hours, 22 minutes, 53 secondsthis is one very common use case of using views in data projects. All right.
16:22:5716 hours, 22 minutes, 57 secondsSo now let's have the following task and it says provide a view for EU sales team that combines details from all tables

16:25:3516 hours, 25 minutes, 35 secondscan see views are really great in order to provide security to our data whether we are protecting the columns or the rows. For example in our view we can say
16:25:4416 hours, 25 minutes, 44 secondsnot only I want to remove the USA orders but let's say the department information is sensitive information and I would
16:25:5216 hours, 25 minutes, 52 secondslike to hide it from the view. So you can just simply remove it from the select and with that you are doing column level security. So now I have two options that I can provide to the users.
16:26:0116 hours, 26 minutes, 1 secondThe first option doesn't has any like role level security. It is the first view the order details. We don't have there any filters. So it's going to show
16:26:0916 hours, 26 minutes, 9 secondsall the orders. So here we give access only to people that are allowed to see all data. And we have another option the details with the EU. It doesn't show all
16:26:1716 hours, 26 minutes, 17 secondsdata. It shows only a subset that is relevant for the EU team. So now it's really easy to control the security of my data using the views. And this is
16:26:2616 hours, 26 minutes, 26 secondsvery important use case for the
16:26:2816 hours, 26 minutes, 28 seconds[Music]
16:26:3116 hours, 26 minutes, 31 secondsviews. Okay. Okay, so moving on to the next use case for the views, we can use it in order to have more dynamic and flexibility in our projects. So let's
16:26:4016 hours, 26 minutes, 40 secondsunderstand what this means. If you have a table and you have multiple users accessing this table, now what can happen? you might change your mind about
16:26:4716 hours, 26 minutes, 47 secondsthe design and the data model of your database where you can say you know what instead of having one table I'm going to go and split it into two tables or maybe
16:26:5516 hours, 26 minutes, 55 secondsanother decision you say you know what I'm going to go and rename a table or in another day you decide you know what let's go and rename few columns or maybe
16:27:0316 hours, 27 minutes, 3 secondsadd a column remove column so you are doing changes to your physical data model and you are changing stuff in the tables you know what's going to happen
16:27:1016 hours, 27 minutes, 10 secondsall those users that are accessing the tables going to scream because all of them having a complex SQL queries and
16:27:1816 hours, 27 minutes, 18 secondsyour small changes at the tables are breaking everything in their queries and what this means this means escalations
16:27:2516 hours, 27 minutes, 25 secondsand you don't have anymore the freedom to change anything in your database without talking before to 100 people before doing any change. So we don't do
16:27:3416 hours, 27 minutes, 34 secondsthat instead of that we use views. So what's going to happen? You create a view and you tell the users, okay, take this view and consume it and leave me
16:27:4216 hours, 27 minutes, 42 secondsalone. And now you have again your freedom to do any changes you want. So you go to your tables and do splitting,
16:27:4816 hours, 27 minutes, 48 secondsrenaming and changing everything you want as long as you are updating the query between the table and the view to
16:27:5616 hours, 27 minutes, 56 secondsmake sure that the users are not noticing any change. So for example, if you go and split the table into two tables, then you have to put in the view
16:28:0316 hours, 28 minutes, 3 secondsa join or union in order to reconstruct the same structure that the users are used to. And if you would like to rename
16:28:1116 hours, 28 minutes, 11 secondssomething in your database, like instead of ID, you are now calling it a key. All what you have to do now is to go to the query of the view and rename it back
16:28:2016 hours, 28 minutes, 20 secondsfrom a key to an ID. So no one going to notice that you are doing changes to the physical tables. So using views and
16:28:2716 hours, 28 minutes, 27 secondsoffering it to users is a gamecher for you because giving the users views kind of gives you more freedom dynamic and
16:28:3416 hours, 28 minutes, 34 secondsflexibility to change anything in your data model and the tables without getting any headache. So this is amazing use case for the
16:28:4516 hours, 28 minutes, 45 secondsviews. Okay, moving on. We have a lot of use cases for the views. They are just amazing. So the next one is we can use
16:28:5216 hours, 28 minutes, 52 secondsviews in order to introduce a second version of my data model in another language. So we could offer multiple languages to the users. Let's understand
16:29:0016 hours, 29 minuteswhat this means. So now we have the following scenario. We have again our table orders where the data is persisted and everything in English and of course

16:30:4916 hours, 30 minutes, 49 secondsviews. Now we come to my favorite use case for the views and that I personally recommend in each project that we can
16:30:5616 hours, 30 minutes, 56 secondsuse views as a virtual data ms in a data warehouse. So now why this is my favorite? Because I'm specialist in data
16:31:0316 hours, 31 minutes, 3 secondswarehouses and data leaks and this topic is very important decision in each project like this. So let's understand what this means. So now a classical data
16:31:1216 hours, 31 minutes, 12 secondswarehouse architecture based on the approach of enmon is going to look like this. We have multiple source systems where our data are spreaded and now we
16:31:2016 hours, 31 minutes, 20 secondswould like to go and extract all our data from these multiple sources and put it in one big database called data
16:31:2816 hours, 31 minutes, 28 secondswarehouse. And there will be a lot of operations on this central database like the data going to be first cleaned and then maybe integrated together and maybe
16:31:3616 hours, 31 minutes, 36 secondswe are building there some historical data. So we're going to be doing multiple steps in order to prepare the data for complex reporting and analyzes.
16:31:4416 hours, 31 minutes, 44 secondsAnd what we usually do in the data warehouse, we're going to store all those informations as a physical table.
16:31:5016 hours, 31 minutes, 50 secondsNow once we have built the data warehouse, what's going to happen? We're going to have multiple use cases that would like to access the data warehouse
16:31:5816 hours, 31 minutes, 58 secondsin order maybe to do some different reporting. Now, it's going to be very complex if we connect immediately like a reporting engine like PowerBI directly
16:32:0616 hours, 32 minutes, 6 secondsto the data warehouse. But instead of this, we try to split the data warehouse into multiple subsets like we can split
16:32:1316 hours, 32 minutes, 13 secondsit after topic or domain or departments and we call those subsets as data marts.
16:32:1916 hours, 32 minutes, 19 secondsSo a data mart is always specific for a use case that's focus on one topic like for example we could have a dedicated
16:32:2616 hours, 32 minutes, 26 secondsmart for the sales and another data m which is dedicated only for finance topics but both of them comes from our
16:32:3416 hours, 32 minutes, 34 secondsdata warehouse. Then the last layer going to be like for example the reporting and dashboarding maybe you have something like powerbi where you are creating a dashboard one data m like
16:32:4316 hours, 32 minutes, 43 secondsthe sales or and as well maybe few stuff from other marts. But now the big question here in the data mart is how should I store the data? Should I store
16:32:5116 hours, 32 minutes, 51 secondsthe data using tables or should I use views? And now the best practice says if you are building data marts then use
16:32:5816 hours, 32 minutes, 58 secondsviews. And we call this virtual data marts. And there are many reasons why using views at a data mart it's way
16:33:0516 hours, 33 minutes, 5 secondsbetter than using tables. Like for example, it is more dynamic and quicker to change them cuz usually at the data mart you are building a lot of business
16:33:1316 hours, 33 minutes, 13 secondslogics and you want to have some flexibility and speed and the maintenance efforts is very simplified.
16:33:1916 hours, 33 minutes, 19 secondsNo need to build any ETLs or data loads from the data warehouse to the data parts and this makes the data warehouse
16:33:2616 hours, 33 minutes, 26 secondsas a real single point of truth for your data. And once you start copying data from one layer to another layer, it's going to be really hard to maintain and
16:33:3516 hours, 33 minutes, 35 secondschaotic and you have to have really restrict monitoring and data quality. So that's why using views you're going to always reflect the status of the data
16:33:4316 hours, 33 minutes, 43 secondswarehouse and this can help you of course with the data consistency which is a critical point in each data warehouse project. So there are many
16:33:5116 hours, 33 minutes, 51 secondsreasons why we build virtual data mart and we go with the views in this layer.
16:33:5616 hours, 33 minutes, 56 secondsSo as you can see how the views are playing a very important role in building a data warehouse. So this is another amazing and very important use
16:34:0516 hours, 34 minutes, 5 secondscase of using views in your data projects. All right friends, so now
16:34:1316 hours, 34 minutes, 13 secondslet's have a quick recap about views. So we have learned that views are a virtual table that is based on the result of a
16:34:2016 hours, 34 minutes, 20 secondsquery without actually storing any data in the database. So we use views in order to presist a complex SQL logic and
16:34:2816 hours, 34 minutes, 28 secondsquery in the database. And we have learned that in some scenarios views are better than CTE because it improves the
16:34:3516 hours, 34 minutes, 35 secondsreusability and reduce the complexity in multiple queries which reduce the complexity of the whole projects where
16:34:4316 hours, 34 minutes, 43 secondsthe CTE only improves the reusability in one query. And we have learned that as well the views in some scenarios are better than tables. We have learned that
16:34:5116 hours, 34 minutes, 51 secondsthey are very flexible and easier to maintain since they don't store any data and it's really fast and easy to change stuff in the view compared to the
16:35:0016 hours, 35 minutestables. But as well we have learned that the tables are faster than views. Now there are like endless use cases for the views. But from my experience in
16:35:0816 hours, 35 minutes, 8 secondsprojects I have choose for you the best use cases for the views. The first use case is if we find like a common repeated logic in SQL queries, we can go
16:35:1816 hours, 35 minutes, 18 secondsand store this logic in view in the database so that the users don't have to keep repeating the logic over and over.
16:35:2416 hours, 35 minutes, 24 secondsSo we use views in order to have a central business logic. Another use case is to hide the complexity of your physical data model and to offer for the
16:35:3316 hours, 35 minutes, 33 secondsusers and high abstracted layer. So you provide for the user something very friendly and you hide all the complex technical data model that you have in
16:35:4116 hours, 35 minutes, 41 secondsthe database because not everyone is expert with your data model. One more use case we can use views in order to implement security and to protect our
16:35:5016 hours, 35 minutes, 50 secondssensitive data in the database. So we can offer multiple views in order to protect columns or rows in a table.
16:35:5616 hours, 35 minutes, 56 secondsAnother use case we have learned that we can use views in order to have more dynamic and flexibility for your database where we offer the users a
16:36:0516 hours, 36 minutes, 5 secondstable view and then you have the freedom to change stuff at your physical data model without affecting all users. And another nice use case for the views we
16:36:1416 hours, 36 minutes, 14 secondscan offer multiple languages from our data model. And the last use case we have learned how views play an important
16:36:2116 hours, 36 minutes, 21 secondsrole in a data warehouse system. So views are amazing. All right my friends.
16:36:2516 hours, 36 minutes, 25 secondsSo with that we have learned everything about this new objects the views in databases. This is amazing for flexibility and dynamic in your projects. Now in the next one we're
16:36:3416 hours, 36 minutes, 34 secondsgoing to learn how to create tables based on query and we will learn about the temporary tables. So let's
16:36:4316 hours, 36 minutes, 43 secondsgo. Okay. So now first let's have a look again to the database structure. We have learned that in each SQL server there are multiple databases and in each
16:36:5216 hours, 36 minutes, 52 secondsdatabase there are multiple schemas. And now inside each schema we can define multiple objects like we can define tables and views. And now we will be
16:37:0116 hours, 37 minutes, 1 secondfocusing on the object table. And we have learned as well we can use the language DDL data definition language which is a set of SQL commands in order
16:37:1016 hours, 37 minutes, 10 secondsto define this database structure. So we can use the SQL command create in order to define a new table or alter in order
16:37:1716 hours, 37 minutes, 17 secondsto update the structure or drop in order to drop the whole table. So a table is an object in the database structure and
16:37:2516 hours, 37 minutes, 25 secondswe have learned as well there is three levels of the database architecture and we have understood that at the logical level the middle one the conceptual
16:37:3316 hours, 37 minutes, 33 secondslevel we deal as application developer or data engineer with the tables. So we define tables and relationship between
16:37:4116 hours, 37 minutes, 41 secondsthem. So if you are an end user or a business analyst it's going to be little bit more hard to work with the tables.
16:37:4716 hours, 37 minutes, 47 secondsYou have to be a developer or a data engineer. But working with tables is way easier than working with the complexity of the database at the physical level.
16:37:5716 hours, 37 minutes, 57 secondsSo you don't have to be a database expert or administrator to work with tables. So the difficulty here is like in the middle. The abstraction is not
16:38:0516 hours, 38 minutes, 5 secondsthat low but as well not that high. So now let's answer the question, what are tables? A database table is a structured collection of data. It's like a simple
16:38:1416 hours, 38 minutes, 14 secondsgrid or spreadsheet that you might find in Excel. So it has different columns like each column represent a field like the ID, name, country and the table has
16:38:2316 hours, 38 minutes, 23 secondsas well multiple rows and each row represent a record or an entry of the data. So for example if this table is
16:38:3016 hours, 38 minutes, 30 secondsabout the employees then each record each row is one employee. Now the intersect between the rows and columns
16:38:3816 hours, 38 minutes, 38 secondswe call it a cell and a cell is a single piece of data. Now the whole table going to be stored physically in the database
16:38:4516 hours, 38 minutes, 45 secondsas database files. So they are in the database like multiple files that are holding the informations about the table
16:38:5316 hours, 38 minutes, 53 secondsand those files are stored physically in that disk storage of the database. So that means your data inside the tables
16:39:0016 hours, 39 minutesare not stored like a spreadsheet like an Excel but they are stored in special database files that usual developers and
16:39:0816 hours, 39 minutes, 8 secondsend users don't have access to those files. So tables again it's like an abstraction and representation for the actual data that are in the files. So
16:39:1616 hours, 39 minutes, 16 secondsactually each time you are querying the database table the database has to go to those files and fetch the data for you.
16:39:2216 hours, 39 minutes, 22 secondsAll right. So this is what we mean with database tables. Okay. So now we have like
16:39:3116 hours, 39 minutes, 31 secondsdifferent types of tables in SQL. We have tables that stays forever. We call it permanent tables. So they stay as
16:39:3816 hours, 39 minutes, 38 secondslong as you don't drop them. And you have another type of tables they called the temporary tables. And those tables going to be deleted and dropped once the
16:39:4716 hours, 39 minutes, 47 secondssession ends. So now we're going to focus first on the first type, the permanent tables. And there are two ways on how to create them. The first way is
16:39:5516 hours, 39 minutes, 55 secondsthe classical way where you create table from the scratch and then you go and insert your data. So we call it create
16:40:0116 hours, 40 minutes, 1 secondinsert and the other way called create table as select. It's going to create as well the table but based on SQL query.
16:40:1016 hours, 40 minutes, 10 secondsSo let's understand the differences between them. The create insert method is the
16:40:1816 hours, 40 minutes, 18 secondsclassical way on how we define and create tables in SQL where first we have to go and create the table and define the structure and after that we insert
16:40:2716 hours, 40 minutes, 27 secondsour data into the database table where the other method the CTAs create table as select. And this one going to create
16:40:3416 hours, 40 minutes, 34 secondsa new table as well but this time based on the result of SQL query. So let's understand what this means. Okay. So now
16:40:4216 hours, 40 minutes, 42 secondsto the first method create insert. So here we have two steps. The first step is we have a DDL statements where we use
16:40:4916 hours, 40 minutes, 49 secondsthe command create. So once we execute the first step what's going to happen the database engine going to go and create for us an empty table. It is a
16:40:5716 hours, 40 minutes, 57 secondsbrand new table where we can hold our data. So with that we have defined the structure of our table but it's still an empty table. So now in the next step we
16:41:0616 hours, 41 minutes, 6 secondshave to go and insert our data inside this new table. So our data can come from multiple sources like a CSV file or
16:41:1316 hours, 41 minutes, 13 secondsmaybe completely from another database where we are doing migration or maybe you are inserting manually your data or maybe it come from an application or you
16:41:2116 hours, 41 minutes, 21 secondsare doing data migration from one database to another. So at the end once you execute insert what's going to happen your data going to be inserted in
16:41:3016 hours, 41 minutes, 30 secondsthis new table. So in this method we have like two steps. First we define the structure of the table and the second
16:41:3716 hours, 41 minutes, 37 secondsstep we take care of inserting our data inside the table. And now this new table and your data going to be persisted permanently. Now let's check the other
16:41:4616 hours, 41 minutes, 46 secondsmethod the CTIS. Here it's only one step where you define a query and once you execute this query what going to happen
16:41:5316 hours, 41 minutes, 53 secondsthe database has to retrieve the data from another table. So it might retrieve data from our new table that we just created using create insert. So once the query is executed we will get a result.
16:42:0516 hours, 42 minutes, 5 secondsSo now what the database going to do going to create a new brand table but this time the definition and the data of
16:42:1216 hours, 42 minutes, 12 secondsthis new table it doesn't come from any definition that we specify. it comes from the result of the query. So
16:42:2016 hours, 42 minutes, 20 secondswhatever structure that we have in the results, it going to be reflected in our new table. So again the definition and
16:42:2716 hours, 42 minutes, 27 secondsthe data that we see in this new table comes one one to one from the result of our query. So in this type we don't have
16:42:3416 hours, 42 minutes, 34 secondsto define anything or to insert any data. We are just writing a query and the output of this query going to define the table. But in this method as you can
16:42:4316 hours, 42 minutes, 43 secondssee it always needs a database table in order to execute the query. But the create insert method we are creating
16:42:5016 hours, 42 minutes, 50 secondssomething from the scratch. So these are the two different ways on how you create tables in SQL and the differences between
16:43:0016 hours, 43 minutesthem. Okay. So now you might ask you know what the CTAs are very similar to the views. We have a query and the output of this query going to be like an
16:43:0816 hours, 43 minutes, 8 secondsobject in the database. So what are the differences between them? Let's check this. Now let's say that in our database we have a table that has three columns
16:43:1716 hours, 43 minutes, 17 secondsA, B, C. And now what we can do, we can go and create view based on a query. So you create the DDL statement in order to create the view in the database. And of
16:43:2516 hours, 43 minutes, 25 secondscourse the database going to go and store the query in the database and it's going to be empty. So there will be no data because views does not store any
16:43:3316 hours, 43 minutes, 33 secondsdata and the query of the view will not be yet executed. But now in the other hand if you go and create a table using
16:43:3916 hours, 43 minutes, 39 secondsCTIS. So here again we have a query attached to the object to the table. So here what happens the database has to
16:43:4616 hours, 43 minutes, 46 secondsexecute the query in order to understand the structure and as well the data that should be inserted inside the table. So
16:43:5416 hours, 43 minutes, 54 secondsour SQL query going to be executed and the result of the query going to be inserted inside the table. So that means
16:44:0116 hours, 44 minutes, 1 secondthis new table is storing already the result of the query. So now this is the first differences between the table and view. As you create view the query will
16:44:1016 hours, 44 minutes, 10 secondsnot be executed and we don't have anything about the result of the query where in the CTIS we have already result
16:44:1816 hours, 44 minutes, 18 secondsof the query stored inside the table and everything is prepared. So now let's see what's going to happen once the user selects something from the view. So now
16:44:2616 hours, 44 minutes, 26 secondsthe database going to go for the first time executing the query of the view in order to fetch the data from the
16:44:3316 hours, 44 minutes, 33 secondsoriginal table and then presented as a result for the user. But now in the other hand if the user go and query the table that is created from the CTIS. So
16:44:4216 hours, 44 minutes, 42 secondsnow what can happen? SQL will not execute again the query of the CTIS because the database already done that
16:44:4916 hours, 44 minutes, 49 secondsand prepared everything. So that means we are not querying anything from the original table and the data can be directly fetched from the new table. So
16:44:5816 hours, 44 minutes, 58 secondsthe user is going to get immediately the result from our table that is created from the CDIS. So here comes the second difference between the tables and views.
16:45:0616 hours, 45 minutes, 6 secondsThe views are slower than CTIS and that's because the database has here an extra task. It must execute the query of
16:45:1416 hours, 45 minutes, 14 secondsthe view in order to get the data. But in the CTIS the query going to be faster than the view because we have already executed everything and prepared it for
16:45:2316 hours, 45 minutes, 23 secondsthe user. So that's why tables from CTIS are way faster than views. And now there is another difference and perspective
16:45:3016 hours, 45 minutes, 30 secondsabout this which is from my point of view is more important than the performance. So now let's say that in the next day we are doing data updates
16:45:3816 hours, 45 minutes, 38 secondson the original table like we are doing updates on the column C and as well in the column P. So now let's see what this means for the user if they are using
16:45:4616 hours, 45 minutes, 46 secondsviews. So the user in the next day is executing again the same query and again here the database has to execute the query of the view in order to fetch the
16:45:5516 hours, 45 minutes, 55 secondsdata from the original table. So that means today in the views we are getting different data than yesterday because we
16:46:0216 hours, 46 minutes, 2 secondshave a new data and new updates and the user in the result going to see as well the new updates and the fresh data. So
16:46:0816 hours, 46 minutes, 8 secondsthe user is seeing exactly the status of the data in the original tables. But now let's see what going to happen if the
16:46:1516 hours, 46 minutes, 15 secondsuser go and query the table from the CATS. So in the table of the CATS, we are still having the data from
16:46:2316 hours, 46 minutes, 23 secondsyesterday. All those new updates from the original data will not be reflected in this new table because once the user selects something from this table, the
16:46:3116 hours, 46 minutes, 31 secondsdatabase will not go and query or fetch the new changes from the original table because we have already prepared the data from yesterday. So that means our
16:46:4016 hours, 46 minutes, 40 secondsuser now is getting old data from the CTAs table and the only way to get new
16:46:4616 hours, 46 minutes, 46 secondsfresh data from the CTIS is to reexecute the CTIS query. And of course this is another step and it is harder to
16:46:5516 hours, 46 minutes, 55 secondsmaintain the table from the CTAs and this is a big difference for the users between the views and the tables from the CTAs. Now think about views you are
16:47:0416 hours, 47 minutes, 4 secondsordering a pizza at restaurants. So every time you are quering the view you are placing an order the chef going to go and make a pizza from the scratch
16:47:1316 hours, 47 minutes, 13 secondsusing the freshest ingredients. So that means you are always getting a fresh hot pizza. And think about the CTS as like a
16:47:2116 hours, 47 minutes, 21 secondsfrozen pizza from a grocery store. The pizza was prepared earlier and stored in the freezer. And if you want to eat it,
16:47:2716 hours, 47 minutes, 27 secondsyou have to go and heat it up in the oven. But it's still not like a fresh pizza that is made on the spot and from the scratch. Now I made myself hungry
16:47:3516 hours, 47 minutes, 35 secondsbecause I love pizza. So I think I'm going to go for a quick break.

16:50:3016 hours, 50 minutes, 30 seconds[Music]
16:50:3316 hours, 50 minutes, 33 secondsdatabases. Okay. So, now we're going to check the scenarios and use cases where it makes sense to use. So, let's start with the first one. Now we have learned
16:50:4216 hours, 50 minutes, 42 secondsbefore it makes sense to have a complex logic stored inside the database so that our end users don't have to keep
16:50:4916 hours, 50 minutes, 49 secondsrepeating the same logic over and over and it's as well maybe complicated for some users. So that's why we have used views and the result of the view going
16:50:5716 hours, 50 minutes, 57 secondsto be used from our users. So everything can stay easy and friendly to consume for our users. But now what might happen
16:51:0416 hours, 51 minutes, 4 secondsis that the logic of the view could be very complicated and needs a lot of time to be executed from the database. So it
16:51:1216 hours, 51 minutes, 12 secondstakes really long time until we get the intermediate result from the database.
16:51:1616 hours, 51 minutes, 16 secondsSo that means if it's going to takes 30 minutes then each users has to wait 30 minutes until the query is executed and
16:51:2316 hours, 51 minutes, 23 secondsnone of your users going to be happy with this situation. In this scenario,
16:51:2716 hours, 51 minutes, 27 secondsif this happens, you have to try maybe to optimize the query. But if you cannot do anything about that, you have to
16:51:3416 hours, 51 minutes, 34 secondsswitch the view to CTAs table. So now what you have to do, you have to take the same logic and then put it
16:51:4116 hours, 51 minutes, 41 secondsin so that the intermediate results are stored in a table. And of course at the moment of creating the table, it will
16:51:4816 hours, 51 minutes, 48 secondstake 30 minutes. It will take long time because it is the same query and the database going to need the time until creating the intermittent results. But
16:51:5616 hours, 51 minutes, 56 secondsthe big advantage is that once everything is prepared maybe at the night at the morning once your users are
16:52:0316 hours, 52 minutes, 3 secondslike online and start querying the data they have everything prepared. So the user is going to go and start selecting
16:52:1016 hours, 52 minutes, 10 secondsand analyzing the intermediate result but this time using the table that you have created from the CTAs and the
16:52:1716 hours, 52 minutes, 17 secondsresponse time going to be for all users again normal and fast. So if you have a scenario where your views are very slow you have to go and prepare the data at
16:52:2516 hours, 52 minutes, 25 secondsthe night using the CTIS and prepare the tables to be analyzed from the end users. So this is the most common use
16:52:3216 hours, 52 minutes, 32 secondscase for the CTIS and this scenario happens a lot in projects where you decide to go instead of views to go with
16:52:4016 hours, 52 minutes, 40 secondsthe CTIS in order to have persistence data and you gain performance. Okay, so finally back to SQL let's go and create
16:52:4716 hours, 52 minutes, 47 secondsa table using now we're going to go and create a table that shows the total number of orders for each month. Let's go and do it. So first what do we need?
16:52:5516 hours, 52 minutes, 55 secondsWe need a query. So let's write it.
16:52:5816 hours, 52 minutes, 58 secondsselect. I'm going to go with the date name in order to get the name of the month from our order dates and we're
16:53:0616 hours, 53 minutes, 6 secondsgoing to call it order month. And then we're going to go and aggregate the data by counting the order ID for total

16:58:1316 hours, 58 minutes, 13 secondsSQL. All right, moving on to another common use case for the CTAs that I usually use as well in my projects. We use CDS in order to create a persistent
16:58:2216 hours, 58 minutes, 22 secondssnapshot of the data at specific time in order to analyze data quality issue. So let's understand what this means. Now in
16:58:2916 hours, 58 minutes, 29 secondssome scenarios you have like a table and you are analyzing an issue. So there is like a data quality issue at your data and you are analyzing this scenario in
16:58:3716 hours, 58 minutes, 37 secondsorder to understand why it happens. But the problem is that at the same time there will be updates on the table and your data is changing. So there will be
16:58:4616 hours, 58 minutes, 46 secondsupdates maybe on some fields or you are getting new records and everything is getting mixed up and you will not be able to analyze the scenario where the
16:58:5416 hours, 58 minutes, 54 secondsdata quality issue happened. So now it's almost impossible to find the ro cause of your issue. But instead of that what
16:59:0216 hours, 59 minutes, 2 secondswe do if we have like an issue of the data we go and create a fixed persisted snapshot of the data in a separate table
16:59:0916 hours, 59 minutes, 9 secondsusing CTS so that we make sure nothing is changing and everything is fixed. And with that I can keep doing my analysis
16:59:1616 hours, 59 minutes, 16 secondson the same data without the worry that data are getting changed. So this is another way why we use CTS in projects
16:59:2316 hours, 59 minutes, 23 secondsto make sure that we have snapshot of the data to ensure that our analyzes are done on the same scenario that caused
16:59:3016 hours, 59 minutes, 30 secondsthe buck and going to be used as a foundation for finding the problem and fixing
16:59:3916 hours, 59 minutes, 39 secondsit. All right, moving on to another use case of the CTAs. We can use it in order to create our data m to make it physical
16:59:4716 hours, 59 minutes, 47 secondsdata m instead of virtual data ms using views. So let's understand what this means now. As we learned before, if you have a data warehouse system, our data
16:59:5616 hours, 59 minutes, 56 secondswarehouse layer going to store the data inside tables. But for the second layer,
17:00:0017 hoursthe data m, we can go and use views in order to have dynamic and flexibility in order to generate multiple data ms. And
17:00:0717 hours, 7 secondswe called it the virtual layer. But now in some scenarios if things get complicated your data m and reports
17:00:1417 hours, 14 secondsgoing to be slow because there for each action you are generating a query. So the powerbi reports and dashboards are creating queries in your data marts and
17:00:2317 hours, 23 secondsyour data marts have always to go to the data warehouse in order to retrieve the data for the reports and the whole thing could take minutes or maybe sometimes
17:00:3217 hours, 32 secondshours. So in these scenarios we cannot stay using views because they are slowing everything down. But instead of that we have to convert our data mart to
17:00:4117 hours, 41 secondsa physical layer. That means instead of using views we have to go and use tables. And one very common way in order to generate the tables of the data marts
17:00:4917 hours, 49 secondson daily basis is to use queries between the data warehouse layer and the data mart layer. It's still going to take maybe 30 minutes. That's why you can go
17:00:5817 hours, 58 secondsand prepare the data at the night. But at the reporting layer where things and the performance really matters, the performance going to be better because
17:01:0517 hours, 1 minute, 5 secondsthe response time from the tables is way faster than views and the reports don't have always to waste time waiting for the data marts to get data from the
17:01:1417 hours, 1 minute, 14 secondswarehouse. So this is another use case where you use CTAs where the views at the data marts are slow and we have to go and replace them with stables using
17:01:2217 hours, 1 minute, 22 secondsCTAs to speed up things. But still my recommendation here is that start first with the views. So create a virtual data
17:01:2917 hours, 1 minute, 29 secondsmart using views because the implementation going to be very dynamic and fast and you are always getting fresh data from the warehouse but maybe
17:01:3717 hours, 1 minute, 37 secondslater if you notice okay some data ms and models are complex then maybe go and replace few marts from views to tables
17:01:4517 hours, 1 minute, 45 secondsusing cis. So this is another use case for the and it is nice workaround for your data warehouse system. All right friends, so with that we have covered
17:01:5317 hours, 1 minute, 53 secondsnow the first type of the tables that we have in databases. The permanent tables where you create a table and it's going to live forever until you go and drop
17:02:0117 hours, 2 minutes, 1 secondit. Now we're going to talk about another type of tables in databases. We have the temporary tables. So let's understand what are temporary
17:02:1217 hours, 2 minutes, 12 secondstables. So temporary tables or sometimes you call them as a shortcut temp tables.
17:02:1817 hours, 2 minutes, 18 secondsThey store intermediate results in a temporary storage in the database during a session and the database automatically
17:02:2517 hours, 2 minutes, 25 secondsdrop these tables after the session ends. So let's understand what this means. Now we have learned in the CIS we
17:02:3217 hours, 2 minutes, 32 secondscould use a query in order to retrieve data from one table and then it puts the intermediate results in brand new table
17:02:4017 hours, 2 minutes, 40 secondsin the database. So with that we are creating another table based on a query. The same thing for the temporary tables.
17:02:4717 hours, 2 minutes, 47 secondsWe have as well a query that goes and retrieves the data from a table and as well the database going to go and create new brand table in the database that has
17:02:5517 hours, 2 minutes, 55 secondsthe structure and the data from the result of the query. So it is exactly at the CTIS. What is the difference here?
17:03:0217 hours, 3 minutes, 2 secondsWell, it is about the lifetime of the table. Now the database tables that you have created using create insert or CTIS
17:03:1017 hours, 3 minutes, 10 secondsthose tables going to stay permanent and they're going to live in the database as long as you don't drop them. So even if
17:03:1717 hours, 3 minutes, 17 secondsthe system is completely offline the data going to stay at the database once it is online again but the temporary tables going to get deleted and dropped
17:03:2617 hours, 3 minutes, 26 secondsfrom the database automatically once the session ends. So what session means like once you open the client and you connect
17:03:3417 hours, 3 minutes, 34 secondsto the database and you are start doing queries we call the time between connecting ourself to the database and disconnecting from the database we call
17:03:4317 hours, 3 minutes, 43 secondsthis a session. So that means once you close the client and you disconnect from the database and maybe shut down your PC and do something else. What going to happen? The database going to go and
17:03:5117 hours, 3 minutes, 51 secondsdestroy and delete all the temporary tables that you have created during the session. So that mean the table going to
17:03:5817 hours, 3 minutes, 58 secondslive as long as you have a session and you can access during this time the table as you are accessing any other permanent table. So this is what we mean
17:04:0617 hours, 4 minutes, 6 secondswith temporary tables or sometimes we call it as a shortcut temp
17:04:1017 hours, 4 minutes, 10 seconds[Music]
17:04:1317 hours, 4 minutes, 13 secondstables. Okay. So now let's check the easiest syntax ever. So for the temporary table the syntax going to look like this. you're going to have like a
