17:10:4617 hours, 10 minutes, 46 secondsit is still living here and still has like the data that we have created. So this is how things works with the temporary tables in
17:10:5617 hours, 10 minutes, 56 secondsSQL. Now let's see how the database server executed that temporary SQL. So now let's say that you are as a data
17:11:0417 hours, 11 minutes, 4 secondsanalyst. You have created a query and then you say into in a temporary table.
17:11:0717 hours, 11 minutes, 7 secondsNow the database engine going to identify the query and first it's going to go and execute the query and then it's going to go and execute it and maybe we're going to get the data from
17:11:1617 hours, 11 minutes, 16 secondsthe table orders and after the query is executed the database engine now has the results. Now two things can happen.
17:11:2317 hours, 11 minutes, 23 secondsFirst the database engine going to go and store the metadata informations in the system catalog. And now the second thing the database engine going to
17:11:3117 hours, 11 minutes, 31 secondscreate a table but this time not in the users but in the temporary storage in the disk. So the table going to live
17:11:3817 hours, 11 minutes, 38 secondsthere for a short time. And now what you can do you can write multiple SQL queries that are doing maybe multiple analysis on top of this table. So each
17:11:4617 hours, 11 minutes, 46 secondstime you select something the database engine has to go to the temporary storage and fetch the data from there.
17:11:5317 hours, 11 minutes, 53 secondsAnd now once you are finished and let's say you close your client the session between you and the database going to ends and now the database going to
17:12:0017 hours, 12 minutesunderstand okay there is no more connection to this user and it going to go and clean up now the temporary
17:12:0717 hours, 12 minutes, 7 secondsstorage with any tables that are created from this session. So that means the database is automatically cleaning up
17:12:1417 hours, 12 minutes, 14 secondsthe storage maybe for other sessions. So this is how the database engine works with the temporary tables.
17:12:2417 hours, 12 minutes, 24 secondsSo now the question is why do we need temporary tables? Let's see the following scenario. Now let's say that in our source database we have a table
17:12:3117 hours, 12 minutes, 31 secondscalled orders and now we would like to go and load the table in our data warehouse. We have to do several transformations in order to prepare the data for the analyzes in the data
17:12:4017 hours, 12 minutes, 40 secondswarehouse. So maybe you have one query to remove the duplicates and another one to handle the nulls and maybe you are doing filtering and cleaning up and the
17:12:4817 hours, 12 minutes, 48 secondslast step you would like to aggregate the data. And now of course those queries those transformations want to change the content of the table orders
17:12:5617 hours, 12 minutes, 56 secondsand there is no scenario where you can do that directly on the source database and of course this is not allowed.
17:13:0217 hours, 13 minutes, 2 secondsThat's why in data warehousing we have to go and get our own copy of the data and then on top of this data we can do our transformations. Now one way to do
17:13:1017 hours, 13 minutes, 10 secondsthis using the temporary tables. So you have one script in order to extract the data from the table orders and put it in
17:13:1717 hours, 13 minutes, 17 secondstemporary table as an intermediate results and then you come with the transformations and all those queries and they start manipulating and changing
17:13:2517 hours, 13 minutes, 25 secondsthe data of this extra copy in the temporary table and the last step you have the load where you go and load the final version of the intermediate
17:13:3417 hours, 13 minutes, 34 secondsresults in the database. This is if you would like to do the whole ETL before inserting the data to the database. So now the orders table and the final table
17:13:4317 hours, 13 minutes, 43 secondsin the data warehouse both of them are tables. So they are permanent tables and they will stay there as long as we don't drop them. So they are very important
17:13:5217 hours, 13 minutes, 52 secondstables. But now for the intermediate results it is not that important. It is just an intermediate step that we have done in order to have our extra copy of
17:14:0117 hours, 14 minutes, 1 secondthe data to manipulate it and so on in order to prepare it to be inserted in the data warehouse. So after we loaded
17:14:0817 hours, 14 minutes, 8 secondsit in the data warehouse, this copy of the data is not anymore important. It shouldn't stay like for a long time.
17:14:1417 hours, 14 minutes, 14 secondsThat's why in this scenario, maybe we can go and use the temporary tables instead of normal tables for the intermediate results. And that's because
17:14:2217 hours, 14 minutes, 22 secondsonly of one advantage is that the database going to go and do an automatic clean up after the host session ends. So
17:14:3017 hours, 14 minutes, 30 secondsit comes out of the box automatically from the database. So that means I don't have to deal with the dropping mechanism of this table for the next load. If
17:14:3817 hours, 14 minutes, 38 secondsthere is like something wrong in the data warehouse, you would like always to check the copy where the transformations are done in order to debug and find
17:14:4617 hours, 14 minutes, 46 secondsissues. So I don't normally use temporary tables in these scenarios, I use just normal tables. But for other small projects, maybe this makes sense.
17:14:5317 hours, 14 minutes, 53 secondsSo this is one use case on when to use the temporary tables in your projects.
17:14:5817 hours, 14 minutes, 58 secondsWe use it in order to store intermediate results temporary until we are done with the session and then once we are done the database can go and drop that
17:15:0717 hours, 15 minutes, 7 secondstemporary table. All right guys, now a quick talk about the temporary tables. To be honest, I never use this in my projects.
17:15:1717 hours, 15 minutes, 17 secondsIf I need an intermediate results in one query, I can go and use the CTEs. And if my intermediate results is very
17:15:2517 hours, 15 minutes, 25 secondsimportant then I put it in either view or CTIS but it is nice technique to learn maybe you can utilize it in one of
17:15:3217 hours, 15 minutes, 32 secondsyour projects. All right guys so now let's have a quick summary about tables.
17:15:3917 hours, 15 minutes, 39 secondsTables in database are like spreadsheet or grid that contains columns and rows and your actual data are stored in these
17:15:4717 hours, 15 minutes, 47 secondstables. And we have learned there are two types of tables. We have permanent tables and temporary tables. Permanent tables lives in the database forever as
17:15:5517 hours, 15 minutes, 55 secondslong as you don't drop them. But in the other hand that temporary tables they have short lifetime. They will be dropped from the database once you end
17:16:0317 hours, 16 minutes, 3 secondsthe session. Now we have learned as well there are two methods on how to create tables in databases. The first method is
17:16:1017 hours, 16 minutes, 10 secondscreate insert. This method involves two steps. The first one is defining and creating the table and the second step
17:16:1717 hours, 16 minutes, 17 secondsis by inserting the data inside this new table. So you are creating something from the scratch. And the second method we call it CTAs. It create as well brand
17:16:2717 hours, 16 minutes, 27 secondsnew table but based on the result of a query. So this type is done with only one step but it always needs another
17:16:3517 hours, 16 minutes, 35 secondsexisting table. And we have learned as well the difference between tables and views where the main advantage of using tables created from CTIS is that to
17:16:4317 hours, 16 minutes, 43 secondsensure the performance is fast enough at the end of the users or your reporting system. So we use CIS instead of views
17:16:5217 hours, 16 minutes, 52 secondsif the logic of the view is very complex and takes a lot of time to be executed in the database. And one more nice use case for the CIS is that we can go and
17:17:0117 hours, 17 minutes, 1 secondpersist a snapshot of the data in order to analyze a bug and data quality issue and to ensure that we have the exact data in order to find a solution for the
17:17:1017 hours, 17 minutes, 10 secondsbug and the issue. Now we have learned as well that we can use temporary tables in order to store intermediate results in a temporary storage and the main
17:17:1817 hours, 17 minutes, 18 secondsadvantage of the temporary table is the database automatically drops all that temporary tables when the session ends
17:17:2617 hours, 17 minutes, 26 secondsand that's because for you the intermediate results are not that important to live long
17:17:3417 hours, 17 minutes, 34 secondstime. Hey my friends. So we have learned that in real data projects if you have a database there will be a lot of
17:17:4217 hours, 17 minutes, 42 secondsanalytical use cases that want to access your data and do analytics. And what going to happen? They're going to write complex queries because in many
17:17:5017 hours, 17 minutes, 50 secondsscenarios they are doing complex analyzes. And if you don't do anything about it in your projects, you're going to face a lot of challenges like
17:17:5717 hours, 17 minutes, 57 secondscomplexity and a lot of redundancy of the same complex logic but from multiple users and maybe performance and security
17:18:0517 hours, 18 minutes, 5 secondsissues. And we have learned we have five amazing techniques in order to solve those problems. We have learned the subqueries and cities and as well how to
17:18:1417 hours, 18 minutes, 14 secondscreate objects like views, CTAs and temporary tables. So now what we're going to do, we're going to go and compare them side by side in order to
17:18:2217 hours, 18 minutes, 22 secondshave a big picture about the advantages and the disadvantages of each method. So let's go and compare them. Okay. So now we have our five methods and the first
17:18:3117 hours, 18 minutes, 31 secondscriteria that I would like to compare them is the storage type. We have learned that if you are using subqueries and CTE, what can happen? and the
17:18:3917 hours, 18 minutes, 39 secondsdatabase going to put the result of those two techniques in the memory in the cache so that later the main query has a fast access to those intermediate
17:18:4817 hours, 18 minutes, 48 secondsresults. But in the other hand if you are using temporary tables or tables from CDS the new created table can be stored inside the disk storage. And now
17:18:5717 hours, 18 minutes, 57 secondsfor the views as we understood there will be no data storage and that means we are not using any storage from the database. Now if you are talking about
17:19:0617 hours, 19 minutes, 6 secondsthe lifetime so that means how long the object going to live or persist in the database. Now our three techniques sub
17:19:1217 hours, 19 minutes, 12 secondsqueries CTE and temporary tables all of them going to live a short time in the database. So all of them are temporary.
17:19:2017 hours, 19 minutes, 20 secondsBut now if you are talking about creating objects using CIS and views those two going to be permanent. So that means they're going to live in the database as long as you don't drop them.
17:19:3017 hours, 19 minutes, 30 secondsNow we're going to compare them with something similar is when the database going to go and drop or delete those objects. Now we have learned that the
17:19:3817 hours, 19 minutes, 38 secondssubqueries and the cities have a short time. They going to live only during the execution of the query. So once the
17:19:4517 hours, 19 minutes, 45 secondsquery ends the database going to go to the cache and delete everything. But for the temporary tables they live little bit longer as long as you are in the session. But once you end the session,
17:19:5617 hours, 19 minutes, 56 secondsthe database as well going to go and drop and delete your table. Now for the objects that comes from the CIS and views as we learned they are persistent
17:20:0517 hours, 20 minutes, 5 secondsand permanent and the database can only delete them if you ask the database to do that by using the DDL command drop.
17:20:1217 hours, 20 minutes, 12 secondsSo the database will not delete anything for these two. So now the next one is the query scope like how we can access those objects. Now for the subquery and
17:20:2117 hours, 20 minutes, 21 secondsthe CTE the scope is here very small. It is accessed only from one single query.
17:20:2617 hours, 20 minutes, 26 secondsThe query itself where you write the city and subquery. So you cannot access it from external queries. But we have learned that the temporary tables cis
17:20:3517 hours, 20 minutes, 35 secondsand views you can access all those objects from multiple queries. So that means you can access those objects from
17:20:4317 hours, 20 minutes, 43 secondsmultiple external queries. Now the next one if you are thinking about the reusability if you look to the subqueries they are very limited. the
17:20:5117 hours, 20 minutes, 51 secondssubquery going to be used only in one query and only in one place. So if you need it in multiple places, you have to go and repeat the same logic. So
17:21:0017 hours, 21 minutessubqueries are the worst with their reusability. But now if you are talking about the CTE, it is little bit better.
17:21:0617 hours, 21 minutes, 6 secondsYou still can access it only from one single query but you can access it in the same query from multiple places. So you can access it multiple times from
17:21:1517 hours, 21 minutes, 15 secondsdifferent joins and you don't have to repeat the same logics over and over.
17:21:1917 hours, 21 minutes, 19 secondsBut still it is limited because you have only one query that is using the logic.
17:21:2417 hours, 21 minutes, 24 secondsNow if you think about the temporary tables I could say the reusability here is medium and that's because you can
17:21:3017 hours, 21 minutes, 30 secondsaccess the data by multiple queries but only during this session. So once the session is ended you cannot access it
17:21:3817 hours, 21 minutes, 38 secondsanymore which means you have to recreate it in order to reuse it again. So it is more reusable than the city and the subqueries but not that good like the
17:21:4617 hours, 21 minutes, 46 secondsCTAs and views. Those techniques can offer the highest reusability for you.
17:21:5217 hours, 21 minutes, 52 secondsSo they are always there for multiple users from multiple queries. So it can eliminate a lot of redundancies and you
17:21:5917 hours, 21 minutes, 59 secondshave to do the job only once. Now moving into the next one. If you are thinking about the intermediate result of those techniques, the question is how fresh is
17:22:0717 hours, 22 minutes, 7 secondsthe data? Is the data from these objects always up to date? Now for the subqueries and the cities they are always up to date because the SQL is
17:22:1617 hours, 22 minutes, 16 secondsexecuting the logic on the fly and storing the data in the memory and immediately after that going to come the main query and get the data. So always
17:22:2417 hours, 22 minutes, 24 secondsthe intermediate results in the memory are up to date. But now if you think about that temporary tables and the CTIS the query is only executed once and if
17:22:3317 hours, 22 minutes, 33 secondsthere is like any update and changes on the original table you will not find those changes in those objects and
17:22:4017 hours, 22 minutes, 40 secondsthat's because SQL executed once and that's all. So if you query those tables there is no guarantee that the data are up to date. So if you want fresh data
17:22:4917 hours, 22 minutes, 49 secondsyou have always to drop the table and create it again from the query. Now if you are talking about the views they are amazing they are always up to date because views does not store any data.
17:22:5917 hours, 22 minutes, 59 secondsSo each time you ask the views for data what's going to happen the database going to go to the original table and fetch the data to the view. So your data
17:23:0717 hours, 23 minutes, 7 secondsare always fresh and up to date. So this is a big picture about the behavior of those advanced techniques that you can use in SQL projects. And if you ask my
17:23:1617 hours, 23 minutes, 16 secondsopinion my favorite is going to be the views in the first place. Then in the second in my list is the city. They are amazing, but don't use more than five
17:23:2417 hours, 23 minutes, 24 secondsCTEs in one query. Otherwise, it's going to be really annoying and hard to read.
17:23:2817 hours, 23 minutes, 28 secondsAnd then I'm going to say in the third place, the sub queries. And then the CDIS. I use CIS if the views are slow.
17:23:3517 hours, 23 minutes, 35 secondsIf that's a scenario, I'm jump to the CDIS and create a permanent physical tables from my query. And the last one that I rarely use is the temporary
17:23:4317 hours, 23 minutes, 43 secondstables. So, this is how I rank those techniques in my skill projects.

17:29:1917 hours, 29 minutes, 19 secondsAnd now once you do that all your SQL statements will not stay at the client side they will be stored now in the
17:29:2617 hours, 29 minutes, 26 secondsserver side of the database. So that means in store procedures we are storing our SQL statements inside the database.

17:29:5217 hours, 29 minutes, 52 secondsis stored inside the server. And once you execute this what can happen the database going to go to the stored procedure and start executing all the
17:30:0017 hours, 30 minutesSQL statements that you have inside the store procedure and it's going to do it exactly in the order that you have defined. So from top to bottom. So now
17:30:0917 hours, 30 minutes, 9 secondsonce the database went through all your SQL statements, it's going to return back to the user the data that we have from the selects. And with that things
17:30:1717 hours, 30 minutes, 17 secondsare really easy and you can tell your colleagues okay just execute this third procedure and the rest can be done from the database. So with that you minimize
17:30:2617 hours, 30 minutes, 26 secondsthe human errors and you make sure that everything can be executed as you wish and as well as you are back from your vacation things are easier. You have to just go and execute the third procedure.
17:30:3617 hours, 30 minutes, 36 secondsSo this is what we mean with start procedure. You can store inside it multiple SQL statements in specific order and you can save it inside the
17:30:4417 hours, 30 minutes, 44 secondsdatabase and each time you need your SQL statements you can go and simply execute them. So now let's have a quick

17:58:4817 hours, 58 minutes, 48 secondsdata? We're going to go and make an update on our table where we say if there is like a null then make it as a zero. And we will do this as a pre-step

17:59:0517 hours, 59 minutes, 5 secondswe usually do inside SQL projects. So the logic going to be very simple. We have to check first do we have nulls inside the score. If the answer is yes

17:59:1817 hours, 59 minutes, 18 secondswe don't have any values then we can skip everything. So now we're going to go and build this logic inside our store procedure in order to clean up and
17:59:2617 hours, 59 minutes, 26 secondsprepare the data. So let's go. Okay. So now this part we're going to call it generating

18:00:4818 hours, 48 secondshave any nulls in their scores. So if this query returns something we have nulls. If it didn't return anything then there is no nulls. And we're going to use exactly this query as a condition.

18:02:4218 hours, 2 minutes, 42 secondschecking our condition and then we execute if the condition is met where we update the table with zero instead of null and if the condition is not met
18:02:5018 hours, 2 minutes, 50 secondsthen don't do anything. Just print a message. Now you might say you know what why you are doing this? we just can use this update statements and we don't need
18:02:5718 hours, 2 minutes, 57 secondsthe whole if else statements. So why we are checking in the first place? I can like each time I run this store procedure I go and update all the nulls
18:03:0518 hours, 3 minutes, 5 secondsif they exist to a zero. Well, this is not really professional because you are wasting resources. So each time you run an update statement like this. So
18:03:1418 hours, 3 minutes, 14 secondsimagine that you have a big table and each time you run your store procedure,
18:03:1718 hours, 3 minutes, 17 secondsSQL have to go and check whether there is any nulls and so on. And this is of course consume resources. It's way better if you go and check first whether
18:03:2618 hours, 3 minutes, 26 secondsit's really needed. So that's why we are doing this logic. Now as you can see our store procedure is getting bigger and bigger. So we have like two parts. The
18:03:3418 hours, 3 minutes, 34 secondsfirst part is preparing and cleaning up the data. And the second part we are generating reports. Let's go and update
18:03:4118 hours, 3 minutes, 41 secondsthe whole thing and execute it. And now we have to do it step by step. So let's check our query over here. And you can see we have here null for USA customers.

18:04:3018 hours, 4 minutes, 30 secondscreated a control flow using the FL statements. And as you can see we are not doing any more like simple queries.
18:04:3618 hours, 4 minutes, 36 secondsWe are creating like a mini program. And now it's like an ETL where first we prepare the data and second we generate

18:04:5418 hours, 4 minutes, 54 secondsdo. Okay. So now we're going to talk about the error handling in store procedure. Error handling it is like essential things to do while programming
18:05:0218 hours, 5 minutes, 2 secondsbecause it gives you the control on what can happen once you have an error. And there's a lot of things that you can do like maybe deleting data, printing a
18:05:1118 hours, 5 minutes, 11 secondsvery structured like message or maybe doing some logging and so on. So you have a full control on what to do if there is an error and of course we can
18:05:1918 hours, 5 minutes, 19 secondsdo that in the store procedure. So now let's check the quickly the syntax. It is usually has two parts. The first part is the try part. So the syntax is like
18:05:2818 hours, 5 minutes, 28 secondsthis begin try end try. So you are defining the boundaries of the try and in between you going to have all your
18:05:3518 hours, 5 minutes, 35 secondsSQL statements and your code and the second part going to be the catch parts.
18:05:3918 hours, 5 minutes, 39 secondsSo you say begin catch and end catch. So you are defining the boundaries and then in between you can tell SQL what to do if there is like an error. So what is
18:05:4818 hours, 5 minutes, 48 secondstry and catch? Like the word it says try it's like you are attempt to do something that might fail. So you are telling SQL try to execute this code. So
18:05:5718 hours, 5 minutes, 57 secondsthe SQL going to go and try to execute your codes. And if any error happens while executing your codes, the SQL
18:06:0418 hours, 6 minutes, 4 secondsgoing to jump to the second block and start doing whatever you have defined in the catch. But if there is no errors at
18:06:1118 hours, 6 minutes, 11 secondsall, this part will not be executed. So the catch is like your backup plan. If something goes wrong here, then go to
18:06:1818 hours, 6 minutes, 18 secondsthe plan B and do something. So let's see the workflow of the try catch. So first the SQL going to go and execute the try and then it going to check is
18:06:2618 hours, 6 minutes, 26 secondsthere any error. If we don't have any error then everything ends and that's it. But while execution if the SQL face any error what going to happen it going
18:06:3518 hours, 6 minutes, 35 secondsto go and execute the catch. So as you can see the workflow is very simple and this is what we mean with try and catch.

18:13:0418 hours, 13 minutes, 4 secondsunderstood that we can put all our SQL statements in one stored procedure and you have to go and manually execute the
18:13:1218 hours, 13 minutes, 12 secondsstore procedure. So that means in order to trigger the start procedure, you have manually to execute it and this is of
18:13:1918 hours, 13 minutes, 19 secondscourse a problem. How about to do that automatically? So triggers in SQL they are special stored procedure that
18:13:2618 hours, 13 minutes, 26 secondsautomatically runs or let's say fired in response to a specific event that happens on a table. So what this exactly
18:13:3418 hours, 13 minutes, 34 secondsmeans? So now let's say that we have a table in our database and now something could happen to this table like inserting data, deleting, updating data,
18:13:4318 hours, 13 minutes, 43 secondsall those stuff that is happening we call them events. And now what we can do we can go and attach like a trigger on top of this table and each time an event
18:13:5218 hours, 13 minutes, 52 secondshappened like insert update delete something else going to be triggered like maybe going and inserting data somewhere else in another table or doing
18:14:0118 hours, 14 minutes, 1 seconda check whether we are allowed to delete the data in the first place or maybe sending a warning message or something.
18:14:0718 hours, 14 minutes, 7 secondsSo based on any changes to the table we can trigger another events and we can do that using the SQL triggers and for the
18:14:1518 hours, 14 minutes, 15 secondsSQL triggers we have like multiple types like the DML triggers and this type of trigger going to respond once we have like insert update delete statements.
18:14:2518 hours, 14 minutes, 25 secondsAnother type of triggers we have the DDL triggers like you can make a trigger to respond to any schema changes like
18:14:3218 hours, 14 minutes, 32 secondscreating altering or dropping a table or even view by the way not only tables.
18:14:3718 hours, 14 minutes, 37 secondsAnd the third type of triggers we have the login trigger. So the trigger can respond to login events. Now in this tutorial we're going to focus on the DML
18:14:4518 hours, 14 minutes, 45 secondstriggers the insert update delete. And for the DML triggers we have two types.
18:14:5018 hours, 14 minutes, 50 secondsWe have after triggers and as well we have instead of triggers. So as the name suggest if you use after so it can be
18:14:5818 hours, 14 minutes, 58 secondsexecuted after the event and the other type that instead of it's something that cannot wait until everything happens. So this time the trigger going to be executed during the event not after it.
18:15:1018 hours, 15 minutes, 10 secondsSo now in order to understand all of this we're going to have really nice use case. And now the use case is about
18:15:1918 hours, 15 minutes, 19 secondsmaintaining an audit logs. So what we mean with that? Let's have for example the table employees. The employee data are usually very sensitive informations
18:15:2818 hours, 15 minutes, 28 secondsbecause there we can see which employees are added, the salary updates, the employee terminations and this makes the
18:15:3518 hours, 15 minutes, 35 secondstable very important because we would like to track all those changes that is happening to this table. So each time we are inserting, updating, deleting, we
18:15:4418 hours, 15 minutes, 44 secondswould like to maintain a log about all those changes in order to analyze it later. It is of course very important such a logs for the compliance and the
18:15:5318 hours, 15 minutes, 53 secondsauditors and in case there is like a problem we can go to the logs to understand when this happened who made
18:15:5918 hours, 15 minutes, 59 secondsthe changes and what exactly changed and now in order to maintain logs we're going to use the power of triggers. So what we're going to do we're going to go
18:16:0718 hours, 16 minutes, 7 secondsand attach like a trigger on the table employees and each time we insert new data to the employees we are triggering
18:16:1618 hours, 16 minutes, 16 secondsanother events. So what can happen this new employee going to be inserted in the audit logs in order to have a record
18:16:2318 hours, 16 minutes, 23 secondsabout this activity in the logs. So that means each time you are inserting data to the table employees you are automatically inserting data inside the
18:16:3218 hours, 16 minutes, 32 secondslogs and this is really amazing use case for the triggers. So let's go and implement it. Okay. So now let's check quickly the syntax of the triggers. So
18:16:4018 hours, 16 minutes, 40 secondswe start with the usuals create trigger then the trigger name and then we have to specify on which table this trigger going to be built in. So now we are

18:23:2118 hours, 23 minutes, 21 secondsthat you have to learn about SQL. Now in the next chapter it's going to be about the performance. So as you start writing queries and so on you will start noticing some queries are really slow.
18:23:3118 hours, 23 minutes, 31 secondsNow what we're going to do in this chapter we're going to learn different techniques on how to optimize the performance. And the first and the very famous one is to go and build indexes in
18:23:4018 hours, 23 minutes, 40 secondsdatabases. So let's understand what this means. So what is an index? An index is
18:23:4918 hours, 23 minutes, 49 secondsa data structure that provides a quick access to the rows to improve the speed of your queries. So an index is like a
18:23:5818 hours, 23 minutes, 58 secondsguide for your database in order to speed up the process of searching for data especially if you have like big tables. So now in order to understand
18:24:0618 hours, 24 minutes, 6 secondswhat are indexes, imagine you have huge book and you want to find a specific topic or a chapter. Instead of flipping
18:24:1318 hours, 24 minutes, 13 secondseach single page in order to find the topic that you are searching for, you would use the index at the back of the book in order to jump straight to the
18:24:2118 hours, 24 minutes, 21 secondsright page. And that's exactly what index does but for your data. Another analogy that I use in order to understand indexes is think about the
18:24:3018 hours, 24 minutes, 30 secondsindexes as a big hotel. Now let's say that in the hotel we don't have any guide and you would like to find the
18:24:3618 hours, 24 minutes, 36 secondsroom number let's say 5001. Now what you going to do? You're going to go and search for your room floor by floor and
18:24:4418 hours, 24 minutes, 44 secondschecking each room until you find your room. But instead of that, thankfully hotels have a numbering system. And you
18:24:5118 hours, 24 minutes, 51 secondscan ask for a map from the reception in order to understand in which building in which floor you can find your room. So by just following the map and maybe some
18:25:0018 hours, 25 minutessigns, it's going to be very quickly to locate and find your room in such a big hotel. And that's exactly what each database needs. It needs an index in
18:25:0818 hours, 25 minutes, 8 secondsorder to help the database finding and locating the right data without having to scan
18:25:1818 hours, 25 minutes, 18 secondseverything. And now let's say that you ask me, you know what, I have this big table and I would like to speed up the queries using indexes. And my first
18:25:2718 hours, 25 minutes, 27 secondsquestion going to be, what are you exactly doing with this table? Are you using this table to search for text or are you doing like complex analyszis
18:25:3518 hours, 25 minutes, 35 secondswith this table? And the reason why I'm asking this is that we have different indexes in databases for different purposes. So now let's have a quick look
18:25:4318 hours, 25 minutes, 43 secondsto the different types of indexes that we have in database. I divide the indexes in databases into three categories. The first one is by the
18:25:5218 hours, 25 minutes, 52 secondsstructure how the database is organizing and referencing the data. And here we have two types. The clustered index and
18:26:0018 hours, 26 minutesthe non-clustered index. Those are very important to understand. Now we have another category for the indexes. We can
18:26:0718 hours, 26 minutes, 7 secondsdivide them by the storage. And in this category we are talking about how the data is stored physically in the
18:26:1418 hours, 26 minutes, 14 secondsdatabase. So we have two types. We have the row store index and the column store index. And the third type is the
18:26:2118 hours, 26 minutes, 21 secondsfunctions and here we have two types. We have the unique index and the filtered index. Now each index type has its own
18:26:2918 hours, 26 minutes, 29 secondsstrings but as well there is always a tradeoff. Some might improve their read performance. The other one might improve
18:26:3618 hours, 26 minutes, 36 secondsthe insert and update operations. So it's all about choosing the right type of index for the job. So now what we're going to do, we're going to go and deep
18:26:4418 hours, 26 minutes, 44 secondsdive into each of those types in order to understand how they work and how we can create them. And we will start with the first category, the structure. We
18:26:5318 hours, 26 minutes, 53 secondshave the clustered index and the nclustered index.
18:27:0018 hours, 27 minutesNow before we dive into how the indexes works in databases, let's understand first what happens to the database
18:27:0718 hours, 27 minutes, 7 secondstables if you don't use any index. When you create a new table in your database like for example the customers table
18:27:1418 hours, 27 minutes, 14 secondswhere you have let's say 20 customers inside this table. What you're going to see at the client side is like spreadsheets like a table with rows and
18:27:2318 hours, 27 minutes, 23 secondscolumns but behind the scenes the database store it a bit differently.
18:27:2718 hours, 27 minutes, 27 secondsIt's going to store the data in a data file on the disk and inside this file the data can be stored inside blocks
18:27:3418 hours, 27 minutes, 34 secondscalled pages. So it's not like rows and columns that are stored inside data files and inside the data files we have
18:27:4218 hours, 27 minutes, 42 secondspages. So what is a page? A page is the unit of data storage in a database and
18:27:4918 hours, 27 minutes, 49 secondsit is a fixed size of 8 kilobyt where the SQL database can store anything inside it. It can store inside it the
18:27:5718 hours, 27 minutes, 57 secondsrows of your tables or columns metadata indexes and every time you are interacting with your data the SQL is
18:28:0518 hours, 28 minutes, 5 secondsreading and writing to those pages. So as you can see the SQL is not storing the data inside like rows and columns.
18:28:1318 hours, 28 minutes, 13 secondsSo if you are running a query the SQL is not like selecting a specific column it always fetch a data page in order to
18:28:2118 hours, 28 minutes, 21 secondsread the rows inside this page. And the main two types that we're going to learn is the data page and the index page. So
18:28:2818 hours, 28 minutes, 28 secondshow the data page looks like it is divided into multiple sections. The first section is the page header where the database can store key informations
18:28:3618 hours, 28 minutes, 36 secondsabout the metadata like the page ID and it has the following format. It start with the file ID like one and then we
18:28:4418 hours, 28 minutes, 44 secondshave a unique number for each page. So for example 150. So the page header is a fixed size of 96 bytes. Now to the next
18:28:5118 hours, 28 minutes, 51 secondssection, we're going to have a variable size. This is where your data row is going to be stored. So your actual data and row is going to be stored in this
18:28:5918 hours, 28 minutes, 59 secondssection. And the SQL going to try and fits as many rows as it can in one single page. And this of course depends
18:29:0618 hours, 29 minutes, 6 secondson the size of each row. So if you have like a large table where the rows are really big, so SQL can fit only few rows
18:29:1418 hours, 29 minutes, 14 secondsin one single page. And now moving on to the last section in the data page, we have the offset array. This is like a
18:29:2118 hours, 29 minutes, 21 secondsquick index for the rows stored inside this page. It keeps track of where each rows begins so that the SQL can easily
18:29:3018 hours, 29 minutes, 30 secondslocate a specific row without having SQL like scanning the entire page in order to find a row. So this is the structure
18:29:3718 hours, 29 minutes, 37 secondsof the data page and this is exactly how the SQL stores data inside the databases. So now back to our example
18:29:4418 hours, 29 minutes, 44 secondswhere we have the customers table and 20 rows. So let's see how SQL going to be creating those pages. Now if you are not using any index in this table. So now
18:29:5318 hours, 29 minutes, 53 secondswhat going to happen? SQL going to insert the data inside those pages as you are inserting the data inside the customers. So maybe first you are
18:30:0118 hours, 30 minutes, 1 secondinserting the customers like 12 5 6 7 and SQL going to insert it to the data pages exactly like that. So that means
18:30:1018 hours, 30 minutes, 10 secondsSQL is just inserting the data as you insert it to the table. So let's say each data page is like fitting only five rows. So after we insert five customers,
18:30:1918 hours, 30 minutes, 19 secondsSQL going to go and create another data page for the next rows. So in the next page, the SQL going to insert the next
18:30:2618 hours, 30 minutes, 26 secondsfive customers. And once it's full, it's going to create another data page in order to start adding the next customer until we have like for example four
18:30:3518 hours, 30 minutes, 35 secondspages for that 20 customers. So now if you check the customers inside those four pages you see that they are not
18:30:4218 hours, 30 minutes, 42 secondssorted at all and that's because in this scenario we are not using any index. So we call this structure as a heap
18:30:4918 hours, 30 minutes, 49 secondsstructure. So a heap table is a table without a clustered index. That means the rows are stored randomly without any
18:30:5718 hours, 30 minutes, 57 secondsparticular order. This is not a really bad because it's going to be very quick to insert data inside this table. But of
18:31:0518 hours, 31 minutes, 5 secondscourse finding something from this table going to be very slow. So this is the first tradeoff. You have a very fast
18:31:1218 hours, 31 minutes, 12 secondswrites but a very bad reads. Think about it like you are throwing all your papers in a drawer without organizing them. So
18:31:2018 hours, 31 minutes, 20 secondsyou can toss things very quickly in this drawer. But if you want to search for specific paper later, it's going to be very long process until you find it
18:31:2818 hours, 31 minutes, 28 secondsbecause nothing's in order. So now let's see how the SQL going to handle if you read something from this table. Let's say that you are searching for the
18:31:3618 hours, 31 minutes, 36 secondscustomer with the ID 14. So now SQL has totally no idea where to find this customer. So SQL going to start fetching
18:31:4318 hours, 31 minutes, 43 secondseach data page and start scanning each row. So it's going to start with the first data page and start scanning.
18:31:4918 hours, 31 minutes, 49 secondsWell, SQL will not find 14 here. So SQL going to go to the next page and start scanning as well. Searching for the ID
18:31:5718 hours, 31 minutes, 57 seconds14 and nothing going to be found. The same thing for the third page as well.
18:32:0018 hours, 32 minutesSQL will not find 14. So SQL going to go to the last data page and there after scanning four rows in this data page
18:32:0818 hours, 32 minutes, 8 secondsfinally SQL going to find the customer number 14 and it's going to return it for the clients. So as you can see in order to find one customer SQL did read
18:32:1818 hours, 32 minutes, 18 secondsfour different pages and scanned like 19 rows in order to find the customer and this process we call it full table scan.
18:32:2718 hours, 32 minutes, 27 secondsSo the full table scans means SQL is scanning the entire table page by page and row by row in order to find specific
18:32:3518 hours, 32 minutes, 35 secondsrow. And of course for this table maybe it's not a big deal. But if you have like a big table where you have like hundred of thousands or maybe millions
18:32:4318 hours, 32 minutes, 43 secondsof rows searching through the heap structure going to be very painful and slow in order to locate one row. And here exactly why we need indexes in SQL
18:32:5218 hours, 32 minutes, 52 secondsdatabases. So let's understand the first type of indexes the clustered
18:33:0018 hours, 33 minutesindex. All right. So now let's understand what can happen if you create clustered index in your table. So say you create a clustered index on the ID
18:33:0918 hours, 33 minutes, 9 secondscolumn of the customers. So the first thing that's going to happen SQL going to physically sort all the data based on
18:33:1618 hours, 33 minutes, 16 secondsthe column ID. So the rows going to rearranged in each data page from the lowest to the highest. So in the first
18:33:2318 hours, 33 minutes, 23 secondspage we're going to have the first customer ID number one then 2 3 4 5 until we reach in the last page the last
18:33:3118 hours, 33 minutes, 31 secondscustomer number 20. So as you can see the first page has the lowest value and the last page has the highest value. So
18:33:3918 hours, 33 minutes, 39 secondsthat's not all. The next step is that SQL going to go and start structuring and building the B tree. So what is a B tree? A B tree short for balance tree.
18:33:5018 hours, 33 minutes, 50 secondsIt is hierarchal structure that store the data as a tree upside
18:33:5618 hours, 33 minutes, 56 seconds[Music]
18:34:0218 hours, 34 minutes, 2 secondsdown. It start with the root the root node and then it keep branching out until we reach eventually the leaves.
18:34:1018 hours, 34 minutes, 10 secondsBetween the leaf nodes and the root nodes we call this section the intermediate nodes. So it could be like one level or multiple levels between the
18:34:1818 hours, 34 minutes, 18 secondsroot and the leaves. And once SQL construct the B tree, it's going to be very easy for SQL to navigate through the B tree in order to find specific
18:34:2718 hours, 34 minutes, 27 secondsinformation. So let's see how SQL is building the B tree for the clustered index. Now very important to understand
18:34:3418 hours, 34 minutes, 34 secondsthat the leaves the leaf nodes and the B tree for the clustered index contain the actual data the data pages. So all your
18:34:4318 hours, 34 minutes, 43 secondsnice sorted data pages and your data is stored at the leaf level. Then after that SQL going to start building the
18:34:5018 hours, 34 minutes, 50 secondsintermediate nodes and here the database going to use different type of pages. We have the index page. So in the index
18:34:5818 hours, 34 minutes, 58 secondspage we cannot find the actual data the entire rows but instead the index page stores a key value that contain a
18:35:0618 hours, 35 minutes, 6 secondspointer to another index page or to a data page. So for example we have here the value one the key and then the value
18:35:1418 hours, 35 minutes, 14 secondsgoing to be the ID of the data page. So here we don't have like the whole row about the data we have here only a
18:35:2118 hours, 35 minutes, 21 secondspointer to another data page. So here we are telling the scale if you are searching for ids between 1 and five you
18:35:2818 hours, 35 minutes, 28 secondscan locate it at the data page ID 1.100 and then we can store in this index page another pointer where we can
18:35:3618 hours, 35 minutes, 36 secondstell SQL if you are searching between 6 and 10 then you can locate it at the second data page. So this is the
18:35:4418 hours, 35 minutes, 44 secondsstructure of the index page it contains only pointers to another page and the same thing for the second two pages. The
18:35:5118 hours, 35 minutes, 51 secondsSQL going to create another index page where it's going to says if you are searching for IDs between 11 and 15, you can find it at the third page 1 double
18:36:0018 hours, 36 minutespoint 10002. And for the last group between 16 and 20, we have another pointer to the last page to the page number
18:36:0818 hours, 36 minutes, 8 secondsone3. So as you can see inside those index pages, we have like a pointer for each group of ids for each cluster. So
18:36:1618 hours, 36 minutes, 16 secondsfor the group of customers between 1 and five we have one pointer and for the second group between six and 10 we have another pointer. So that means we don't
18:36:2518 hours, 36 minutes, 25 secondshave here a pointer for each row. We have a pointer for each group for each cluster. That's why we call it clustered
18:36:3318 hours, 36 minutes, 33 secondsindex. And now once SQL is done building the intermediate nodes, SQL going to go and build the last node, the root node where it says if you are searching for
18:36:4218 hours, 36 minutes, 42 secondscustomers between 1 and 10, then go to the index page with the ID 1.200. So that means the route node here
18:36:5118 hours, 36 minutes, 51 secondsis pointing to another index page, not directly to the data page. And the same thing, we need another pointer for the
18:36:5918 hours, 36 minutes, 59 secondssecond index page. So the customers between 11 and 20 go to the index page with the ID
18:37:0618 hours, 37 minutes, 6 seconds1.201 and this is exactly what going to happen if you create a clustered index in SQL. First it going to go and
18:37:1418 hours, 37 minutes, 14 secondsphysically sort all your data in the databases. So if it's from the first time sorted randomly SQL has to arrange
18:37:2218 hours, 37 minutes, 22 secondseverything and sort the data from the scratch. And then it's going to go and build this structure where you have in the root node and index page in the
18:37:3018 hours, 37 minutes, 30 secondsintermediate nodes the index pages but at the leaf level at the leaves we have the actual data the data pages. So now
18:37:3818 hours, 37 minutes, 38 secondslet's see what going to happen if you query the table where you search for the ID number 14. So it's going to check which pointer to use since 14 is in the
18:37:4718 hours, 37 minutes, 47 secondsgroup between 11 and 20. It's going to go and use the second pointer to the index page with the ID one double point 2011. And here the SQL going to open this index page and check the pointers.
18:37:5918 hours, 37 minutes, 59 secondsSo since 14 is between 11 and 15 it going to go and use the pointer to the data page one point 102 and with that
18:38:0818 hours, 38 minutes, 8 secondsSQL located the correct data page the third page and now SQL going to open this data page and find the customer ID
18:38:1718 hours, 38 minutes, 17 secondsnumber 14. So as you can see it was very fast for SQL to locate the correct data page with only three jumps from the root
18:38:2418 hours, 38 minutes, 24 secondsnode to the intermediate node. The SQL were able to find fast the correct data page. And here SQL needs only to read
18:38:3118 hours, 38 minutes, 31 secondsone data page instead of reading as we saw in the heap structure four different data pages. And of course you might say but still here we are reading like three
18:38:3918 hours, 38 minutes, 39 secondspages. Well, reading an index page is very fast compared to the data page because reading a data page is always slower than reading an index page. So,
18:38:4918 hours, 38 minutes, 49 secondsas you can see, this P3 structure, the clustered index structure did help the SQL and the database to locate the right
18:38:5618 hours, 38 minutes, 56 secondsdata in the right

18:50:3718 hours, 50 minutes, 37 secondscriteria in order to be good candidate for the clustered index. First, it's going to be good if the values inside the columns are unique. And second, and
18:50:4518 hours, 50 minutes, 45 secondsit is way more important than that, the values of this column should not change a lot because if this column having a
18:50:5318 hours, 50 minutes, 53 secondslot of update operators and the data is keep changing, that means each time SQL going to go and start sorting the data
18:51:0018 hours, 51 minutesagain left and right. So having a column that is frequently changing, it's not good for clustered index. And that's why
18:51:0818 hours, 51 minutes, 8 secondsthe primary keys of tables are a perfect candidate because first they are unique and second we will never go and update a
18:51:1518 hours, 51 minutes, 15 secondsprimary key value. We always append a new primary key value and that's why primary keys are perfect for clustered
18:51:2318 hours, 51 minutes, 23 secondsindex. And one more thing where I go and use clustered index is that to optimize the performance of a range query. If you
18:51:3018 hours, 51 minutes, 30 secondsare quering the data between one value and another one clusters index works really well. Now in the other hand if we are talking about the non-clustered
18:51:3718 hours, 51 minutes, 37 secondsindex we could use it on coms that are used in the search conditions or if you are joining tables without using the
18:51:4418 hours, 51 minutes, 44 secondsprimary keys then you can go and apply the nclustered index in order to have faster joins or you can go and use it to optimize the performance if you are
18:51:5218 hours, 51 minutes, 52 secondssearching for an exact value exact match. So those are the main and important differences between the clustered and the nclustered indexes.

18:53:5718 hours, 53 minutes, 57 secondsright. So back to SQL and the first question is where do we find indexes in the database? Well you can go and explore it. If you go to the object

18:57:0618 hours, 57 minutes, 6 secondsID. Now as we learned we cannot create multiple clustered index. But let's go and test that. So I will just take the whole thing and let's say I would like

18:58:2118 hours, 58 minutes, 21 secondsmaybe multiple customers having the same name. And as well updates could happen on the first name and that's going to be very expensive. So that means I'm going
18:58:2918 hours, 58 minutes, 29 secondsto stick with my index on the customer ID. Let's go and execute it. And now I have again my index on my table. Now

18:59:1818 hours, 59 minutes, 18 secondsconvention. So DB customers and we're going to use the last name for this index. So on

18:59:5718 hours, 59 minutes, 57 secondstable. Let's say for example, now we our query looks like this. We are searching for the first name using for example the
19:00:0419 hours, 4 secondsvalue Anna. And now this query happens a lot and maybe slow. So we can go and create new nonclustered index. So let me
19:00:1219 hours, 12 secondsjust have it like this. And for the nonclustered index you don't have to specify always like nonclustered index.
19:00:1919 hours, 19 secondsAs default it's going to be nonclustered. So we can skip that. And here let's call it first name. And the
19:00:2619 hours, 26 secondscolumn that we are using is the first name. So let's go and create this index.
19:00:3319 hours, 33 secondsAnd now let's go and refresh our indexes. And as you can see, SQL did create a nonclustered index for the
19:00:4019 hours, 40 secondsfirst name. So if you don't specify the type of the index, it's going to be as a default nonclustered
19:00:4919 hours, 49 secondsindex. All right. So now let's talk about the composite index. It is an index that has multiple columns inside
19:00:5619 hours, 56 secondsthe same index. So far we have used only one column in the index but we can go and specify multiple columns and that's
19:01:0419 hours, 1 minute, 4 secondsbecause sometimes our wear conditions are complicated and based on multiple columns. So for example let's say that
19:01:1119 hours, 1 minute, 11 secondswe are searching for country equal to USA and at the same time we are saying
19:01:1819 hours, 1 minute, 18 secondsthe score should be higher than 500. So that means in this condition we are using two columns and we would like to
19:01:2519 hours, 1 minute, 25 secondsspeed up this query. So how we going to do it? So we're going to go and create let's say an index and give it a name DB
19:01:3319 hours, 1 minute, 33 secondscustomers and let's say country score on sales DB customers. And now it is very
19:01:4319 hours, 1 minute, 43 secondsimportant to do the following thing. Now we have to go and define a list of columns that we want to be included in
19:01:5019 hours, 1 minute, 50 secondsthis index. And it is very crucial and important that you get the same order as your query. So your query start with the
19:01:5919 hours, 1 minute, 59 secondscountry and then the score. You have to do it the same thing in the index. So the first column it's going to be the
19:02:0619 hours, 2 minutes, 6 secondscountry and then the score. So it must be the same order as your query. So let's go and create this index. And if
19:02:1519 hours, 2 minutes, 15 secondsyou go to the indexes over here, you can see that we have created our new index.
19:02:1919 hours, 2 minutes, 19 secondsSo now once you create such a index and your table going to be like always updating this index you have to be committed and responsible. So in your
19:02:2819 hours, 2 minutes, 28 secondsqueries if you want to filter the data using country and score always start with the country then the score in order to be able to use the index optimizer.
19:02:3819 hours, 2 minutes, 38 secondsSo if you do it like this the index going to be working but if you go and query the way around. So you start with
19:02:4519 hours, 2 minutes, 45 secondsthe score and then the country the SQL will not be using your index. So either you adjust your queries or you have to
19:02:5419 hours, 2 minutes, 54 secondsgo and recreate the index based on this switch. So be very careful with the composite indexes. The order is very
19:03:0219 hours, 3 minutes, 2 secondscrucial. So you're going to have it exactly like the query. And now you might say you know what now we have like a nice index for those two columns. What
19:03:1119 hours, 3 minutes, 11 secondsgoing to happen if I go and use in my query only one of them like for example the country. So now the question is if I
19:03:1819 hours, 3 minutes, 18 secondsgo and execute this query is the SQL is using this index even though that I don't have the score. Well yes because
19:03:2719 hours, 3 minutes, 27 secondsit follows the leftmost prefix rule. So this means SQL can use the index if you are using always the lift columns. So
19:03:3619 hours, 3 minutes, 36 secondshere in our index country is on the left that's why it is working over here. But if you go and skip the lift column it
19:03:4419 hours, 3 minutes, 44 secondswill not work. So if you go over here for example and say let's go and select only the score and it is like higher than 500.
19:03:5519 hours, 3 minutes, 55 secondsWhat we have done, we have skipped the country in this query and that's why it will not be working. So as long as you
19:04:0219 hours, 4 minutes, 2 secondsare including the left columns, it will work even though it is only one column.
19:04:0719 hours, 4 minutes, 7 secondsSo in this scenario, the first query going to use the index, the second one will not be using it. So now let me give you a very simple example in order to
19:04:1519 hours, 4 minutes, 15 secondsunderstand how this works. So let's say that we have an index using four columns A, B, C, D. Now in your query if you go and target the column A the index going
19:04:2419 hours, 4 minutes, 24 secondsto be used. Now the same thing going to happen if you go and use A and P. So if you're using those two columns you will
19:04:3119 hours, 4 minutes, 31 secondsbe using the index. So those are where the index will be used. So now let's
19:04:3819 hours, 4 minutes, 38 secondshave the scenarios where the index wants be used. So for example if you go and just jump immediately to the column B.
19:04:4719 hours, 4 minutes, 47 secondsSo you are not using the left column the A that's why you will not be using the index and as well in your query if you are using A and you are skipping the P.
19:04:5619 hours, 4 minutes, 56 secondsSo you have A and then C you will not be using the index. So you have always to use always the lift columns. So here if
19:05:0519 hours, 5 minutes, 5 secondsyou are using A B C you will be using the index. And let's see here you are using A B and then you jump and skip to the D you will not be using the index.
19:05:1419 hours, 5 minutes, 14 secondsSo this is what we mean with the leftmost prefix rule by using the composite index. So if you're using multiple columns inside one index, be
19:05:2219 hours, 5 minutes, 22 secondscareful with the order of the columns that you are defining. All right. So that's all for this category, clustered and uncclustered index. Now we're going
19:05:3019 hours, 5 minutes, 30 secondsto move to the second category where we talk about the indexes by the storage, the row store and the column store.
19:05:4119 hours, 5 minutes, 41 secondsSo now let's say that we have a table we have multiple rows and multiple columns.
19:05:4519 hours, 5 minutes, 45 secondsNow if we use a row store index this is the classical one. What going to happen?
19:05:5019 hours, 5 minutes, 50 secondsOur table going to be splitted into multiple rows. And as we learned each group of rows going to be stored inside a data page. So that means we are
19:05:5819 hours, 5 minutes, 58 secondsorganizing the data row by row which means all the columns for each row going to be stored together. This is the
19:06:0619 hours, 6 minutes, 6 secondstraditional way on how the databases organize their data where the informations are stored row by row. But now in the other side if you use column
19:06:1519 hours, 6 minutes, 15 secondsstore index the SQL going to go and split your table into multiple separate columns and then SQL going to go and
19:06:2119 hours, 6 minutes, 21 secondsstore the values of one column together in data page. So that means if you go and open a data page you will find only
19:06:2919 hours, 6 minutes, 29 secondsthe values of one column. You will not find the entire row. So if it's like the first name you will see only the first name informations you will not see the last name information in this data page.
19:06:3919 hours, 6 minutes, 39 secondsSo if you compare them the row store index stores the data row by row the column store index stores the data column by column. So this is a very high
19:06:4819 hours, 6 minutes, 48 secondslevel representation on how the column store index is stored. As you know me we go in details in order to understand exactly how SQL works with the column store index. So let's go.
19:07:0319 hours, 7 minutes, 3 secondsAll right. So now let's say that we have a table for the customers. We have three columns ID, name and status. And as well we have around 2 million rows, 2 million
19:07:1219 hours, 7 minutes, 12 secondscustomers. And as we learned as a default, the table going to be built as a heap structure where the rows are stored row by row inside data pages. But
19:07:2019 hours, 7 minutes, 20 secondsnow we go and create a column store index on top of this table. So now once you do that SQL going to go through a
19:07:2819 hours, 7 minutes, 28 secondsprocess in order to build the column store. So the first step is SQL going to go and divide the data the rows into row
19:07:3519 hours, 7 minutes, 35 secondsgroups. Now in SQL server each row group can contain around like 1 million row.
19:07:4019 hours, 7 minutes, 40 secondsSo in this example our table going to be splitted into two row groups. The first one million row in one group and the second one in another row group. Now you
19:07:4919 hours, 7 minutes, 49 secondsmight ask me we are talking about columns. Why we are splitting the rows?
19:07:5319 hours, 7 minutes, 53 secondsWell, this is just a pre-step in order just to optimize the performance and to do parallel processing. And of course,
19:07:5919 hours, 7 minutes, 59 secondsthe data will not be stored like this because we have the second step. Now, in the next step, SQL going to go and segment the columns. So now, SQL will go
19:08:0819 hours, 8 minutes, 8 secondsfor each row group and start splitting the data by the columns. And that's why we call it a column store because we are separating the columns from each others.
19:08:1719 hours, 8 minutes, 17 secondsSo that means we have one segment for the ID, another one for the name and a third one for the status. And this can happen for each row group. And now it's
19:08:2619 hours, 8 minutes, 26 secondsgoing to move to the third step in this process. We have the data compression.
19:08:3119 hours, 8 minutes, 31 secondsAnd this is the most important step in this process because it is the reason why column store is very fast compared
19:08:3819 hours, 8 minutes, 38 secondsto the ro store. So in this process there are like different techniques on how to do data compression and the most famous one is that it's going to go and
19:08:4719 hours, 8 minutes, 47 secondscreate like a dictionary. Let's take for example the column status the status of the customer whether it is active or
19:08:5419 hours, 8 minutes, 54 secondsinactive. So the word active and inactive going to be repeated like 2 million times because we have 2 million customers and since it is like string it
19:09:0319 hours, 9 minutes, 3 secondsis like taking a lot of space and storage. But now instead of that we're going to go and compress the data. So first it's going to go and create a
19:09:1019 hours, 9 minutes, 10 secondsdictionary by replacing the value active and inactive into smaller values like one and two. So we have like a mapping between the long value to a small value.
19:09:2219 hours, 9 minutes, 22 secondsAnd after that SQL going to store like a data stream where we have like only two values one two one two. So we're going to have like a big stream of 2 million
19:09:3119 hours, 9 minutes, 31 secondsrows. So it's going to go and do this for each column and with that the size of each column going to be changed depends of course on how much different
19:09:3919 hours, 9 minutes, 39 secondsvalues you have in each column. So this step is very important in order to reduce the size of the data and as well to increase the performance. So now once everything is organized and compressed,
19:09:5019 hours, 9 minutes, 50 secondsSQL going to go and start storing the results in databases. But TSQL will not use the standard databases that we have
19:09:5819 hours, 9 minutes, 58 secondslearned previously. But instead going to use a special database called LOB large object page. So now let's quickly
19:10:0619 hours, 10 minutes, 6 secondscompare the structure of the normal database that we have learned in the row store with the new one, the column store, the LOB data page. So as usual
19:10:1419 hours, 10 minutes, 14 secondseach page has a header. This is same as any data page. But the next section is going to be the segment header. It has like metadata informations about the
19:10:2319 hours, 10 minutes, 23 secondscolumn segment that is stored in this page. Like we have the segment ID, the row group ID, the column ID and it has
19:10:3119 hours, 10 minutes, 31 secondsas well very important information the ID to the dictionary page. So the dictionary page is as well a type of
19:10:3819 hours, 10 minutes, 38 secondspages in SQL. It has as well a header but inside it we have like a mapping. So it maps the original value, the long
19:10:4519 hours, 10 minutes, 45 secondsone, the inactive to the smaller version of this value, for example, one. And that's all for the dictionary page. It has the mapping between the original
19:10:5419 hours, 10 minutes, 54 secondsvalues and the smaller values. And beneath the segment header, we can have now the important place where our data can be stored. We have the data stream.
19:11:0319 hours, 11 minutes, 3 secondsSo it is like sequence of ids from the dictionary that represents the values of the columns side by side. And of course,
19:11:1119 hours, 11 minutes, 11 secondswe cannot fit the whole 1 million rows inside this data stream. We're going to have like multiple LOP databases. So
19:11:1819 hours, 11 minutes, 18 secondsthis is how exactly the SQL stores your data. If you decided to go with the column store, so let's go back to the process. So back to the process. As you
19:11:2719 hours, 11 minutes, 27 secondscan see, SQL is storing the data as LO data storage. So this is the last step and with that SQL did convert your table
19:11:3519 hours, 11 minutes, 35 secondsinto a column store. So now we cannot just create a column store without defining whether it is clustered index or non-clustered index. So let's start
19:11:4419 hours, 11 minutes, 44 secondswith the first one the clustered column store index. So if you create such a index SQL of course will not be building
19:11:5119 hours, 11 minutes, 51 secondsa B3 structure. SQL going to use exactly this structure the column store structure. So as we learned the cluster
19:11:5819 hours, 11 minutes, 58 secondsindex is a complete makeover of your table. when you apply it then SQL going to format everything column-wise and it
19:12:0519 hours, 12 minutes, 5 secondsis fully replacing the old row based table structure that we have at the start. So once you apply the clustered column store index it will not leave
19:12:1419 hours, 12 minutes, 14 secondsanything behind and your table going to be completely structured as a column store and one more thing which is makes sense of course all the columns from the
19:12:2319 hours, 12 minutes, 23 secondsoriginal table going to be converted to a column store. So it is not leaving anything behind it. But in the other hand, if you are using non-clustered
19:12:3119 hours, 12 minutes, 31 secondscolumn store index, as we learned, it is like a companion to your existing table.
19:12:3719 hours, 12 minutes, 37 secondsSo it coexist with the table and it will not replace anything. So the column store index can be an additional thing
19:12:4419 hours, 12 minutes, 44 secondsthat is stored beside your table. So that means the original table will not be deleted at all like the clustered
19:12:5119 hours, 12 minutes, 51 secondscolumn store index. The first one is in the old row based storage. the regular table, the first one, and your data
19:12:5819 hours, 12 minutes, 58 secondsgoing to be as well stored in a separate structure in the column store index. And of course, in the non-clustered column
19:13:0519 hours, 13 minutes, 5 secondsstore index, since we are creating an extra index outside of your original table, you can go and define which column should be included in this process. It must not be all the columns.
19:13:1619 hours, 13 minutes, 16 secondsYou can go for example with only the status. So that means you build a column store index only for one column for the status of the customers. So this is what
19:13:2419 hours, 13 minutes, 24 secondswe mean with the clustered column store index and the nclustered column store
19:13:3219 hours, 13 minutes, 32 secondsindex. All right friends, so now you might ask me why we are doing all those stuff. Why I would split my data by the
19:13:3919 hours, 13 minutes, 39 secondscolumns? Well, it's all because of analytics. Because in analytics we have like big complex query where we have a
19:13:4619 hours, 13 minutes, 46 secondslot of data aggregations and stuff on big tables. And the roster index is perfectly designed in order to improve
19:13:5419 hours, 13 minutes, 54 secondsthe performance of such big queries. And that's why SQL databases like SQL server and as well BI tools like Tableau and
19:14:0119 hours, 14 minutes, 1 secondPowerBI did adopt this methods in order to offer fast platform for data analyzes. So now let's understand
19:14:0919 hours, 14 minutes, 9 secondsexactly why the column store index is way faster for data analyzes than the row store index. So let's go. So again
19:14:1619 hours, 14 minutes, 16 secondswe have the customers tables and let's say we have like five customers where we have ID, name and status and as we learned before if we are using roster
19:14:2419 hours, 14 minutes, 24 secondsindex the data can be stored in multiple databases and in each database we're going to have the whole record the whole information about one customer. So for
19:14:3319 hours, 14 minutes, 33 secondsthis example we're going to have like three databases but if you are using the column store index it's going to be stored little bit differently. So the first column the id going to be stored
19:14:4119 hours, 14 minutes, 41 secondsin one data page and here the SQL will not go and build a dictionary because the ids are already short. So we're going to have like one data stream with
19:14:4919 hours, 14 minutes, 49 secondsall ids and now for the next column name is going to be stored in separate data page where we're going to have an extra dictionary page where each name going to
19:14:5719 hours, 14 minutes, 57 secondsbe mapped to one small value. So the data going to be compressed and we're going to save storage. Now the database going to create for the third column the
19:15:0419 hours, 15 minutes, 4 secondsstatus one more data page and the dictionary here going to be very small.
19:15:0819 hours, 15 minutes, 8 secondsSo for active we're going to have one and for the inactive we're going to have two and in the data stream we will be storing only the ids of the dictionary.
19:15:1519 hours, 15 minutes, 15 secondsSo now let's understand why the column store is faster. Let's have the following query. We want to find the total number of customers that are
19:15:2319 hours, 15 minutes, 23 secondsactive. So we have the query select count star from customers and we're going to filter the data by the status where it is equal to active. So now if
19:15:3219 hours, 15 minutes, 32 secondswe query the table with the row store what can happen? SQL have first to go and collect the data. So it's going to go to the first data page and collect
19:15:3919 hours, 15 minutes, 39 secondsthe first two customer then to the second to the third and so on. And as you can see SQL here is reading everything the whole row the ID the name
19:15:4819 hours, 15 minutes, 48 secondsthe status even though that for the query we actually we don't need all those informations we just need to count how many customers we need with the
19:15:5619 hours, 15 minutes, 56 secondsstatus active but still cannot go and selectively only reading the status has to read the whole record. So after SQL
19:16:0519 hours, 16 minutes, 5 secondshas all the data it's going to go and filter the data. So it's going to go and remove the inactive rows and then SQL going to do the aggregate operation and with that we're going to get three rows.
19:16:1619 hours, 16 minutes, 16 secondsSo that's why the total count of active customers going to be three. But now let's see how SQL going to query the column store. So SQL first have to
19:16:2319 hours, 16 minutes, 23 secondsanalyze okay which columns do I need actually for this query. Well, we need only the status. So SQL will not go and open all three data pages and read it.
19:16:3319 hours, 16 minutes, 33 secondsSQL will target only one data page the database where we have the column status. So it's going to take this very simple data stream and then it's going
19:16:4119 hours, 16 minutes, 41 secondsto go and understand the dictionary and it going to go and remove all the values where it is equal to two. So without in the output we have only three values and
19:16:4919 hours, 16 minutes, 49 secondsSQL going to go and do a very quick count for those values. So in the output we will get as well three total number of active customers. So now if you
19:16:5719 hours, 16 minutes, 57 secondscompare this intermediate result sets from the row store and the column store you can see that in the row store we have fetched and retrieved a lot of
19:17:0619 hours, 17 minutes, 6 secondsunnecessary informations for this query and this of course going to make the speed of the query very slow but in the
19:17:1319 hours, 17 minutes, 13 secondscolumn store reads exactly what it needs for this aggregation and we didn't read any extra informations about the names
19:17:2019 hours, 17 minutes, 20 secondsof the customers the ids it didn't like open any extra data pages it exactly gets the data that it needs for the
19:17:2819 hours, 17 minutes, 28 secondsaggregation and that's exactly why the performance of queries where we have aggregations and data analyzes is going
19:17:3519 hours, 17 minutes, 35 secondsto be very fast if you are using column store compared to the row store. So that's why we use column store for big
19:17:4219 hours, 17 minutes, 42 secondsdata and data analytics. All right. So now let's summarize the differences between the row store and the column store indexes side by side. So let's
19:17:5019 hours, 17 minutes, 50 secondsstart by the definition. The row store going to go and organize and store the data row by row. It is really nice method if you need a lot of columns in
19:17:5719 hours, 17 minutes, 57 secondsone row. But in the other hand, the column store index going to go and store the data and organize it column by column which is really great if you're
19:18:0519 hours, 18 minutes, 5 secondsfocusing on specific column. Now if you are talking about the storage efficiency, the row store index going to take more space compared to the column
19:18:1319 hours, 18 minutes, 13 secondsstore index and that's because as we learned the column store going to go and compress the data which going to save a lot of storage if you have large tables.
19:18:2019 hours, 18 minutes, 20 secondsNow to the next point which is more important about the performance. The read and write optimizations we can say for the row store things are more
19:18:2819 hours, 18 minutes, 28 secondsbalanced. So you will get a decent speed for both write and read operations but things in the column store is different.
19:18:3619 hours, 18 minutes, 36 secondsIt is fast for reading especially if you are doing data analytics but writing data like inserting and updating it is
19:18:4319 hours, 18 minutes, 43 secondsslower because as we learned there are like multiple steps until the data is written in the pages. So in one hand you are optimizing the speed of your
19:18:5219 hours, 18 minutes, 52 secondsanalytical queries but in the other hand changing data it is slower than the roster index. Now let's talk about the next point input and output efficiency.
19:19:0119 hours, 19 minutes, 1 secondWell the roster index it's not really good because you are retrieving a lot of columns. So a lot of data should be read
19:19:0819 hours, 19 minutes, 8 secondsfrom the disk storage in order to answer your queries. But in the other hand for the column store it is lower and that's
19:19:1519 hours, 19 minutes, 15 secondsbecause it targets exactly the data and columns that is needed for the query. So there will be generally less data that
19:19:2219 hours, 19 minutes, 22 secondsis read from the disk storage and of course that's why we are getting fast read performance. So now if you are thinking which systems are best for ro
19:19:3019 hours, 19 minutes, 30 secondsstore index well the roster index is very suitable for the OLTB systems online transactional systems like
19:19:3719 hours, 19 minutes, 37 secondsbanking and commerce systems where the full records are accessed very frequently but in the other hand the column store index is great for OLAP.
19:19:4619 hours, 19 minutes, 46 secondsAll app systems are online analytical processing where you have like data warehouses, data league, business intelligence. You are building reports
19:19:5419 hours, 19 minutes, 54 secondsand analyzes. You have large data sets and very complicated aggregated queries.
19:20:0019 hours, 20 minutesSo if you have such a project then the column store index is the way to go. So that means the use case for the row store index if you have high frequency
19:20:0819 hours, 20 minutes, 8 secondstransactions where the system has to quickly access records and the use case for the column store is big data analytics where the SQL has to scan
19:20:1719 hours, 20 minutes, 17 secondslarge data sets. So those are the main differences between the row store index and the column store
19:20:2719 hours, 20 minutes, 27 secondsindex. All right. So now let's check the syntax of the column store index. Well,
19:20:3119 hours, 20 minutes, 31 secondsit is really easy what we're going to do. we can just put a column store keyword between the clustered or nonclustered and the index. So once you
19:20:4019 hours, 20 minutes, 40 secondsspecify that then you are telling SQL you want to create a column store index and the rest is going to stay as it is.
19:20:4619 hours, 20 minutes, 46 secondsNow if you want to create row column store then you don't have to specify anything. There is no keyword for the row store. So as we learned before we
19:20:5419 hours, 20 minutes, 54 secondscan go and create a nonclustered index and cluster on the index and both of those syntax is going to tell SQL we are creating row store index but if you go
19:21:0319 hours, 21 minutes, 3 secondsand use the column store keyword then you are telling SQL that you want to create either clustered or nclustered column store index and here there is
19:21:1119 hours, 21 minutes, 11 secondslike a syntax rule if you are creating a clustered column index then you must not specify anything for the columns. So you
19:21:1919 hours, 21 minutes, 19 secondscannot go and specify anything like an ID or country or any columns over here because it makes no sense once you say
19:21:2619 hours, 21 minutes, 26 secondscluster column store then all the columns going to be included in the new structure. So this is the syntax of the
