19:31:0119 hours, 31 minutes, 1 secondrow store clustered index. So that's true. column store index is consuming less space than the other type of
19:31:1219 hours, 31 minutes, 12 secondsindexes. All right. So now what is unique index? Unique index is a special type of indexes that going to make sure
19:31:1919 hours, 31 minutes, 19 secondsno duplicates in your data. And there are a couple of reasons why is it important to have a unique index. The first one and the most obvious reason is
19:31:2819 hours, 31 minutes, 28 secondsto have data integrity. So the unique index going to go and enforce uniqueness in your data and that is very helpful.
19:31:3519 hours, 31 minutes, 35 secondsFor example, if you have a column like an email address or a product ID. Having duplicate in such a columns can mess up
19:31:4319 hours, 31 minutes, 43 secondsyour data very badly. So having a unique index on a column like an email going to make sure there are no sneaky duplicates
19:31:5019 hours, 31 minutes, 50 secondsinside your data. And the second important reason why unique index is important is to improve the performance.
19:31:5719 hours, 31 minutes, 57 secondsSo for example, if you are searching for specific email, the SQL going to start searching for the email value and once the SQL find the value, the SQL will
19:32:0619 hours, 32 minutes, 6 secondsstop searching because we are sure that there is no duplicates in the data. So with that you are improving the performance of your queries. So if you
19:32:1419 hours, 32 minutes, 14 secondsare creating an index and you know this column is unique then make sure to make the index as unique index. So now if you
19:32:2119 hours, 32 minutes, 21 secondshave a look again to our clustered index where we have the B structure if you make this index as unique then you are
19:32:2819 hours, 32 minutes, 28 secondsgiving an extra task for the SQL that's going to go and make sure that all those ids of the customer going to be unique.
19:32:3719 hours, 32 minutes, 37 secondsSo SQL has to guarantee that there are no duplicates at all inside your data in the databases. So now since we are
19:32:4519 hours, 32 minutes, 45 secondsgiving SQL an extra task to prove the uniqueness of the data building the clustered index going to be little bit
19:32:5219 hours, 32 minutes, 52 secondsslower. So that means inserting new data writing data going to be slower as the normal clustered index. But now if you
19:33:0019 hours, 33 minutesare talking about the read performance the performance of our query it's going to be optimized a little bit faster than
19:33:0619 hours, 33 minutes, 6 secondsa normal clustered index. So again this tradeoff we are making writing data slower but we are gaining more speed on
19:33:1519 hours, 33 minutes, 15 secondsthe query performance. So this is what we mean with unique index. Okay. So let's keep extending the syntax of the index. So now in order to tell whether
19:33:2319 hours, 33 minutes, 23 secondsit is unique or not we can specify it exactly at the start. So we say create unique is just before the clustered or
19:33:3119 hours, 33 minutes, 31 secondsnonclustered and then afterward the cl store and nothing changed for the rest. So we can specify this keyword to TSQL,
19:33:3819 hours, 33 minutes, 38 secondsit should be unique. And if you don't write anything before the clustered index, it's going to be not unique. So
19:33:4519 hours, 33 minutes, 45 secondsfor example, this one says create an index. So we didn't specify anything here, duplicates are allowed in the
19:33:5219 hours, 33 minutes, 52 secondsindex. But if you go and specify a unique index, then the duplicates are not allowed. So it is very simple. Okay.
19:33:5919 hours, 33 minutes, 59 secondsSo now let's go and create unique cluster. Now let's go and target the table products. Let's go and first

19:36:2819 hours, 36 minutes, 28 secondsyou will get an error saying you cannot insert duplicates to this table because we have unique index. So as you can see
19:36:3519 hours, 36 minutes, 35 secondsthis index is now helping us and improving the quality of my table. So this is how we work with the unique
19:36:4219 hours, 36 minutes, 42 secondsindex in SQL. Okay. So now what is a filtered
19:36:4919 hours, 36 minutes, 49 secondsindex? A filtered index is a regular index but with a twist. It only includes rows that meet specific condition. So
19:36:5819 hours, 36 minutes, 58 secondslet's understand what this means. So again we have our nonclustered index and the B3 structure. So now at the leaf
19:37:0419 hours, 37 minutes, 4 secondsnodes we will get only the ids the data that fulfill a specific condition. So for example if we are saying we want
19:37:1219 hours, 37 minutes, 12 secondsonly the active customers this is the condition. So that means on the leaf nodes we will have only the customer ids
19:37:1919 hours, 37 minutes, 19 secondsthat are active and any inactive customer will not be included at all at the data page and at the nodes. So that
19:37:2719 hours, 37 minutes, 27 secondsmeans our B structure going to be little bit smaller as usual because we have less data included in the structure. So
19:37:3519 hours, 37 minutes, 35 secondsour index going to be smaller than the regular nclustered index. So now the question is why is it important to have a filtered index? Well the biggest
19:37:4419 hours, 37 minutes, 44 secondsbenefit is we going to have targeted optimizations. So for example if our analyzes always focuses on the active
19:37:5219 hours, 37 minutes, 52 secondsusers and the inactive users are totally unrelevant. So that means having only relevant subset of data in the index
19:38:0019 hours, 38 minutesgoing to make the whole index much smaller which leads to faster performance. So it's going to be faster
19:38:0719 hours, 38 minutes, 7 secondsto query this filtered B3 structure. So that means we are doing targeted optimizations and we are improving the
19:38:1519 hours, 38 minutes, 15 secondsquery performance. Now the second benefit if you think about the storage since the size of the B structure going to be smaller that means we're going to
19:38:2419 hours, 38 minutes, 24 secondsneed less storage space in order to store the index which is great thing if you have large tables in your database.
19:38:3219 hours, 38 minutes, 32 secondsSo the filter the index going to make the structure of the index smaller which going to improve the speed and the performance and as well reduce the storage that is needed for your index.
19:38:4319 hours, 38 minutes, 43 secondsOkay. So now let's check the syntax of the filtered index. It's very simple.
19:38:4619 hours, 38 minutes, 46 secondsIt's like any query you can go and add at the end of creating the index the wear clause and then the condition as you are doing in any select statements.
19:38:5619 hours, 38 minutes, 56 secondsBut the SQL server is very restrictive using this type of index. So you cannot use filtered index on a clustered index.
19:39:0519 hours, 39 minutes, 5 secondsSo it is only allowed for the nclustered index because it makes no sense. If you create a clustered index, the entire table should be reorganized and ordered.
19:39:1619 hours, 39 minutes, 16 secondsSo it will not work for only subset of data and as well you cannot create a filtered index on a column store. So it
19:39:2419 hours, 39 minutes, 24 secondsis only allowed if you are using row store but you can go and combine the unique index together with the filtered index. There's no restrictions. So it's
19:39:3319 hours, 39 minutes, 33 secondsgoing to be like this. Create unique nonclustered index on the table and then you specify the wear condition. So this is the syntax of the filtered index and we have these restrictions. All right.
19:39:4419 hours, 39 minutes, 44 secondsSo now let's say that we have the following query where we are selecting data from customers but always in our program or in our report we are selecting only the customers from USA.
19:39:5519 hours, 39 minutes, 55 secondsSo we have the following condition. It says where country equal to USA and execute. So this is the basics of many
19:40:0419 hours, 40 minutes, 4 secondsqueries that we have in our project and we are always filtering the customers based on the country. So in one query we are finding maybe the top customers and
19:40:1319 hours, 40 minutes, 13 secondsanother query we are finding the average of scores and so on. But we are always filtering the data like this where country equal to USA. So now since we

19:40:4719 hours, 40 minutes, 47 secondscolumn country like this. So if you do it like this SQL going to go and create a nclustered index for all customers not
19:40:5519 hours, 40 minutes, 55 secondsonly from USA but for everything. So even if the customers come from Germany which is not really necessary because in
19:41:0219 hours, 41 minutes, 2 secondsour project we only focus on the customers from USA. So instead of that we can go and include the wear condition
19:41:1119 hours, 41 minutes, 11 secondsinside our cluster. So it's very simple we're going to go and say where country equal to USA exactly like our query. So
19:41:2019 hours, 41 minutes, 20 secondsnow the index that's going to be built it will be focused and targeted only for subset of data only for the data that
19:41:2719 hours, 41 minutes, 27 secondsfulfill this condition. So now let's go and create our filtered index and it is working. Let's go and check our indexes
19:41:3519 hours, 41 minutes, 35 secondson the customers. So let's go to the indexes over here and refresh. Now we can see our index over here. It says it
19:41:4219 hours, 41 minutes, 42 secondsis not unique because we didn't define anything at the start. So duplicates are allowed of course which is what we defined here. And as well it is
19:41:5019 hours, 41 minutes, 50 secondsfiltered. So it doesn't contain all the rows from your table. It contains only the rows that fulfill our condition. So
19:41:5819 hours, 41 minutes, 58 secondsthat means now if I go and execute this query, the index going to be used because the rows of this query is
19:42:0519 hours, 42 minutes, 5 secondsincluded in the index. But if I go over here and say Germany and execute the query, it's going to be slower because
19:42:1219 hours, 42 minutes, 12 secondsall those rows inside the query is not part of our index. So this index will not be used at all in order to improve
19:42:2119 hours, 42 minutes, 21 secondsthe query. So this is how we work with the filtered index in SQL. All right. So now we're going to
19:42:3019 hours, 42 minutes, 30 secondssummarize and talk quickly about how to use the right index. So when to use which type? Let's start with the first one. We have the heap structure. So as
19:42:3819 hours, 42 minutes, 38 secondswe learned it is a table without any index. So in which scenario we don't have to use any indexes in case you want
19:42:4619 hours, 42 minutes, 46 secondsto have fast inserts. So if you want to have a fast write performance then don't take any index. So you stay with the
19:42:5319 hours, 42 minutes, 53 secondsdefault with the he structure of your table and we usually use it in not very important tables like the staging tables or temporary tables where we want to
19:43:0219 hours, 43 minutes, 2 secondsinsert the data fast and then get rid of the data later. So here there is no need to utilize any index. Now if you are
19:43:0919 hours, 43 minutes, 9 secondstalking about the clustered index, we usually use the clustered index for primary keys. It is even a default from
19:43:1619 hours, 43 minutes, 16 secondsthe database. If you create any primary keys, then SQL going to go and create a clustered index. So this is the main usage of the clustered index, you use it
19:43:2519 hours, 43 minutes, 25 secondsin the primary keys. And if there's like no primary key in your table, then you can go and pick another column where sorting the data is important like for
19:43:3319 hours, 43 minutes, 33 secondsexample a date column. So it could be a good candidate for your clustered index.
19:43:3719 hours, 43 minutes, 37 secondsNow moving on to another type we have the column store index. So when I said here clustered index I mean clustered row store index of course. But now the
19:43:4619 hours, 43 minutes, 46 secondsquestion is when do we use the column store index. If you have like big complex analytical queries where you are aggregating a lot of data doing data
19:43:5419 hours, 43 minutes, 54 secondsaggregations then go for the column store index because it going to give you amazing performance. And as well if you are struggling with the size of tables
19:44:0219 hours, 44 minutes, 2 secondsso if you have a super large table you can go and use the column store index because it can go and compress the data and reduce the size of the whole table.
19:44:1119 hours, 44 minutes, 11 secondsSo for those scenarios we use the column store index. So again for the row store clustered index we use it usually for the old TB systems where you have a lot
19:44:2019 hours, 44 minutes, 20 secondsof transactions and so on but for the column store we use it usually for the OLAP systems where you have a data warehouse reporting system business
19:44:2819 hours, 44 minutes, 28 secondsintelligence and so on. Now moving on to another type we have the nonclustered index. We usually use this index for non
19:44:3519 hours, 44 minutes, 35 secondsprimary key columns. So that means the rest of the columns of your tables could be candidate for the nonclustered index.
19:44:4119 hours, 44 minutes, 41 secondsAnd there are a lot of reasons why you would do that. For example, for the foreign keys or using it on the columns that are used in order to join two
19:44:5019 hours, 44 minutes, 50 secondstables and another place where you can use the nonclustered index for the columns that are used for the work clause. So there are like many scenarios
19:44:5819 hours, 44 minutes, 58 secondswhere we can use the nonclustered index but not for the primary keys. Now moving on to another type, we have the filtered index. We use it in order to target a
19:45:0719 hours, 45 minutes, 7 secondssubset of data. So if in our query and analyzes we are only focusing on a subset of data all time, it makes no
19:45:1419 hours, 45 minutes, 14 secondssense to have one big index for all data, we can use the filtered index to have focused index. And of course if the
19:45:2119 hours, 45 minutes, 21 secondssize of the index is a problem then you can use a filtered index in order to reduce the overall size of the storage of the index. And then to the last type
19:45:3019 hours, 45 minutes, 30 secondswe have the unique index. you can go and use the unique index in order to ensure data integrity of your table and as well it might prove slightly the performance
19:45:3919 hours, 45 minutes, 39 secondsof your query and that's because SQL has less task to do if the index is unique once SQL finds a match it going to skip
19:45:4619 hours, 45 minutes, 46 secondsthe search so this is a quick summary and guide on when to use which index type that usually help me finding the
19:45:5419 hours, 45 minutes, 54 secondsright index all right friends so now let's say that you have created your index ES in
19:46:0219 hours, 46 minutes, 2 secondsyour database and your query is optimized and you have fast performance but the job is not done yet.
19:46:1019 hours, 46 minutes, 10 secondsNo god no god please no no no no
19:46:1719 hours, 46 minutes, 17 secondsbecause over the time the indexes get fragmented outdated unused and this going to lead to a poor performance in
19:46:2419 hours, 46 minutes, 24 secondsyour queries and as well going to increase the storage costs and the overall performance of your database going to drop down. So indexes like

20:02:5920 hours, 2 minutes, 59 secondsdatabase. Okay. Okay. So now to the next step, we have to go and monitor the duplicates in indexing. If you are

20:04:0820 hours, 4 minutes, 8 secondstable. So it's very simple and makes sense. Let's go and execute the whole query. Now as you can see it is sorted by the table name and the column name
20:04:1620 hours, 4 minutes, 16 secondsand that's because we can find then easier the duplicate. So let's go and check the first table. So the country is part of this index where we have the

20:04:3520 hours, 4 minutes, 35 secondsSo this is of course bad thing. We have to go and decide now do we want it as a column store or row store. And if we check as well this table, we can find
20:04:4320 hours, 4 minutes, 43 secondsthe first name in two different clusters the same story. And that's because we were practicing and creating those indexes. And that's it. But now if you
20:04:5120 hours, 4 minutes, 51 secondshave like large schema and a lot of indexes, I would go and make like a flag in order to understand whether we have a duplicate or not. And that's by
20:04:5820 hours, 4 minutes, 58 secondscalculating the number of rows of unique table name and index name. And we can do that very easily using the window
20:05:0520 hours, 5 minutes, 5 secondsfunctions. So let's have new row. And we're going to go and use the function count since we want to find the number of rows over. Then we're going to go and
20:05:1420 hours, 5 minutes, 14 secondspartition by we need the table name and as well the column name. Our expectation of this
20:05:2220 hours, 5 minutes, 22 secondscolumn should be one. If we have more than one then there is an issue and that means the column is inside two different
20:05:3020 hours, 5 minutes, 30 secondsindexes. And now let's go and sort it by the column name and descending. So let's go and execute it. And now we have here
20:05:3820 hours, 5 minutes, 38 secondsa nice flag where we can see how many rows we have for a specific column in a table. So if it's one like those columns, they are fine. Those columns
20:05:4720 hours, 5 minutes, 47 secondsare involved only once in one index. But for the first four rows, we have here an issue because we count here two columns.
20:05:5420 hours, 5 minutes, 54 secondsThat means we have two indexes for the same column. So as you can see the query is very simple and with that we have a nice report about the duplicates of indexes inside our database.
20:06:0820 hours, 6 minutes, 8 secondsOkay, one more thing in order to maintain our indexes is by updating the statistics. The database engines usually
20:06:1520 hours, 6 minutes, 15 secondsuse statistics in order to understand which index should be used for our query. And if these statistics are not
20:06:2220 hours, 6 minutes, 22 secondsup to date, SQL going to make wrong decisions. So let's understand what this means. Now let's say that you have created a table and you start inserting
20:06:3020 hours, 6 minutes, 30 secondsdata to this new table. Now the database engine going to go and create your new table and insert the data. Behind the scenes the database engine going to go
20:06:3820 hours, 6 minutes, 38 secondsand create for your new table statistics. It's like metadata informations about your data and that's
20:06:4520 hours, 6 minutes, 45 secondslike a report or insights about your table where you can find a lot of informations like the number of rows
20:06:5220 hours, 6 minutes, 52 secondsthat distribution of values in a column and as well we can find the number of distinct values and histogram and
20:07:0020 hours, 7 minutespatterns and many other informations about your table. So now of course the question is why do we have those informations in the database? Now
20:07:0820 hours, 7 minutes, 8 secondsimagine that you are doing select from where what going to happen the database engine has to go and create an execution plan. We're going to learn about this
20:07:1720 hours, 7 minutes, 17 secondslater in details. It is just a road map on how to execute this query. So here for example in order to load the data from the table there are like different
20:07:2620 hours, 7 minutes, 26 secondsways on how to do it. So there is like a table scan, index scan, index seek. So that means the database engine has here three different ways on how to do it.
20:07:3620 hours, 7 minutes, 36 secondsAnd now in order for the database to decide which way to use, it's going to go and read the statistics of the table.
20:07:4320 hours, 7 minutes, 43 secondsSo it's going to go and collect informations. Okay, how many rows do we have? Are the informations are unique?
20:07:4920 hours, 7 minutes, 49 secondsHow is the distribution of the data and so on. And now based on those statistics and numbers, the database can now make a
20:07:5620 hours, 7 minutes, 56 secondsgood decision about which methods to use in order to load the data. So for example, here the index scan is the best
20:08:0320 hours, 8 minutes, 3 secondsway to load our table. So this is exactly why the database needs the statistics in order to make the correct decision and to use the correct index.
20:08:1320 hours, 8 minutes, 13 secondsSo now you might ask okay this is something internal for the database why do we have to care about it? Well there is an issue. Now for example in our
20:08:2120 hours, 8 minutes, 21 secondstable we have 50 rows and let's say that in the next day you went and inserted to this table like around 1 million row.
20:08:2820 hours, 8 minutes, 28 secondsNow the issue that could happen is that the statistics will not get updated about this table and the statistics can
20:08:3520 hours, 8 minutes, 35 secondsstill say that we have only 50 rows. So that means the statistics of this table is now outdated. And the big issue that
20:08:4320 hours, 8 minutes, 43 secondsonce you query this table, the SQL engine don't know at all about the 1 million row that you have inserted in the table because it's going to go and
20:08:5220 hours, 8 minutes, 52 secondsask the statistics and it's going to answer with only 50 rows and the database going to say okay this is very small table and let's maybe skip an
20:09:0020 hours, 9 minutesindex or something. So that means the database going to make wrong decisions because the statistics are outdated. And
20:09:0820 hours, 9 minutes, 8 secondsnow your task is to monitor those statistics and to keep updating them. So let's see how we can do that. Okay. So now the first thing that we have to do

20:14:0820 hours, 14 minutes, 8 secondsthe project that there will be in one day a lot of new incoming data. So we are doing some kind of data migrations.

20:14:2420 hours, 14 minutes, 24 seconds[Music]
20:14:2820 hours, 14 minutes, 28 secondsdatabase. Okay. Okay, so now moving on to the final task that I usually do in order to monitor and manage the indexes is to monitor the index fragmentations.
20:14:3720 hours, 14 minutes, 37 secondsOver the time as your data is inserted, updated, deleted into your tables,
20:14:4220 hours, 14 minutes, 42 secondsindexes can become fragmented. So what is fragmentation? It means like there is unused spaces in your databases and the
20:14:5020 hours, 14 minutes, 50 secondsdatabase is not filling them or your data is not anymore sorted correctly in the index and this of course leads to
20:14:5820 hours, 14 minutes, 58 secondsinefficient use of the storage and as well going to slow down your
20:15:0720 hours, 15 minutes, 7 secondsqueries and in SQL in order to get everything organized again we have two methods the first method is reorganize
20:15:1520 hours, 15 minutes, 15 secondsso it's going to go and def fragment the leaf level of the index in order to get it organized and sorted again with the
20:15:2220 hours, 15 minutes, 22 secondslogical order. So it is very light operation and it will not block the user from using your table. And the second method called rebuild this is
20:15:3120 hours, 15 minutes, 31 secondsheavyweight operation. It going to go and drop the whole index and recreate it from the scratch. And this means of
20:15:3920 hours, 15 minutes, 39 secondscourse not only the data going to get sorted again but as well the fragmentations inside your databases and the index going to be eliminated. So let's see how we can do that in SQL.
20:15:4920 hours, 15 minutes, 49 secondsOkay. So now back to our database and the first question that you have to ask do we have an issue with the fragmentations in our indexes. So we
20:15:5720 hours, 15 minutes, 57 secondshave to check the health of our indexes in the database. And in order to do that, we have again to go to the system metadata that we have and we're going to
20:16:0620 hours, 16 minutes, 6 secondscheck their dynamic management functions. So there is like a special functions in order to get an answer in the SQL server. Let's go and do that. So
20:16:1420 hours, 16 minutes, 14 secondswe're going to go and select star from the function. So it is sis dot so it's
20:16:1920 hours, 16 minutes, 19 secondsgoing to be sis dot dm db index physical states this one. And this is a function
20:16:2620 hours, 16 minutes, 26 secondsthat we have to pass few parameters. We will not go in details just follow me with this. So we have to give it the DB ID and a null another null and a third
20:16:3720 hours, 16 minutes, 37 secondsnull and the last one going to be limited. So we have to do it like this.
20:16:4120 hours, 16 minutes, 41 secondsSo let's go and query it. Now what do we find? We have the object ID. We have the index ID and few other informations but
20:16:4820 hours, 16 minutes, 48 secondsthe most important one is the average fragmentation in percent. So this columns gives us the degree of the fragmentations in a word index. If it is
20:16:5720 hours, 16 minutes, 57 secondszero then it is perfect. We have no fragmentation in the index and our index is very healthy. But if it is like 100
20:17:0520 hours, 17 minutes, 5 secondsthen that means it is completely out of order and we have to do something about it. And now you might say you know what I don't know which object it does and
20:17:1320 hours, 17 minutes, 13 secondswhich index. Well you have to go and join few tables like the cy.ts and cis.index in order to get those informations. So we have to go and do

20:17:4520 hours, 17 minutes, 45 secondshere different numbers. And here is my recommendations about the percentage. If the fragmentation is between like zero and 10 that means everything is like
20:17:5420 hours, 17 minutes, 54 secondsokay and you don't have to do anything about it. But if the percentage is between like 10 and 30 then here we have to do something about it. So here I
20:18:0320 hours, 18 minutes, 3 secondsrecommend to use the reorganize method in order to sort the data again correctly. But if you have more than 30%
20:18:1020 hours, 18 minutes, 10 secondsthen here my recommendation is to go and rebuild the whole index because not only the data is in wrong order but as well
20:18:1820 hours, 18 minutes, 18 secondsthere is a new spaces in your data page in the index. So you have to do something about it. So now let's go and imagine one of those indexes for example

20:20:2020 hours, 20 minutes, 20 secondsfragmentations and make sure you have healthy indexes. So with that you have learned how I manage and monitor the indexes once I create them and I really
20:20:2920 hours, 20 minutes, 29 secondsrecommend you to follow those steps. All right friends, so now let's
20:20:3620 hours, 20 minutes, 36 secondssay that you have a large complex analytical SQL query and it involves a lot of joins and aggregations and so on
20:20:4420 hours, 20 minutes, 44 secondsbut it is slow and of course you want to go and optimize the performance of your query by maybe using indexes. And now the big question is where exactly I'm
20:20:5420 hours, 20 minutes, 54 secondsgoing to go build this index on which table on which columns. So that means you have to understand where exactly the problem is. Is it by joining tables or
20:21:0220 hours, 21 minutes, 2 secondssorting data or by the aggregations? Now in order to answer all those questions we have something called execution plan.
20:21:1020 hours, 21 minutes, 10 secondsSo what is that? The execution plan going to show you how the database exactly process your query step by step.
20:21:1920 hours, 21 minutes, 19 secondsAnd this is what we need. It's going to show us where exactly we have a performance issue. So in other words,
20:21:2520 hours, 21 minutes, 25 secondsthe execution plan it's like your window on how the SQL database thinks and once you understand that then you're going to
20:21:3320 hours, 21 minutes, 33 secondsmake a right decision on building an index. So let's understand exactly what this means. Okay. So now let's imagine that you are doing a query like
20:21:4120 hours, 21 minutes, 41 secondsselecting from table and then joining the data with another table. So now once you execute this query the database engine will not go immediately and start
20:21:4920 hours, 21 minutes, 49 secondsfetching data from the disk but instead of that first the SQL has to make a plan. So it's like you are planning a
20:21:5620 hours, 21 minutes, 56 secondstrip where you check the Google map in order to find the best route in order to reach the destination and the execution
20:22:0320 hours, 22 minutes, 3 secondsplan is exactly the same thing. The database has first to plan how to execute your query and it's going to build this plan step by step based on your query and as well the statistics.
20:22:1520 hours, 22 minutes, 15 secondsSo the first step for example how to get the data from the tables and there are like multiple ways like scan index or
20:22:2220 hours, 22 minutes, 22 secondsfull table scan and then after that it need to decide which type of joins going to be done like is it hash join or a loop join and then at the end of this
20:22:3020 hours, 22 minutes, 30 secondsplan it's going to be the select statements. So once the execution plan is ready the database engine going to start implementing the steps. So it's
20:22:3820 hours, 22 minutes, 38 secondsgoing to go and start reading your tables for example from the disk and then after that it's going to join the tables and then select the columns and
20:22:4520 hours, 22 minutes, 45 secondssend at the end the results to the end user. And now once everything is done the database engine going to do one more thing where it's going to go and take
20:22:5320 hours, 22 minutes, 53 secondsthis execution plan and store it at the cache. And that's because the database engine can go and reuse this plan if we
20:23:0220 hours, 23 minutes, 2 secondshave a similar query. So for example, if you go and execute the same query again,
20:23:0620 hours, 23 minutes, 6 secondsthe database engine here going to understand ah this is the same query. We have already built an execution plan for that. So it going to go and check the
20:23:1520 hours, 23 minutes, 15 secondscache and it is way faster to get it immediately from the cache instead of building it. So in this scenario, the database engine doesn't have to make any
20:23:2220 hours, 23 minutes, 22 secondsdecisions or something like that. going to go and get the plan from the cache and start immediately by executing the plan. And of course, the database engine
20:23:3120 hours, 23 minutes, 31 secondswill not hide the execution plan from the users. You can go and check it because you can go and check how the database loaded the data, how they are
20:23:3920 hours, 23 minutes, 39 secondsjoined and so on. And then you can make a correct decision on how to optimize your query maybe by adding indexes. So let's go back to SQL and see how we can do that.

20:31:3320 hours, 31 minutes, 33 secondsclustered index is only sorted and SQL don't have to go and sort the data again. So it doesn't have to go and sort
20:31:4120 hours, 31 minutes, 41 secondsanything. The data is already sorted. So this is the first win that you have if you have an index. So everything is
20:31:4820 hours, 31 minutes, 48 secondsalready sorted and if you have an order by on this column then SQL don't have to do it during the query. So now if you want to go and compare the cost you can
20:31:5620 hours, 31 minutes, 56 secondssee here we still have the same cost for the CPU and the input output in the h structure without any index we have here

20:32:1220 hours, 32 minutes, 12 secondsclustered but as well on top of it we have high cost for sorting the data. So we are consuming more CPU and input
20:32:1920 hours, 32 minutes, 19 secondsoutput. And if you summarize those cost of course this query going to be slower and bad compared to the clustered index.

20:45:5620 hours, 45 minutes, 56 secondsnot. All right friends, so so far we have learned that the SQL server going to make its own decisions on how to
20:46:0320 hours, 46 minutes, 3 secondsexecute your queries and the SQL make those plans based on the statistics. But sometimes the plan that you are getting from the database might be not the best
20:46:1220 hours, 46 minutes, 12 secondsone for your query and there could be many reasons why this could happen.
20:46:1620 hours, 46 minutes, 16 secondsMaybe the statistics are not up to date or you have a lot of indexes and the database engine get confused and here
20:46:2420 hours, 46 minutes, 24 secondsexactly where we need the SQL hints. So you can use the SQL hints in order to command to force the SQL database on how
20:46:3420 hours, 46 minutes, 34 secondsexactly your SQL query should be executed. So you can intervene and change the steps in the execution plan.
20:46:4120 hours, 46 minutes, 41 secondsSo let's see how we can do that. All right. So now let's have a very simple query. We are just joining the table orders with the customers and we are showing like few columns. Now if you go
20:46:5020 hours, 46 minutes, 50 secondsand execute it and we go and check the execution plan, we can see in this plan that it is using the clustered index in order to read the data from the orders
20:46:5920 hours, 46 minutes, 59 secondsand the customers and then it is using the nested loop in order to do the joins. Now let's say that our tables are really big but still the SQL is using
20:47:0820 hours, 47 minutes, 8 secondsthe nested loops and of course this is not good for large tables and maybe the SQL was confused with the indexes and
20:47:1520 hours, 47 minutes, 15 secondsstatistics and so on and it decided to use the nested loops. So now in order to force the SQL to use another type of
20:47:2320 hours, 47 minutes, 23 secondsjoin, we can go and give a hint in our query for the SQL to use different types for the join. So let's go and do that.
20:47:3120 hours, 47 minutes, 31 secondsWe're going to go at the end of our query and we're going to say option and inside it we're going to say use the
20:47:3820 hours, 47 minutes, 38 secondshash join like this. So that's it. This is our query and at the end we are giving the database a hint for the
20:47:4620 hours, 47 minutes, 46 secondsexecution plan. So let's go and try that out. So let's check the execution plan.
20:47:5020 hours, 47 minutes, 50 secondsAnd now as you can see is using different type of join. So with that we are intervening in the execution plan
20:47:5820 hours, 47 minutes, 58 secondsand we are making choices. So with that we have changed the technicality on how the SQL is joining those two tables. All
20:48:0520 hours, 48 minutes, 5 secondsright. So now let's go and change something else like for example instead of having index scan I would like to have an index seek. So if you have the
20:48:1420 hours, 48 minutes, 14 secondsright index in your table, you can go and tell SQL how to read your data in the table. So let's go and do that.
20:48:2220 hours, 48 minutes, 22 secondsCurrently here we have an index scan on the table customers. So we can go over here near the table and we're going to
20:48:2920 hours, 48 minutes, 29 secondssay with and inside it we're going to say for SQL force seek. So we are forcing SQL to use the seek index. So we
20:48:3720 hours, 48 minutes, 37 secondscan use those keywords near the table in order to specify for SQL how to load the data. If you are not specifying anything
20:48:4520 hours, 48 minutes, 45 secondslike here with the orders, we don't have here any hints. That means we are counting on the execution plan that is
20:48:5220 hours, 48 minutes, 52 secondsgenerated from the SQL. But if you don't want the recommendations, you can go and specify which one should be used. So now
20:49:0020 hours, 49 minuteslet's go and execute it. Now we got an error because the SQL is not able to process what we are asking for and I
20:49:0820 hours, 49 minutes, 8 secondsthink maybe we are using the force command and as well the hash join. Let me just uncomment this and let's go and give it another try and now it is
20:49:1620 hours, 49 minutes, 16 secondsworking. So let's go to the execution plan. So you can see we got again the nested loop. And now if you go to the customers table you can see now it is
20:49:2420 hours, 49 minutes, 24 secondsusing the index seek. So it is not using anymore the index scan. So as you can see again we are intervening and forcing
20:49:3120 hours, 49 minutes, 31 secondsSQL to use the method that might be better for our query. Now if you are creating a lot of indexes in one table
20:49:3820 hours, 49 minutes, 38 secondsand the SQL is still not targeting the right index. So if you check the object you can see it is targeting specific
20:49:4520 hours, 49 minutes, 45 secondsindex. But if you have a better index than that you can give a hint for the SQL to use a specific index. And we can
20:49:5320 hours, 49 minutes, 53 secondsdo that like this. If you go over here and remove the force seek and you say use index and then we have to go and
20:50:0120 hours, 50 minutes, 1 secondspecify the index name. So let's go and get again the primary key over here. Now I'm telling SQL you have to go and use
20:50:0820 hours, 50 minutes, 8 secondsthis index in order to scan the table customers. So let's go and try this out.
20:50:1320 hours, 50 minutes, 13 secondsAnd if you go to the execution plan you can see it is as well targeting this index. So not only you can force SQL for a specific type of loading or joining,
20:50:2520 hours, 50 minutes, 25 secondsyou can force SQL to use a specific index that you created. All right friends, so as you can see, SQL hands are very powerful, but we have to be
20:50:3320 hours, 50 minutes, 33 secondsvery careful with them because I really had a bad experience using them in my projects. So here are my recommendations and what happens. So what could happen
20:50:4120 hours, 50 minutes, 41 secondsis that you are optimizing the performance in the development database and you start using the hints and the speed was really good and once you roll
20:50:5020 hours, 50 minutes, 50 secondsthat out to another database the production database this hint will not be working correctly. The same hint that
20:50:5820 hours, 50 minutes, 58 secondsyou are using might not improve the performance and one reason is that sometimes the productive database has
20:51:0520 hours, 51 minutes, 5 secondslike large data compared to the development database. So you have really to test the hint in each database that
20:51:1220 hours, 51 minutes, 12 secondsyou have. So if your hint is working in one environment that doesn't mean it going to work in the other one. So always make sure to test. And the second
20:51:2120 hours, 51 minutes, 21 secondsrecommendation is that don't use the hint as a permanent fix for your queries. So what this means? Let's say that you are working in the project and one of your queries are very slow. Now,
20:51:3120 hours, 51 minutes, 31 secondsif it's not clear why the execution plan is really bad, you can go and use the hints as a workaround in order to speed
20:51:3820 hours, 51 minutes, 38 secondsup your query again, but it's still as a workaround temporary. You still have to invest and spend time in order to
20:51:4520 hours, 51 minutes, 45 secondsanalyze what was the road cause. So maybe it is an old statistics or you have wrong indexing and so on. So use
20:51:5220 hours, 51 minutes, 52 secondshints only to work around and speed up your queries, but don't use it as a permanent fix. So friends, SQL hints are
20:52:0020 hours, 52 minutesreally amazing in order to control the execution plan, but use it very carefully and only if there is like an emergency.
20:52:1120 hours, 52 minutes, 11 secondsAll right friends, so now for each SQL data project, we have to make sure that we create a clear guidance about the
20:52:1920 hours, 52 minutes, 19 secondsindex strategy and everyone in the team has to commit and follow the strategy in order to make sure that each index that
20:52:2720 hours, 52 minutes, 27 secondsis created in the project to fulfill a purpose and that's because without a clear strategy about the indexing, I'm going to promise you there will be a lot
20:52:3520 hours, 52 minutes, 35 secondsof redundancy, unused indexes, uh waste of storage and the whole system of your project is going to be slow and bad. So
20:52:4320 hours, 52 minutes, 43 secondsnow what we're going to do, I'm going to show you my indexing strategy that I usually follow in my projects. But I'm going to tell you from now there is like not one strategy that can fit any
20:52:5220 hours, 52 minutes, 52 secondsproject and any scenario. That's why the team of each project should brainstorm in order to make their own strategy. So now let's have a look to my indexing strategy.
20:53:0520 hours, 53 minutes, 5 secondsAnd now if I have to pick only one recommendation from me to you in this indexing tutorial, I'm going to have this advice for you. Avoid overindexing.
20:53:1520 hours, 53 minutes, 15 secondsOverindexing is the biggest mistake and trap that a lot of developers do where they think adding more indexes. That
20:53:2320 hours, 53 minutes, 23 secondssounds like we are speeding up things and our queries can be fast. But I have to tell you this exactly lead to the opposite. And here's why. As we learned,
20:53:3320 hours, 53 minutes, 33 secondseach time you add a new data to your table, your index has to get updated,
20:53:3720 hours, 53 minutes, 37 secondssorted, rearranged. That means having too many indexes, what's going to happen? Your insert, update, delete operations going to be slow. And this
20:53:4620 hours, 53 minutes, 46 secondsmeans your database is slower and not faster. And one more very important reason why overindexing is bad is you
20:53:5420 hours, 53 minutes, 54 secondsmake the database confused while creating the execution plan. As we learned, the SQL database has to create the best execution plan for your query.
20:54:0420 hours, 54 minutes, 4 secondsAnd if you have a lot of indexes in your database, it's going to make the process of creating an execution plan complicated for the database, which
20:54:1220 hours, 54 minutes, 12 secondsmakes it of course for database harder to choose the best path and index. And as well, you open the door for bad
20:54:1920 hours, 54 minutes, 19 secondsexecution plans. And this means it's going to slow the query because first the database has to create the execution plan before executing your query. So
20:54:2720 hours, 54 minutes, 27 secondsagain it has a bad effect for the performance and as well there is another bad thing. It can make it harder for the database to decide what is the best
20:54:3620 hours, 54 minutes, 36 secondsexecution plan for a query and having too many indexes might make the SQL database choosing a really bad execution
20:54:4420 hours, 54 minutes, 44 secondsplan. So overindexing confuse the execution plan and as well makes the query slower. So that's why I call this
20:54:5120 hours, 54 minutes, 51 secondsa golden rule and you have to commit to it. Just avoid overindexing because it is double-edged sword and exactly you
20:54:5820 hours, 54 minutes, 58 secondshave to have the mindset of less is more. So having a few effective indexes is way better than having a lot of
20:55:0620 hours, 55 minutes, 6 secondsindexes. So keep it in mind and write it in your development guideline for the team with big statement avoid
20:55:1320 hours, 55 minutes, 13 secondsoverindexing. So this is the first statement in your indexing strategy. So now let's check the
20:55:1820 hours, 55 minutes, 18 seconds[Music]
20:55:2120 hours, 55 minutes, 21 secondsrest. All right. So now we can split the indexing strategy into four phases and each phase has multiple steps. So now
20:55:2920 hours, 55 minutes, 29 secondsthe first step is we're going to go and create an initial indexing strategy. So now once you start a new SQL project you have to define the objectives of the
20:55:3820 hours, 55 minutes, 38 secondsprojects very clearly. So that means we have to make it clear what we are focusing on what we want to achieve and in order to define the goal of your
20:55:4620 hours, 55 minutes, 46 secondsindexing strategy you have to understand your system. We have mainly two types of databases. In one hand we have OLAB
20:55:5420 hours, 55 minutes, 54 secondsdatabases. It stands for online analytical processing. The purpose of this database is for data analytics and an example for that is the data
20:56:0220 hours, 56 minutes, 2 secondswarehouse. So in data warehousing we go and extract the data from multiple sources and then we prepare it and transform it and put it in one big
20:56:1120 hours, 56 minutes, 11 secondsstorage and we call this process an ETL process. And then the front end we have like reports and dashboards where the
20:56:1820 hours, 56 minutes, 18 secondsdata is summarized and aggregated and presented for the end user. And these reports could be used from users in order to analyze and have insights about
20:56:2620 hours, 56 minutes, 26 secondsthe data. And now in order to generate those reports there will be like heavy reading on the data warehouse database.
20:56:3420 hours, 56 minutes, 34 secondsSo that means there will be huge queries that's going to access the database in order to aggregate and prepare the data for the visualization. But now in the
20:56:4220 hours, 56 minutes, 42 secondsother hand we have the OLTP systems online transactional processing. It is like an e-commerce finance banking where
20:56:5020 hours, 56 minutes, 50 secondsyou have at the back end a database where the data is stored and on the front end we have like an applications for the end users. So now as the users
20:56:5820 hours, 56 minutes, 58 secondsare interacting with the app this can cause write operations on the database.
20:57:0320 hours, 57 minutes, 3 secondsSo inserting new data or changing data and as well there will be read operations on the database in order to show the data in the app. So we have
20:57:1120 hours, 57 minutes, 11 secondsboth write and read. So now of course we have to ask ourself what is the goal what do we want to achieve and here mainly there is like two strategy either
20:57:1920 hours, 57 minutes, 19 secondsyou want to improve the read performance or the right performance. Now if you are looking to the OLAP system here it's really you have to understand the
20:57:2720 hours, 57 minutes, 27 secondsproject where is the struggle sometimes it could be like the ATL process itself it's slow and mainly the ATL is writing
20:57:3420 hours, 57 minutes, 34 secondsdata from the sources in the data warehouse and maybe you have scenario where it takes like every day 10 hours and 10 hours is of course a problem
20:57:4220 hours, 57 minutes, 42 secondsbecause you cannot wait so long in order to get a new data fresh data to the report every day. So you can make the
20:57:4920 hours, 57 minutes, 49 secondsgoal of the project is to optimize the right performance. You want to speed up the ETL. But actually most of those
20:57:5620 hours, 57 minutes, 56 secondsprojects having another issue. Well, it is the read operation on the database because data warehouses normally have
20:58:0320 hours, 58 minutes, 3 secondsreally big data sets and at the front end the reports generate large complex queries on the database. So that means
20:58:1120 hours, 58 minutes, 11 secondsthe rate process going to be the pain point in each OLAP system. So normally the big goal in each OLAP system going
20:58:1820 hours, 58 minutes, 18 secondsto be how to optimize the read performance. But now in the right hand with the OLTB we have different nature

20:58:3420 hours, 58 minutes, 34 secondshappening between the application and the database. So you're going to have like massive amount of read and write transactions. So the whole time we are
20:58:4320 hours, 58 minutes, 43 secondsreading, writing, reading, writing and so on. But with the OL app we have like something bigger and slower because in the ATL we usually run it only once.

20:59:0020 hours, 59 minutesreadrs all time. Again depend on the project but usually the main pain point in the OLTP is the right operation. So
20:59:0820 hours, 59 minutes, 8 secondsit could be like this. If you are building OTP system, the main goal is to optimize the right performance. Now of course the question is how to do that?
20:59:1720 hours, 59 minutes, 17 secondsHow we going to optimize that? Well,

20:59:3420 hours, 59 minutes, 34 secondsreally big tables in the database and each time they are used in order to build a report and the report going to

20:59:5020 hours, 59 minutes, 50 secondsnow of course you have to answer now the question which type of index should we use in this scenario. Well we have a perfect one called a column store index.

21:00:1521 hours, 15 secondscompletely different at the right side here. We're going to have like a lot of tables and they have like different sizes and so on and there are like a lot
21:00:2321 hours, 23 secondsof relationship between all those tables. So it is completely connected.

21:09:0321 hours, 9 minutes, 3 secondslast step we don't have to forget about monitoring the fragmentations as we learned over the time as you are doing modifications on the tables. What could

21:09:1721 hours, 9 minutes, 17 secondshave like fragmentations in the index and the same thing we have to monitor the fragmentations of each tables and here if the percentage is between 0 and
21:09:2621 hours, 9 minutes, 26 seconds10 then there is no issue but if the fragmentation is between 10 and 30 then we have to go and reorganize the index
21:09:3321 hours, 9 minutes, 33 secondsand if it's more than 30 then this is alerting you have to go and rebuild the whole index and usually for the

21:10:5321 hours, 10 minutes, 53 secondsone there is another important techniques on how to optimize the performance. So we're going to talk about the partitions. So how to divide our data in order to optimize the
21:11:0221 hours, 11 minutes, 2 secondsperformance. So let's go. All right. So what is SQL
21:11:0921 hours, 11 minutes, 9 secondspartitioning? It's a technique in order to divide a large table into small pieces and each piece we call it a partition. Well, this sounds like we are
21:11:1821 hours, 11 minutes, 18 secondsdividing one big table into smaller tables but it's not like that. We are just dividing one table into smaller partitions. So we going to see it in the
21:11:2721 hours, 11 minutes, 27 secondsdatabase still as one solid table but behind the scenes it is splitted into multiple partitions. So now let's go and
21:11:3421 hours, 11 minutes, 34 secondsunderstand what this means. Okay. So now let's say that you have a table at your database and over the time this table is getting bigger and bigger where you have
21:11:4321 hours, 11 minutes, 43 secondslike hundreds of millions of rows. Now once you have such a big table what's going to happen everything going to be slow. So for example, if you are reading
21:11:5021 hours, 11 minutes, 50 secondsthe table and the execution plan is doing full scan of the table, this can take SQL long time until all the rows
21:11:5721 hours, 11 minutes, 57 secondsare fetched. And if you decide to make like an index for this table, what's going to happen? SQL going to go and build a very big B tree index where
21:12:0621 hours, 12 minutes, 6 secondsthere are a lot of branches and files and so on. And having a big index is not always a good thing because if you do
21:12:1321 hours, 12 minutes, 13 secondsoperations like delete rows, update rows or inserting rows, these operations going to need long time to process. So
21:12:2121 hours, 12 minutes, 21 secondshaving a big index doesn't mean that you can have a good performance for your big table. So that means having a big table is a problematic because everything
21:12:3021 hours, 12 minutes, 30 secondsgoing to be slow. So now what we can do in order to optimize the performance of this big table? Well, we can use SQL
21:12:3821 hours, 12 minutes, 38 secondspartitioning and in order to do that, we have to understand the behavior and the transactions that are happening on our
21:12:4521 hours, 12 minutes, 45 secondstable and what usually happen with that the table grows over the time. So, you can have like subset of data that belongs to 2023 and another one that is
21:12:5421 hours, 12 minutes, 54 secondscreated and updated in 2024 and then you have something like more current in 2025. So that means we have like in our
21:13:0221 hours, 13 minutes, 2 secondstable old data and as well new data and we usually interact with the new data more often than the old data. So maybe
21:13:1021 hours, 13 minutes, 10 secondsfor example for 2023 there is like only one read transaction and for the data in 2024 we have done like two reads and one
21:13:1821 hours, 13 minutes, 18 secondsrights. So it is little bit more than 2023 but for the new data for the current year there will be heavy transactions. So we're going to have a
21:13:2721 hours, 13 minutes, 27 secondslot of reads a lot of rights. We are updating, inserting, reading. So a lot of things are going on for the new data.
21:13:3321 hours, 13 minutes, 33 secondsSo that means we are accessing frequently the big table only to interact with the new data and we rarely need the old data. So what we can do, we
21:13:4221 hours, 13 minutes, 42 secondscan go and divide this big table and we usually divide it by like a date. So that means we can go and split this table by the year and we put each year
21:13:5221 hours, 13 minutes, 52 secondsin one partition. So at the end we're going to have like three partitions. And now it's really important to understand that that those are three partitions.
21:13:5921 hours, 13 minutes, 59 secondsThey are not three tables. So that means at the client side the users can see only one table but behind the scenes we
21:14:0721 hours, 14 minutes, 7 secondshave like three partitions. Now let's say that you have a query in order to read the data from 2025. And now what going to happen? SQL will not go and
21:14:1421 hours, 14 minutes, 14 secondsscan all the data from the table. It's going to go and only target one partition the 2025. So that means SQL is
21:14:2221 hours, 14 minutes, 22 secondsonly scanning the relevant informations the relevant partition and not the entire table. And now we have another benefits of having partitions. Let's say
21:14:3021 hours, 14 minutes, 30 secondsthat you're using a modern database and normally they support parallel processing. So if you have the infrastructure for that what can happen
21:14:3721 hours, 14 minutes, 37 secondsthe database engine can process each partition independently and parallelly.
21:14:4321 hours, 14 minutes, 43 secondsSo whether you are reading or writing data. So what's going to happen? SQL going to process your queries parallelly which of course can reduce the overall
21:14:5221 hours, 14 minutes, 52 secondsexecution time. So that means if you have a modern infrastructure like maybe for example the Azure Synapse and so on go with the partitions because the
21:15:0021 hours, 15 minutespartition then could be stored in different servers and this helps of course the SQL engine to use all the resources at once. So that means
21:15:0921 hours, 15 minutes, 9 secondspartitions allow scalability and as well parallel processing. partitions going to make the indexing more efficient. So
21:15:1721 hours, 15 minutes, 17 secondsinstead of having one very big index for the whole table, if you put an index on a partition table, what's going to happen? Each partition going to get its
21:15:2621 hours, 15 minutes, 26 secondsown index, which means the size of the indexes going to be smaller. And of course, this helps a lot with searching
21:15:3321 hours, 15 minutes, 33 secondsfor data or as well extending the index itself. So for example, if you are inserting data to the partition 2025,
21:15:4021 hours, 15 minutes, 40 secondsthe SQL will not go and change anything on the other indexes, it's going to go and only change the index of the partition 2025. So that you can see the
21:15:4821 hours, 15 minutes, 48 secondspower of the partitioning. It improves significantly the performance of your table whether you are reading or writing data to this big table. So this is what
21:15:5821 hours, 15 minutes, 58 secondswe mean with partitioning and why we need it. All right, friends. So now we're
21:16:0521 hours, 16 minutes, 5 secondsgoing to go to the process of creating partitions in SQL. At the start it might sounds a little bit complicated but we're going to do it step by step and I
21:16:1321 hours, 16 minutes, 13 secondshave a sketch for that. So we have like four steps because we have in the database like multiple layers. So let's see how we can do that. Let's go. So the
21:16:2221 hours, 16 minutes, 22 secondsfirst step is that we're going to go and define the partition function. So what is that? We're going to go and define here in the function the logic on how to
21:16:3021 hours, 16 minutes, 30 secondsdivide the table into partitions. And this can be based on the partition key.
21:16:3521 hours, 16 minutes, 35 secondsSo that means we need a column in order to define the logic. And we usually use columns with the dates like for example the order dates or in other scenarios we can use the region or country and so on.
21:16:4621 hours, 16 minutes, 46 secondsBut the most famous one is the dates and that's because our tables like get bigger over the time and there are like multiple types of functions. We're going
21:16:5521 hours, 16 minutes, 55 secondsto focus on the range function. So how it going to work? We're going to have like a range of dates and then we have to define like boundary values and let's
21:17:0321 hours, 17 minutes, 3 secondssay that I would like to make a partition for each year and in order to do that we have to define the partition boundary. So it is like a value the
21:17:1221 hours, 17 minutes, 12 secondsboundary of the years could be like the first day of the year or the last day of the year. So here in this example we're going to take for the boundary the last
21:17:2021 hours, 17 minutes, 20 secondsday of the year. So the last day of 2023, 2024 and 2025. So we call those
21:17:2621 hours, 17 minutes, 26 secondsvalues the boundary of our function. Now between the boundaries we going to have our partitions. So for examples all the
21:17:3421 hours, 17 minutes, 34 secondsrows for 2025 and earlier years is going to be the partition one. So between the boundary and everything before is one
21:17:4221 hours, 17 minutes, 42 secondspartition and after that between the two boundaries we have partition two. So this partition going to be for all rows of 2024. And then we have another
21:17:5121 hours, 17 minutes, 51 secondssection the partition three where we have all rows of 2025 and then between the last boundary and everything onwards
21:17:5921 hours, 17 minutes, 59 secondsis going to be partition 4 and here we're going to have all the rows from 2026 onward. So with that we have now a logic we are telling SQL how to divide
21:18:0821 hours, 18 minutes, 8 secondsour data into multiple partitions and here there is like two methods the left and the right. So what are those two
21:18:1521 hours, 18 minutes, 15 secondsmethods? So again we have our boundary and now the big question to which partition does this boundary belongs to
21:18:2221 hours, 18 minutes, 22 secondsis it partition one or partition two and that's why we have those two methods. If you say it is left that mean the boundary belongs to the partition number
21:18:3021 hours, 18 minutes, 30 secondsone. But in the other hand if you say it is right then the boundary going to be part and belongs to the partition number two. So you have to decide whether the
21:18:3921 hours, 18 minutes, 39 secondsboundaries belongs to the left partition or to the right partition. And with that in the partition one, we're going to have all the rows of 2023 including the
21:18:4721 hours, 18 minutes, 47 secondslast day of 2023 because in the partition 2 we only focus on 2024. So it's just the boundary belongs to the
21:18:5621 hours, 18 minutes, 56 secondsleft partition. It's very simple. Now let's go and implement that in SQL. So let's do it. The syntax is very simple.
21:19:0221 hours, 19 minutes, 2 secondsWe're going to say create partition function and then we have to give it a name. So it's going to be
21:19:0921 hours, 19 minutes, 9 secondspartition by year since we are dividing the data by the year. And after that we have to define the data type. So we are
21:19:1721 hours, 19 minutes, 17 secondssplitting the data by a date. So it's going to be date. And after that we have to define the partition function type.
21:19:2421 hours, 19 minutes, 24 secondsSo in our example we are using the range. And now we have to define whether it is left or right. We're going to stick with the left. And now comes the
21:19:3321 hours, 19 minutes, 33 secondsvery important step. We have to define the boundaries. So we're going to say for values and we're going to enter here
21:19:4121 hours, 19 minutes, 41 secondsthree boundaries like in our example for each year we're going to define a date.
21:19:4521 hours, 19 minutes, 45 secondsSo 2023 and the last day of the year. Same goes for
21:19:5221 hours, 19 minutes, 52 seconds2024 and for the last one 2025. So with that we have defined the
21:19:5921 hours, 19 minutes, 59 secondslogic the range we have defined the boundaries and we tell SQL the boundaries are a date. So let's go and
21:20:0621 hours, 20 minutes, 6 secondsexecute our function. Okay, so that's it. As you can see, it's very simple. We just created a function that split the data by the date using the range lift.
21:20:1621 hours, 20 minutes, 16 secondsAnd of course, this function is not yet attached to any tables or anything. It is just a logic that is stored in the database. All right. So now since our
21:20:2421 hours, 20 minutes, 24 secondspartition function is stored inside the database, we will have metadata about those functions stored in the system schema. So we have there a dedicated
21:20:3321 hours, 20 minutes, 33 secondstable called partition functions and there we're going to find informations about all functions that we have inside our database. So let's go and execute
21:20:4121 hours, 20 minutes, 41 secondsit. And as you can see we find now our new created partition function. So partition by year it is a range and it

21:20:5421 hours, 20 minutes, 54 secondsMaybe you have already one in the projects. Okay. Okay. So now let's check
21:21:0121 hours, 21 minutes, 1 secondthe next step in our process. We're going to go and build now the file groups. So what is a file group? It is
21:21:0821 hours, 21 minutes, 8 secondslike a logical container of one or more data files. So it's very simple. It's like folders. We're going to go and
21:21:1521 hours, 21 minutes, 15 secondscreate now like multiple folders. So later we can insert inside them files.
21:21:2021 hours, 21 minutes, 20 secondsAnd this is really nice because it gives us like freedom and flexibility where we can go and decide how the data files are organized for each partition. So what we
21:21:3021 hours, 21 minutes, 30 secondsusually do, we go and create for each partition a file group. So we're going to have like four folders or four file groups for 2023, 2024 and so on. So now

21:43:0221 hours, 43 minutes, 2 secondsqueries and this has a great impact on big tables. The number of resources and the number of reads going to be reduced
21:43:0921 hours, 43 minutes, 9 secondsmassively. All right my friends. So that's all about the partitions in SQL.
21:43:1321 hours, 43 minutes, 13 secondsIt is amazing and you can use it as well not only in databases but as well in many other data platforms and tools where you always can divide your data in
21:43:2121 hours, 43 minutes, 21 secondsorder to optimize the performance. Now in the next step what I have prepared for you after 15 years working in real projects using SQL. I have a lot of best
21:43:2921 hours, 43 minutes, 29 secondspractices and tips for you. So I have collected everything that I know and now I'm going to show you the best practices and tips and tricks that I can give you
21:43:3621 hours, 43 minutes, 36 secondsin order to optimize the performance in SQL. So let's go.
21:43:4421 hours, 43 minutes, 44 secondsAnd now before we deep dive into the 30 best practices, I'm going to give you the golden rule. The SQL optimizer responds differently for different sizes
21:43:5321 hours, 43 minutes, 53 secondsof tables. So that means if you have small and medium tables like hundred of thousands, you might not notice any performance differences if you are
21:44:0021 hours, 44 minutesfollowing the best practices. And that's because the size of the data is small.
21:44:0421 hours, 44 minutes, 4 secondsBut if you have like million or hundred of millions of records in tables, you will immediately notice how things can be faster if you follow the best practices. And here is my golden rule.
21:44:1421 hours, 44 minutes, 14 secondsIf you get any best practice from me or let's say you are reading something in the internet, always you have to test using the execution plan. So for
21:44:2221 hours, 44 minutes, 22 secondsexample, if you have like two queries are returning the same result of the data, I'm going to recommend you here to check the execution plan. And if you
21:44:2921 hours, 44 minutes, 29 secondsnotice there is no differences between them in the execution plan then pick the one that you see it is easier to read and to understand because sometimes if
21:44:3821 hours, 44 minutes, 38 secondsyou are following the best practices for the performance your query might be like little bit more complicated. So always write the query to be understandable and
21:44:4721 hours, 44 minutes, 47 secondsonly optimize it if you notice it is slow. So the golden rule here is always test. If you find you are optimizing the
21:44:5421 hours, 44 minutes, 54 secondsperformance with the new query then pick that and if there is no gain in the performance then focus on making your queries readable. So this is the golden
21:45:0321 hours, 45 minutes, 3 secondsrule always test test test using execution plan. So let's deep dive into best practices and we're going to start
21:45:0921 hours, 45 minutes, 9 secondsby optimizing the performance of our queries. All right let's start with the
21:45:1721 hours, 45 minutes, 17 secondseasy stuff. The first step is select only what you need. What I usually see in many queries is that the developers just go and select all the columns from
21:45:2521 hours, 45 minutes, 25 secondsone table and I can tell you I cannot think of one scenario where you need all the columns of one table in one query.
21:45:3121 hours, 45 minutes, 31 secondsSo for sure in the result we will get like unnecessary columns and of course reading unnecessary informations going to make your query slower. So this is
21:45:4021 hours, 45 minutes, 40 secondsusually a bad practice. Don't use select star but instead of that go list all the columns that you need for your query. So
21:45:4721 hours, 45 minutes, 47 secondsmake sure that you only select what you need. Don't go and select all the columns from one table and with that you don't risk reading unnecessary informations from the database. So
21:45:5621 hours, 45 minutes, 56 secondsalways make sure that you select exactly what you need for a query don't go with a star. Okay. Tip number two avoid unnecessary distinct and order by. I

21:46:1221 hours, 46 minutes, 12 secondsand order by for each query. And as we review the code and discuss it with the developer, we see that we really don't need to remove any duplicates in the

21:46:2821 hours, 46 minutes, 28 secondssituations there is no need to sort the data at all. And those operations, the distinct removing the duplicate and sorting the data, they are very

21:53:3921 hours, 53 minutes, 39 secondsand outer join. And if we talk about the performance, the best performance you will get from the inner join. And that's because SQL going to work only on the
21:53:4721 hours, 53 minutes, 47 secondsmatching rows. That means the effort and the processing time is better than the other joins. Now in the next one in ranking we have the left and right
21:53:5621 hours, 53 minutes, 56 secondsjoins. They are slightly slower than the inner join because usually they process more data and more rows than the inner
21:54:0321 hours, 54 minutes, 3 secondsjoin because SQL will work not only with the matching rows as well with the unmatching rows. So for right and left SQL has to do more stuff than the inner
21:54:1221 hours, 54 minutes, 12 secondsjoin. And now the worst type of joins we have the outer join. And that and that's because this type works with the biggest
21:54:2021 hours, 54 minutes, 20 secondsnumber of rows compared to the other types. It's going to present unmatching rows from the left and from the right tables. So that means SQL has a lot of
21:54:2821 hours, 54 minutes, 28 secondsto-do and that's why this join has the worst performance. So here my advice is always try to use the inner join if it's
21:54:3621 hours, 54 minutes, 36 secondsenough to work with the matching rows and if the matching rows is not enough then go with the lift join maybe. But try your best always to bring the inner
21:54:4421 hours, 54 minutes, 44 secondsjoin instead of lift join. But don't forget inner join filters the data.

21:55:1021 hours, 55 minutes, 10 secondsfor this scenario, it's very simple. But if you have like a complex query, then joining table like this might be very confusing and really hard to read and as

22:11:2522 hours, 11 minutes, 25 secondsin your query. This happens a lot if you have a lot of sub queries and if you analyze it you might find sometimes there is like redundancy. So for example

22:12:4822 hours, 12 minutes, 48 secondshigher then write above average. If it's lower then below average. So as you can see it is easier to read and it is smaller and the performance here is way
22:12:5622 hours, 12 minutes, 56 secondsbetter than reading four times the employees and repeating the same logic.
