23:22:3323 hours, 22 minutes, 33 secondsbefore we deep dive into the tools and the cool stuff, we have first to have good understanding about what is exactly data warehouse why the companies try to
23:22:4223 hours, 22 minutes, 42 secondsbuild such a data management system. So now the question is what is a data warehouse? I will just use the definition of the father of the data
23:22:4923 hours, 22 minutes, 49 secondswarehouse bill in a data warehouse is subject-oriented integrated time variant and nonvolatile collection of data
23:22:5723 hours, 22 minutes, 57 secondsdesigned to support the management's decision-making process. Okay, I I know that might be confusing.
23:23:0323 hours, 23 minutes, 3 secondsSubject-oriented it means that the warehouses always focus on a business area like the sales, customers, finance and so on. Integrated because it goes and integrate multiple source systems.
23:23:1323 hours, 23 minutes, 13 secondsUsually you build a warehouse not only for one source but for multiple sources.
23:23:1823 hours, 23 minutes, 18 secondsTime variance it means you can keep historical data inside the data warehouse. Nonvolatile it means once the data enter the data warehouse it is not
23:23:2623 hours, 23 minutes, 26 secondsdeleted or modified. So this is how build inmon defined data warehouse.
23:23:3123 hours, 23 minutes, 31 secondsOkay. So now I'm going to show you the scenario where your company don't have a real data management. So now let's say that you have one system and you have like one data analyst has to go to this
23:23:4023 hours, 23 minutes, 40 secondssystem and start collecting and extracting the data and then he going to spend days and sometimes weeks transforming the raw data into something
23:23:4823 hours, 23 minutes, 48 secondsmeaningful. Then once they have the reports they're going to go and share it. And this data analyst is sharing the report using an Excel. And then you have
23:23:5623 hours, 23 minutes, 56 secondslike another source of data and you have another data analyst that she is doing maybe the same steps collecting the data spending a lot of time transforming the
23:24:0423 hours, 24 minutes, 4 secondsdata and then share at the end like a report and this time she is sharing the data using PowerPoint and a third system and the same story but this time he is
23:24:1223 hours, 24 minutes, 12 secondssharing the data using maybe PowerBI. So now if the company works like this then there is a lot of issues. First this process it take two way long. I saw a
23:24:2223 hours, 24 minutes, 22 secondslot of scenarios where sometimes it takes weeks and even months until the employee manually generating those reports. And of course, what can happen
23:24:2923 hours, 24 minutes, 29 secondsfor the users? They are consuming multiple reports with multiple state of the data. One report is 40 days old,
23:24:3623 hours, 24 minutes, 36 secondsanother one 10 days and a third one is like 5 days. So it's going to be really hard to make a real decision based on this structure. A manual process is
23:24:4523 hours, 24 minutes, 45 secondsalways slow and stressful and the more employees you involved in the process the more you open the door for human errors and errors of course in reports
23:24:5323 hours, 24 minutes, 53 secondsleads to bad decisions and another issue of course is handling the big data. If one of your sources generating like
23:25:0123 hours, 25 minutes, 1 secondmassive amount of data then the data analyst going to struggle collecting the data and maybe in some scenarios it will not be anymore possible to get the data.
23:25:0923 hours, 25 minutes, 9 secondsSo the whole process can breaks and you cannot generate anymore fresh data for specific reports. And one last very big issue with that. If one of your
23:25:1723 hours, 25 minutes, 17 secondsstakeholders asks for an integrated report from multiple sources, well good luck with that because merging all those data manually is very chaotic,
23:25:2723 hours, 25 minutes, 27 secondstime-conuming and full of risk. So this is just a picture. If a company is working without a proper data management, without a data leak, data
23:25:3623 hours, 25 minutes, 36 secondswarehouse, data lake houses. So in order to make real and good decisions, you need data management. So now let's talk about the scenario of a data warehouse.
23:25:4423 hours, 25 minutes, 44 secondsSo the first thing that's going to happen is that you will not have your data team collecting manually the data.
23:25:5123 hours, 25 minutes, 51 secondsYou're going to have a very important component called ETL. ETL stands for extract, transform and load. It is a
23:25:5823 hours, 25 minutes, 58 secondsprocess that you do in order to extract the data from the sources and then apply multiple transformations on those sources and at the end it loads the data
23:26:0623 hours, 26 minutes, 6 secondsto the data warehouse and this one going to be the single point of truth for analyzes and reporting and it is called data warehouse. So now what can happen
23:26:1523 hours, 26 minutes, 15 secondsall your reports going to be consuming this single point of truth. So with that you create your multiple reports and as
23:26:2223 hours, 26 minutes, 22 secondswell you can create integrated reports from multiple sources not only from one single source. So now by looking to the right side it looks already organized
23:26:3123 hours, 26 minutes, 31 secondsright and the whole process is completely automated. There is no more manual steps which of course it reduces the human error and as well it is pretty
23:26:4023 hours, 26 minutes, 40 secondsfast. So usually you can load the data from the sources until the reports in matter of hours or sometimes in minutes.
23:26:4823 hours, 26 minutes, 48 secondsSo there is no need to wait like weeks and months in order to refresh anything.
23:26:5223 hours, 26 minutes, 52 secondsAnd of course the big advantage is that the data warehouse itself it is completely integrated. So that means it goes and bring all those sources
23:27:0023 hours, 27 minutestogether in one place which makes it really easier for reporting and not only integrated you can build in the data warehouse as well history. So we have
23:27:0923 hours, 27 minutes, 9 secondsnow the possibility to access historical data and what is also amazing is that all those reports having the same data
23:27:1623 hours, 27 minutes, 16 secondsstatus. So all those reports can have the same status maybe sometimes one day old or something. And of course if you have a modern data warehouse in cloud
23:27:2423 hours, 27 minutes, 24 secondsplatforms you can really easily handle any big data sources. So no need to panic if one of your sources is
23:27:3123 hours, 27 minutes, 31 secondsdelivering massive amount of data. And of course in order to build the data warehouse you need different types of developers. So usually the one that
23:27:3823 hours, 27 minutes, 38 secondsbuilds the ETL component and the data warehouse is the data engineer. So they are the one that is accessing the
23:27:4523 hours, 27 minutes, 45 secondssources, scripting the ATLs and building the database for the data warehouse. And now for the other part, the one that is responsible for that is the data
23:27:5423 hours, 27 minutes, 54 secondsanalyst. They are the one that is consuming the data warehouse, building different data models and reports and sharing it with the stakeholders. So
23:28:0323 hours, 28 minutes, 3 secondsthey are usually contacting the stakeholders, understanding the requirements and building multiple reports based on the data warehouse. So now if you have a look to those two
23:28:1123 hours, 28 minutes, 11 secondsscenarios, this is exactly why we need data management. Your data team is not wasting time and fighting with the data.
23:28:2023 hours, 28 minutes, 20 secondsThey are now more organized and more focused and with like a data warehouse and you are delivering professional and fresh reports that your company can
23:28:2923 hours, 28 minutes, 29 secondscount on in order to make good and fast decisions. So this is why you need a data management like a data warehouse.
23:28:3623 hours, 28 minutes, 36 secondsThink about data warehouse as a busy restaurant. Every day different suppliers bring in fresh ingredients, vegetables, spices, meat, you name it.
23:28:4423 hours, 28 minutes, 44 secondsThey don't just use it immediately and throw everything in one pot, right? They clean it, shop it, and organize everything and store each ingredients in the right place, fridge or freezer. So,
23:28:5523 hours, 28 minutes, 55 secondsthis is the preparing phase. And when the order comes in, they quickly grab the prepared ingredients and create a perfect dish and then serve it to the
23:29:0423 hours, 29 minutes, 4 secondscustomers of the restaurant. And this process is exactly like the data warehouse process. It is like the kitchen where the raw ingredients, your
23:29:1123 hours, 29 minutes, 11 secondsdata are cleaned, sorted and stored. And when you need a report or analyzes, it is ready to serve up exactly like what
23:29:1923 hours, 29 minutes, 19 secondsyou need. Okay. So now we're going to zoom in and focus on the component ETL. If
23:29:2723 hours, 29 minutes, 27 secondsyou are building such a project, you're going to spend almost 90% just building this component, the ETL. So it is the core element of the data warehouse and I
23:29:3623 hours, 29 minutes, 36 secondswant you to have a clear understanding what is exactly an ETL. So our data exist in a source system. And now what
23:29:4323 hours, 29 minutes, 43 secondswe want to do is is to get our data from the source and move it to the target.
23:29:4823 hours, 29 minutes, 48 secondsSource and target could be like database tables. So now the first step that we have to do is to specify which data we have to load from the source. Of course
23:29:5623 hours, 29 minutes, 56 secondswe can say that we want to load everything but let's say that we are doing incremental loads. So we're going to go and specify a subset of the data
23:30:0323 hours, 30 minutes, 3 secondsfrom the source in order to prepare it and load it later to the target. So this step in the ATL process we call it extract. We are just identifying the
23:30:1223 hours, 30 minutes, 12 secondsdata that we need. We pull it out and we don't change anything. It's going to be like one to one like the source system.
23:30:1823 hours, 30 minutes, 18 secondsSo the extract has only one task to identify the data that we have to pull out from the source and to not change anything. So we will not manipulate the
23:30:2623 hours, 30 minutes, 26 secondsdata at all. It can stay as it is. So this is the first step in the ETL process, the extract. Now moving on to the stage number two. We're going to
23:30:3523 hours, 30 minutes, 35 secondstake this extract data and we will do some manipulations, transformations and we're going to change the shape of those
23:30:4223 hours, 30 minutes, 42 secondsdata. And this process is really heavy working. We can do a lot of stuff like data cleansing, data integration and a lot of formatting and data
23:30:5123 hours, 30 minutes, 51 secondsnormalizations. So a lot of stuff we can do in this step. So this is the second step in the ETL process, the transformation. We're going to take the
23:30:5823 hours, 30 minutes, 58 secondsoriginal data and reshape it, transform it into exactly the format that we need into a new format and shapes that we need for analyzes and reporting. Now,
23:31:0823 hours, 31 minutes, 8 secondsfinally, we get to the last step in the ATL process. We have the load. So, in this step, we're going to take this new data and we're going to insert it into
23:31:1623 hours, 31 minutes, 16 secondsthe target. So, it is very simple. We're going to take this prepared data from the transformation step and we're going to move it into its final destination,
23:31:2423 hours, 31 minutes, 24 secondsthe target like for example data warehouse. So that's ETL in a nutshell.
23:31:2923 hours, 31 minutes, 29 secondsFirst extract the raw data, then transform it into something meaningful and finally load it to a target where it's going to make a difference. So
23:31:3723 hours, 31 minutes, 37 secondsthat's it. This is what we mean with the ETL process. Now in real projects, we don't have like only source and targets.
23:31:4323 hours, 31 minutes, 43 secondsOur data architecture going to have like multiple layers depend on your design whether you are building a warehouse or a data lake or a data warehouse. And
23:31:5223 hours, 31 minutes, 52 secondsusually there are like different ways on how to load the data between all those layers. And in order now to load the data from one layer to another one there
23:31:5923 hours, 31 minutes, 59 secondsare like multiple ways on how to use the ATL process. So usually if you are loading the data from the source to the layer number one like only extract the
23:32:0723 hours, 32 minutes, 7 secondsdata from the source and load it directly to the layer number one without doing any transformations because I want to see the data as it is in the first
23:32:1623 hours, 32 minutes, 16 secondslayer. And now between the layer number one and the layer number two you might go and use the full ETL. So we're going to extract from the layer one, transform
23:32:2423 hours, 32 minutes, 24 secondsit and then load it to the layer number two. So with that we are using the whole process the ATL. And now between layer two and layer three we can do only
23:32:3223 hours, 32 minutes, 32 secondstransformation and then load. So we don't have to deal with how to extract the data because it is maybe using the same technology and we are taking all
23:32:4023 hours, 32 minutes, 40 secondsdata from layer 2 to layer three. So we transform the whole layer 2 and then load it to layer three. And now between three and four you can use only the LM.
23:32:4923 hours, 32 minutes, 49 secondsSo maybe it's something like duplicating and replicating the data and then you are doing the transformation. So you load to the new layer and then transform
23:32:5823 hours, 32 minutes, 58 secondsit. Of course, this is not a real scenario. I'm just showing you that in order to move from source to a target,
23:33:0323 hours, 33 minutes, 3 secondsyou don't have always to use a complete ETL. Depend on the design of your data architecture. You might use only few components from the ETL. Okay. So this is how ETL looks like in real projects.
23:33:1623 hours, 33 minutes, 16 secondsOkay. So now I would like to show you an overview of the different techniques and methods in the ETLs. We have wide range of possibilities where you have to make
23:33:2423 hours, 33 minutes, 24 secondsdecisions on which one you want to apply to your projects. So let's start first with the extraction. The first thing that I want to show you is we have
23:33:3123 hours, 33 minutes, 31 secondsdifferent methods of extraction. Either you are going to the source system and pulling the data from the source or the source system is pushing the data to the
23:33:4023 hours, 33 minutes, 40 secondsdata warehouse. So those are the two main methods on how to extract data. And then we have in the extraction two types. We have a full extraction
23:33:4823 hours, 33 minutes, 48 secondseverything all the records from tables and every day we load all the data to the data warehouse or we make more smarter one where we say we're going to
23:33:5523 hours, 33 minutes, 55 secondsdo an incremental extraction where every day we're going to identify only the new changing data. So we don't have to load the whole thing only the new data we go
23:34:0423 hours, 34 minutes, 4 secondsextract it and then load it to the data warehouse. And in data extraction we have different techniques. The first one is like manually where someone has to
23:34:1123 hours, 34 minutes, 11 secondsaccess a source system and extract the data manually or we connect ourselves to a database and we have then a query in order to extract the data or we have a
23:34:2023 hours, 34 minutes, 20 secondsfile that we have to parse it to the data warehouse or another technique is to connect ourself to API and do their calls in order to extract the data or if
23:34:2923 hours, 34 minutes, 29 secondsthe data is available in streaming like in CFKA we can do eventbased streaming in order to extract the data. Another
23:34:3623 hours, 34 minutes, 36 secondsway is to use the change data capture CDC is as well something very similar to streaming or another way is by using web
23:34:4423 hours, 34 minutes, 44 secondsscrabbing where you have a code that going to run and extract all the informations from the web. So those are the different techniques and types that
23:34:5123 hours, 34 minutes, 51 secondswe have in the extraction. Now if you are talking on the transformation there are wide range of different transformations that we can do on our
23:34:5923 hours, 34 minutes, 59 secondsdata like for example doing data enrichment where we add values to our data sets or we do a data integration where we have multiple sources and we
23:35:0723 hours, 35 minutes, 7 secondsbring everything to one data model or we derive new columns based on already existing one. Another type of data transformations we have the data
23:35:1523 hours, 35 minutes, 15 secondsnormalization. So the sources has values that are like a code and you go and map it to more friendly values for the
23:35:2223 hours, 35 minutes, 22 secondsanalyzers which is more easier to understand and to use. Another transformations we have the business rules and logic depend on the business
23:35:3023 hours, 35 minutes, 30 secondsyou can define different criterias in order to build like new columns. And what belongs to transformations is the data aggregation. So here we aggregate
23:35:3823 hours, 35 minutes, 38 secondsthe data to a different granularity and then we have type of transformation called data cleansing. There are many different ways on how to clean our data.
23:35:4723 hours, 35 minutes, 47 secondsFor example, removing the duplicates,
23:35:4923 hours, 35 minutes, 49 secondsdoing data filtering, handling the missing data, handling invalid values or removing unwanted spaces, casting the
23:35:5623 hours, 35 minutes, 56 secondsdata types and detecting the outliers and many more. So we have different types of data cleansing that we can do in our data warehouse and this is very
23:36:0623 hours, 36 minutes, 6 secondsimportant transformation. So as you can see we have different types of transformations that we can do in our data warehouse. Now moving on to the
23:36:1323 hours, 36 minutes, 13 secondsload. So what do we have over here? We have different processing types. So either we are doing patch processing or stream processing. Patch processing
23:36:2223 hours, 36 minutes, 22 secondsmeans we are loading the data warehouse in one big patch of data that's going to run and load the data warehouse. So it
23:36:2923 hours, 36 minutes, 29 secondsis only one time job in order to refresh the content of the data warehouse and as well the reports. So that means we are scheduling the data warehouse in order
23:36:3723 hours, 36 minutes, 37 secondsto load it in the day once or twice. And the other type we have the stream processing. So this means if there is like a change in the source system,
23:36:4423 hours, 36 minutes, 44 secondswe're going to process this change as soon as possible. So we're going to process it through all the layers of the data warehouse once something changes
23:36:5223 hours, 36 minutes, 52 secondsfrom the source system. So we are streaming the data in order to have real time data warehouse which is very challenging things to do in data
23:36:5923 hours, 36 minutes, 59 secondswarehousing. And if you are talking about the loads we have two methods either we are doing a full load or incremental load. It's the same thing as
23:37:0723 hours, 37 minutes, 7 secondsextraction right? So for the full load in databases there are like different methods on how to do it like for example we truncate and then insert that means
23:37:1523 hours, 37 minutes, 15 secondswe make the table completely empty and then we insert everything from the scratch or another one you are doing an update insert we call it upsert. So we
23:37:2423 hours, 37 minutes, 24 secondscan go and update all the records and then insert the new one and another way is to drop create and insert. So that means we drop the whole table and then
23:37:3223 hours, 37 minutes, 32 secondswe create it from scratch and then we insert the data. It is very similar to the truncate but here we are as well removing and dropping the whole table.
23:37:4023 hours, 37 minutes, 40 secondsSo those are the different methods of full loads. The incremental load we can use as well the upserts. So update and insert. So we're going to do an update
23:37:4823 hours, 37 minutes, 48 secondsor insert statements to our tables. Or if the source is something like a log,
23:37:5223 hours, 37 minutes, 52 secondswe can do only insert. So we can go and append the data always to the table without having to update anything.
23:37:5823 hours, 37 minutes, 58 secondsAnother way to do incremental load is to do a merge. And here it is very similar to the upsert but as well with a delete.
23:38:0423 hours, 38 minutes, 4 secondsSo update, insert, delete. So those are the different methods on how to load the data to your tables. And one more thing in data warehousing, we have something
23:38:1223 hours, 38 minutes, 12 secondscalled slowly changing dimensions. So here it's all about the historicizations of your table. And there are many different ways on how to handle the
23:38:2023 hours, 38 minutes, 20 secondshistoriizations in your table. The first type is sedd0. We say there is notoriizations and nothing should be changed at all. So that means you are
23:38:2823 hours, 38 minutes, 28 secondsnot going to update anything. The second one which is more famous, it is the sedd one. you are doing an overwrite. So that
23:38:3523 hours, 38 minutes, 35 secondsmeans you are updating the records with the new informations from the source system by overwriting the old value. So we are doing something like the upsert.
23:38:4323 hours, 38 minutes, 43 secondsSo update and insert but you are losing of course history. Another one we have the sedd2 and here you want to add historiizations to your table. So what
23:38:5123 hours, 38 minutes, 51 secondswe do each change that we get from the source system that means we are inserting new records and we are not going to overwrite or delete the old
23:38:5923 hours, 38 minutes, 59 secondsdata. we are just going to make it inactive and the new record going to be active one. So there are different methods on how to do historiizations as
23:39:0723 hours, 39 minutes, 7 secondswell while you are loading the data to the data warehouse. All right. So those are the different types and techniques that you might encounter in data

23:50:2723 hours, 50 minutes, 27 secondslet's start with the first epic. We have the requirements analyzes. Now analyzing the requirement,
23:50:3623 hours, 50 minutes, 36 secondsit is very important to understand which type of data warehouse you're going to go and build because there is like not only one standard on how to build it.
23:50:4423 hours, 50 minutes, 44 secondsAnd if you go blindly implementing the data warehouse, you might be doing a lot of stuff that is totally unnecessary and you will be burning a lot of time. So
23:50:5323 hours, 50 minutes, 53 secondsthat's why you have to sit with the stakeholders with the department and understand what we exactly have to build and depend on the requirements you design the shape of the data warehouse.
23:51:0323 hours, 51 minutes, 3 secondsSo now let's go and analyze the requirement of this project. Now the whole project is splitted into two main sections. The first section we have to
23:51:1023 hours, 51 minutes, 10 secondsgo and build a data warehouse. So this is a data engineering task and we will go and develop ETLs and data warehouse.
23:51:1723 hours, 51 minutes, 17 secondsAnd once we have done that we have to go and build analytics and reporting business intelligence. So we're going to do data analyszis. But now first we will
23:51:2623 hours, 51 minutes, 26 secondsbe focusing on the first part building the data warehouse. So what do we have here? The statement is very simple. It says develop a modern data warehouse
23:51:3523 hours, 51 minutes, 35 secondsusing SQL server to consolidate sales data enabling analytical reporting and informed decision making. So this is the
23:51:4323 hours, 51 minutes, 43 secondsmain statements and then we have specifications. The first one is about the data sources. It says import data from two source systems ERB and CRM and
23:51:5223 hours, 51 minutes, 52 secondsthey are provided as CSV files. And now the second task is talking about the data quality. We have to clean and fix
23:51:5923 hours, 51 minutes, 59 secondsdata quality issues before we do the data analyzers because let's be real there is no raw data that is perfect is
23:52:0623 hours, 52 minutes, 6 secondsalways messy and we have to clean that up. Now the next task is talking about the integration. So it says we have to go and combine both of the sources into
23:52:1523 hours, 52 minutes, 15 secondsone single userfriendly data model that is designed for analytics and reporting.
23:52:2123 hours, 52 minutes, 21 secondsSo that means we have to go and merge those two sources into one single data model. And now we have here another specifications. It says focus on the
23:52:2923 hours, 52 minutes, 29 secondslatest data sets. So there is no need for historiization. So that means we don't have to go and build histories in the database. And the final requirement
23:52:3623 hours, 52 minutes, 36 secondsis talking about the documentation. So it says provide clear documentations of the data model. So that means the last product of the data warehouse to support
23:52:4523 hours, 52 minutes, 45 secondsthe business users and the analytical teams. So that means we have to generate a manual that's going to help the users that makes lives easier for the
23:52:5323 hours, 52 minutes, 53 secondsconsumers of our data. So as you can see maybe this is very generic requirements but it has a lot of informations already for you. So it's saying that we have to
23:53:0123 hours, 53 minutes, 1 seconduse the platform SQL server. We have two source systems using the CSV files and it sounds that we really have a bad data quality in the sources and as well it
23:53:1023 hours, 53 minutes, 10 secondswants us to focus on building completely new data model that is designed for reporting and it says we don't have to
23:53:1823 hours, 53 minutes, 18 secondsdo historiization and it is expected from us to generate documentations of the system. So these are the requirements for the data engineering
23:53:2623 hours, 53 minutes, 26 secondspart where we're going to go and build a data warehouse that fulfill these requirements. All right. Right. So with that we have analyzed the requirements
23:53:3323 hours, 53 minutes, 33 secondsand as well we have closed the first easiest ebick. So we are done with this.
23:53:3823 hours, 53 minutes, 38 secondsLet's go and close it. And now let's open another one. Here we have to design the data architecture and the first task is to choose data management approach.
23:53:4723 hours, 53 minutes, 47 secondsSo let's go. Now designing the data architecture it is exactly like building a house. So
23:53:5623 hours, 53 minutes, 56 secondsbefore construction starts, an architect's going to go and design a plan, a blueprint for the house. How the rooms will be connected, how to make the house functional, safe and wonderful.
23:54:0723 hours, 54 minutes, 7 secondsAnd without this blueprint from the architects, the builders might create something unstable, inefficient or maybe unlivable. The same goes for data
23:54:1523 hours, 54 minutes, 15 secondsprojects. A data architect is like a house architecture. They design how your data will flow, integrate and be accessed. So as data architects we make
23:54:2323 hours, 54 minutes, 23 secondssure that the data warehouse is not only functioning but also scalable and easy to maintain. And this is exactly what we will do now. We will play the role of
23:54:3123 hours, 54 minutes, 31 secondsthe data architect and we will start brainstorming and designing the architecture of the data warehouse. So now I'm going to show you a sketch in order to understand what are the
23:54:4023 hours, 54 minutes, 40 secondsdifferent approaches in order to design a data architecture. And this phase of the projects usually is very exciting for me because this is my main role in
23:54:4823 hours, 54 minutes, 48 secondsdata projects. I am a data architect and I discuss a lot of different projects where we try to find out the best design for the projects. All right. So now
23:54:5623 hours, 54 minutes, 56 secondslet's go. Now the first step of building a data architecture is to make a very
23:55:0423 hours, 55 minutes, 4 secondsimportant decision to choose between four major types. The first approach is to build a data warehouse. It is very suitable if you have only structured
23:55:1323 hours, 55 minutes, 13 secondsdata and your business want to build solid foundations for reporting and business intelligence. And another approach is to build a data leak. This
23:55:2223 hours, 55 minutes, 22 secondsone is way more flexible than a data warehouse where you can store not only structured data but as well semi and unstructured data. We usually use this
23:55:3123 hours, 55 minutes, 31 secondsapproach if you have mixed types of data like database tables, logs, images,
23:55:3523 hours, 55 minutes, 35 secondsvideos and your business want to focus not only on reporting but as well on advanced analytics or machine learning but it's not that organized like a data
23:55:4423 hours, 55 minutes, 44 secondswarehouse and data leaks if it's too much unorganized and turns into data swamp and this is where we need the next approach. So the next one we can go and
23:55:5323 hours, 55 minutes, 53 secondsbuild data lakehouse. So it is like a mix between data warehouse and data lake. You get the flexibility of having
23:56:0023 hours, 56 minutesdifferent types of data from the data lake but you still want to structure and organize your data like we do in the data warehouse. So you mix those two
23:56:0723 hours, 56 minutes, 7 secondswords into one and this is a very modern way on how to build that architecture and this is currently my favorite way of building data management system. Now the
23:56:1623 hours, 56 minutes, 16 secondslast and very recent approach is to build data mesh. So this is a little bit different. Instead of having centralized data management system the idea now in
23:56:2423 hours, 56 minutes, 24 secondsthe data mesh is to make it decentralized. You cannot have like one centralized data management system because always if you say centralized
23:56:3123 hours, 56 minutes, 31 secondsthen it means bottleneck. So instead you have multiple departments and multiple domains where each one of them is building a data product and sharing it
23:56:3923 hours, 56 minutes, 39 secondswith others. So now you have to go and pick one of those approaches and in this project we will be focusing on the data warehouse. So now the question is how to
23:56:4723 hours, 56 minutes, 47 secondsbuild the data warehouse. Well there is as well four different approaches on how to build it. The first one is the enimmon approach. So again you have your
23:56:5523 hours, 56 minutes, 55 secondssources and the first layer you start with the staging where the row data is landing and then the next layer you organize your data in something called
23:57:0323 hours, 57 minutes, 3 secondsenterprise data warehouse where you go and model the data using the third normal format. It's about like how to
23:57:1023 hours, 57 minutes, 10 secondsstructure and normalize your tables. So you are building a new integrated data model from the multiple sources. And then we go to the third layer. It's
23:57:1823 hours, 57 minutes, 18 secondscalled the data marts where you go and take like small subset of the data warehouse and you design it in a way that is ready to be consumed from
23:57:2723 hours, 57 minutes, 27 secondsreporting and it focus on only one topic like for example the customers sales or products and after that you go and
23:57:3423 hours, 57 minutes, 34 secondsconnect your BI tool like PowerBI or Tableau to the data marts. So with that you have three layers to prepare the data before reporting. Now moving on to
23:57:4223 hours, 57 minutes, 42 secondsthe next one we have the Kimple approach. He says you know what building this enterprise data warehouse it is wasting a lot of time. So what we can do
23:57:5023 hours, 57 minutes, 50 secondswe can jump immediately from the stage layer to the final data because building this enterprise data warehouse it is a
23:57:5823 hours, 57 minutes, 58 secondsbig struggle and usually waste a lot of time. So he always want you to focus and building the data ms quickly as possible. So it is faster approach than
23:58:0623 hours, 58 minutes, 6 secondsin but with the time you might get chaos in the data MS cuz you are not always focusing in the big picture and you might be repeating same transformations and integrations in different data ms.
23:58:1723 hours, 58 minutes, 17 secondsSo there is like trade-off between the speed and consistent data warehouse. Now moving on to the third approach we have the data vault. So we still have the
23:58:2523 hours, 58 minutes, 25 secondsstage and the data marts but it says we still need this central data warehouse in the middle but this middle layer we're going to bring more standards and
23:58:3423 hours, 58 minutes, 34 secondsrules. So it tells you to split this middle layer into two layers the row vault and the business vault. In the row
23:58:4123 hours, 58 minutes, 41 secondsvault you have the original data but in the business vault you have all the business rules and transformations that prepares the data for the data marks. So
23:58:4923 hours, 58 minutes, 49 secondsthat vault it is very similar to the inmon but it brings more standards and rules to the middle layer. Now I'm going to go and add a fourth one that I'm
23:58:5823 hours, 58 minutes, 58 secondsgoing to call it medallion architecture and this one is my favorite one because it is very easy to understand and to build. So it says you're going to go and
23:59:0623 hours, 59 minutes, 6 secondsbuild three layers bronze, silver and gold. The bronze layer it is very similar to the stage but we have understood with the time that the stage
23:59:1423 hours, 59 minutes, 14 secondslayer is very important because having the original data as it is it going to helps a lot by traceability and finding issues. Then the next layer we have the
23:59:2223 hours, 59 minutes, 22 secondssilver layer. It is where we do transformations data cleansing but we don't apply yet any business rules. Now moving on to the last layer the gold
23:59:3023 hours, 59 minutes, 30 secondslayer. It is as well very similar to the data marts but there we can build different type of objects not only for reporting but as well for machine
23:59:3823 hours, 59 minutes, 38 secondslearning for AI and for many different purposes. So they are like business ready objects that you want to share as
23:59:4523 hours, 59 minutes, 45 secondsa data products. So those are the four approaches that you can use in order to build a data warehouse. So again if you are building a data architecture you
23:59:5423 hours, 59 minutes, 54 secondshave to specify which approach you want to follow. So at the start we said we want to build a data warehouse and then we have to decide between those four approaches on how to build a data
24:00:0224 hours, 2 secondswarehouse and in this project we will be using the medallion architecture. So this is a very important question that you have to answer as the first step of building a data architecture. All right.
24:00:1224 hours, 12 secondsSo with that we have decided on the approach. So we can go and mark it as done. The next step we're going to go and design the layers of the data warehouse.
24:00:2424 hours, 24 secondsNow there is like not 100% standard way and rules for each layer. What you have to do as a data architects you have to
24:00:3224 hours, 32 secondsdefine exactly what is the purpose of each layer. So we start with the bronze layer. So we say it's going to store row
24:00:3924 hours, 39 secondsand unprocessed data as it is from the sources. And why we are doing that it is for traceability and debugging. If you have a layer where you are keeping the
24:00:4724 hours, 47 secondsraw data, it is very important to have the data as it is from the sources because we can go always back to the bronze layer and investigate the data of specific source if something goes wrong.
24:00:5824 hours, 58 secondsSo the main objective is to have raw untouched data that's going to helps you as a data engineer by analyzing the root
24:01:0524 hours, 1 minute, 5 secondscause of issues. Now moving on to the server layer. It is the layer where we're going to store clean and standardized data and this is the place
24:01:1324 hours, 1 minute, 13 secondswhere we're going to do basic transformations in order to prepare the data for the final layer. Now for the go layer it's going to contain business
24:01:2024 hours, 1 minute, 20 secondsready data. So the main goal here is to provide data that could be consumed by business users and analysts in order to build reporting and analytics. So with
24:01:2924 hours, 1 minute, 29 secondsthat we have defined the main goal for each layer. Now next what I would like to do is to define the object types and since we are talking about a data
24:01:3724 hours, 1 minute, 37 secondswarehouse in database we have here generally two types either a table or a view. So we are going for the bronze layer and the silver layer with tables
24:01:4524 hours, 1 minute, 45 secondsbut for the gold layer we are going with the views. So the best practice says for the last layer in your data warehouse make it virtual using views. It going to
24:01:5424 hours, 1 minute, 54 secondsgives you a lot of dynamic and of course speed in order to build it since we don't have to make a load process for it. And now the next step is that we're going to go and define the load method.
24:02:0324 hours, 2 minutes, 3 secondsSo in this project I have decided to go with the full load using the method of truncating and inserting. It is just faster and way easier. So we're going to say for the bronze layer we're going to
24:02:1124 hours, 2 minutes, 11 secondsgo with the full load. And you have to specify as well for the silver layer as well. We're going to go with the full load. And of course for the views we don't need any load process. So each
24:02:2024 hours, 2 minutes, 20 secondstime you decide to go with tables you have to define the load methods with our full load, incremental loads and so on.
24:02:2524 hours, 2 minutes, 25 secondsNow we come to the very interesting part the data transformations. Now for the bronze layer, it is the easiest one about this topic because we don't have
24:02:3324 hours, 2 minutes, 33 secondsany transformations. We have to commit ourself to not touch the data, do not manipulate it, don't change anything. So
24:02:4024 hours, 2 minutes, 40 secondsit's going to stay as it is. If it comes bad, it's going to stay bad in the bronze layer. And now we come to the silver layer where we have the heavy lifting. As we committed in the
24:02:4824 hours, 2 minutes, 48 secondsobjective, we have to make clean and standardized data. And for that we have different types of transformations. So we have to do data cleansing, data standardizations, data normalizations.
24:02:5824 hours, 2 minutes, 58 secondsWe have to go and derive new columns and data enrichment. So there are like bunch of transformations that we have to do in
24:03:0624 hours, 3 minutes, 6 secondsorder to prepare the data. Our focus here is to transform the data to make it clean and following standards and try to push all business transformations to the
24:03:1424 hours, 3 minutes, 14 secondsnext layer. So that means in the god layer we will be focusing on business transformations that is needed for the consumers for the use cases. So what we
24:03:2324 hours, 3 minutes, 23 secondsdo here we do data integrations between source system we do data aggregations we apply a lot of business logics and rules
24:03:3024 hours, 3 minutes, 30 secondsand we build a data model that is ready for for example business intelligence.
24:03:3424 hours, 3 minutes, 34 secondsSo here we do a lot of business transformations and in the silver layer we do basic data transformations. So it is really here very important to make
24:03:4324 hours, 3 minutes, 43 secondsthe fine decisions what type of transformations to be done in each layer and make sure that you commit to those rules. Now the next aspect is about the
24:03:5124 hours, 3 minutes, 51 secondsdata modeling in the bronze layer and the silver layer. We will not break the data model that comes from the source system. So if the source system deliver
24:03:5924 hours, 3 minutes, 59 secondsfive tables, we're going to have here like five tables and as well in the silver layer. We will not go and denormalize or normalize or like make
24:04:0624 hours, 4 minutes, 6 secondssomething new, we're going to leave it exactly like it comes from the source system because what we're going to do,
24:04:1124 hours, 4 minutes, 11 secondswe're going to build the data model in the gold layer. And here you have to define which data model you want to follow. Are you following the star schema, the snowflake or are you just
24:04:2024 hours, 4 minutes, 20 secondsmaking aggregated objects? So you have to go and make a list of all data models types that you're going to follow in the gold layer. And at the end, what you can
24:04:2724 hours, 4 minutes, 27 secondsspecify in each layer is the target audience. And this is of course very important decision. In the bronze layer,
24:04:3324 hours, 4 minutes, 33 secondsyou don't want to give access to any end user. It is really important to make sure that only data engineers access the bronze layer. It makes no sense for data
24:04:4124 hours, 4 minutes, 41 secondsanalysts or data scientists to go to the bad data because you have a better version for that in the silver layer. So
24:04:4824 hours, 4 minutes, 48 secondsin the silver layer of course the data engineers have to have an access to it and as well the data analysts and the data scientists and so on but still you
24:04:5524 hours, 4 minutes, 55 secondsdon't give it to any business user that can't deal with the raw data model from the sources because for the business users you're going to get a better layer
24:05:0424 hours, 5 minutes, 4 secondsfor them and that is the go layer. So in the gold layer it is suitable for the data analyst and as well the business users because usually the business users
24:05:1324 hours, 5 minutes, 13 secondsdon't have a deep knowledge on the technicality of the server layer. So if you are designing multiple layers you have to discuss all those topics and
24:05:2024 hours, 5 minutes, 20 secondsmake clear decision for each layer. All right my friends. So now before we proceed with the design I want to tell you a secret principle concept that each
24:05:2924 hours, 5 minutes, 29 secondsdata architect must know and that is the separation of concerns. So what is that? As you are designing an architecture,
24:05:3624 hours, 5 minutes, 36 secondsyou have to make sure to break down the complex system into smaller independent parts and each part is responsible for a specific task. And here comes the magic.
24:05:4724 hours, 5 minutes, 47 secondsThe component of your architecture must not be duplicated. So you cannot have two parts are doing the same thing. So the idea here is to not mix everything.
24:05:5624 hours, 5 minutes, 56 secondsAnd this is one of the biggest mistakes in any big projects and I have shown that almost everywhere. So a good data
24:06:0324 hours, 6 minutes, 3 secondsarchitects follow this concept this principle. So for example if you are looking to our data architecture we have already done that. So we have defined
24:06:1124 hours, 6 minutes, 11 secondsunique set of tasks for each layer. So for example we have said in the server layer we do data cleansing but in the gold layer we do business
24:06:2024 hours, 6 minutes, 20 secondstransformations and with that you will not be allowing to do any business transformations. In the server layer and the same thing goes for the gold layer.
24:06:2824 hours, 6 minutes, 28 secondsYou don't do in the gold layer any data cleansing. So each layer has its own unique tasks and the same thing goes for the bronze layer and the silver layer.
24:06:3624 hours, 6 minutes, 36 secondsYou do not allow to load data from the source systems directly to the silver layer because we have decided the landing layer. The first layer is the
24:06:4524 hours, 6 minutes, 45 secondsbronze layer otherwise you will have like set of source systems that are loaded first to the bronze layer and another set is skipping the layer and
24:06:5224 hours, 6 minutes, 52 secondsgoing to the silver and with that we have overlapping. You are doing data ingestion in two different layers. So my friends, if you have this mindset,
24:07:0124 hours, 7 minutes, 1 secondseparation of concerns, I promise you, you're going to be a top data architect.

24:16:3624 hours, 16 minutes, 36 secondsprojects. All right. So now at this phase of the projects we usually define the naming conventions. So what is that?
24:16:4224 hours, 16 minutes, 42 secondsIt is set of rules that you define for naming everything in the projects whether it is a database, schema,
24:16:4924 hours, 16 minutes, 49 secondstables, stored procedures, folders,
24:16:5224 hours, 16 minutes, 52 secondsanything. And if you don't do that at the early phase of the projects, I promise you chaos can happen because what going to happen? You will have different developers in your projects
24:17:0124 hours, 17 minutes, 1 secondand each of those developers have their own style of course. So one developer might name a table dimension customers
24:17:0824 hours, 17 minutes, 8 secondswhere everything is lowerase and between them underscore and you have another developer creating another table called dimension products but using the camel
24:17:1624 hours, 17 minutes, 16 secondscase. So there is no separation between the words and the first character is capitalized and maybe another one using some prefixes like
24:17:2424 hours, 17 minutes, 24 secondsdim categories. So we have here like a shortcut of the dimension. So as you can see there are different designs and styles and if you leave the door open
24:17:3224 hours, 17 minutes, 32 secondswhat can happen in the middle of the project you will notice okay everything looks inconsistent and you can define a big task to go and rename everything
24:17:4124 hours, 17 minutes, 41 secondsfollowing a specific rule. So instead of wasting all this time at this phase you go and define the naming conventions and
24:17:4824 hours, 17 minutes, 48 secondslet's go and do that. So we usually start with a very important decision and that is which naming convention we going to follow in the whole project. So you
24:17:5624 hours, 17 minutes, 56 secondshave different cases like the camel case, the Pascal case, the kebab case,
24:18:0224 hours, 18 minutes, 2 secondsand the snake case. And for this project, we're going to go with the snake case where all the letters of a word going to be lowercased. And the
24:18:1024 hours, 18 minutes, 10 secondsseparation between words going to be an underscore. For example, a table name called customer info. Customer is lowercased. Info is as well lowercased.
24:18:1924 hours, 18 minutes, 19 secondsAnd between them an underscore. So this is always the first thing that you have to decide for your data projects. The second thing is to decide the language.
24:18:2824 hours, 18 minutes, 28 secondsSo for example, I work in Germany and there is always like a decision that we have to make whether we use Germany or English. So we have to decide for our
24:18:3524 hours, 18 minutes, 35 secondsproject which language we're going to use. And a very important general rule is that avoid reserved words. So don't
24:18:4324 hours, 18 minutes, 43 secondsuse a square reserved word as an object name like for example table. Don't give a table name as a table. So those are
24:18:5024 hours, 18 minutes, 50 secondsthe general principles. So those are the general rules that you have to follow in the whole project. This applies for everything for tables, columns, stored
24:18:5824 hours, 18 minutes, 58 secondsprocedures, any names that you are giving in your scripts. Now moving on,
24:19:0224 hours, 19 minutes, 2 secondswe have specifications for the table names. And here we have different set of rules for each layer. So here the rule
24:19:0924 hours, 19 minutes, 9 secondssays source system underscore entity. So we are saying all the tables in the bronze layer should start first with the
24:19:1624 hours, 19 minutes, 16 secondssource system name like for example CRM or ARB and after that we have an underscore and then at the end we have
24:19:2324 hours, 19 minutes, 23 secondsthe entity name or the table name. So for example we have this table name CRM.
24:19:2824 hours, 19 minutes, 28 secondsSo that means this table comes from the source system CRM and then we have the table name the entity name customer info. So this is the rule that we're
24:19:3724 hours, 19 minutes, 37 secondsgoing to follow in naming all tables in the bronze layer. Then moving on to the silver layer, it is exactly like the bronze because we are not going to
24:19:4524 hours, 19 minutes, 45 secondsrename anything. We are not going to build any new data model. So the naming going to be one one to one like the bronze. So it is exactly the same rules
24:19:5424 hours, 19 minutes, 54 secondsas the bronze. But if we go to the gold here, since we are building new data model, we have to go and rename things.
24:20:0124 hours, 20 minutes, 1 secondAnd since as well we are integrating multiple sources together, we will not be using the source system name in the tables because inside one table you
24:20:0924 hours, 20 minutes, 9 secondscould have multiple sources. So the rule says all the names must be meaningful business aligned names for the tables starting with the category prefix. So
24:20:1824 hours, 20 minutes, 18 secondshere the rule says it start with category then underscore and then entity. Now what is category? We have in the code layer different types of
24:20:2724 hours, 20 minutes, 27 secondstables. So we could build a table called a fact table. Another one could be a dimension. A third type could be an aggregation or a report. So we have
24:20:3624 hours, 20 minutes, 36 secondsdifferent types of tables and we can specify those types as a prefix at the start. So for example we are saying here
24:20:4424 hours, 20 minutes, 44 secondseffect sales. So the category is fact and the table name called sales. And here I just made like a table with
24:20:5124 hours, 20 minutes, 51 secondsdifferent type of patterns. So we could have a dimension. So we say it start with the dim underscore for example dimim customers or products. And then we
24:21:0024 hours, 21 minuteshave another type called fact table. So it start with fact underscore or aggregated table where we have the first three characters like aggregating the
24:21:0824 hours, 21 minutes, 8 secondscustomers or the sales monthly. So as you can see as you are creating a naming convention you have first to make it clear what is the rule describe each
24:21:1724 hours, 21 minutes, 17 secondspart of the rule and start giving examples. So with that we make it clear for the whole team which names they should follow. So we talked here about
24:21:2524 hours, 21 minutes, 25 secondsthe table naming convention. Then you can as well go and make naming convention for the columns. Like for example in the code layer we're going to go and have surrogate keys. So we can
24:21:3424 hours, 21 minutes, 34 secondsdefine it like this. The surrogate key should start with a table name and then underscore a key. Like for example we can call it customer key. It is a
24:21:4324 hours, 21 minutes, 43 secondssurrogate key in the dimension customers. The same thing for technical columns. As a data engineer, we might add our own columns to the tables that
24:21:5124 hours, 21 minutes, 51 secondsdon't come from the source system. And those columns are the technical columns or sometimes we call them metadata columns. Now, in order to separate them
24:21:5924 hours, 21 minutes, 59 secondsfrom the original columns that comes from the source system, we can have like a prefix for that. Like for example, the rule says if you are building any
24:22:0824 hours, 22 minutes, 8 secondstechnical or metadata columns, the column should start with DWH underscore and then the column name. For example,
24:22:1524 hours, 22 minutes, 15 secondsif you want the metadata load dates, we can have DWH load dates. So with that, if anyone sees that column starts with DWH, we
24:22:2424 hours, 22 minutes, 24 secondsunderstand this data comes from a data engineer. And we can keep adding rules like for example the store procedure over here. If you are making an ETL
24:22:3224 hours, 22 minutes, 32 secondsscript, then it should start with the prefix load underscore and then the layer. For example, the store procedure that is responsible for loading the bronze going to be called load bronze.
24:22:4324 hours, 22 minutes, 43 secondsand for the silver load underscore silver. So those are currently the rules for the start procedure. So this is how I do it usually in my projects. All

26:48:2826 hours, 48 minutes, 28 secondsOr is it like maybe a flat table? So what type of table that we have built and the last step is of course we have now to rename all the columns into
26:48:3626 hours, 48 minutes, 36 secondssomething friendly and easy to understand so that our consumers don't struggle with technical names. So once we have all those steps what we're going to do it's time to validate what we have
26:48:4426 hours, 48 minutes, 44 secondscreated. So what we have to do the new data model that we have created it should be connectable and we have to check that the data integration is done
26:48:5226 hours, 48 minutes, 52 secondscorrectly and once everything is fine we cannot skip the last step. we have to document and as well commit our work in the g. And here we will be introducing a
26:49:0026 hours, 49 minutesnew type of documentations. So we're going to have a diagram about the data model. We're going to build a data dictionary where we're going to describe the data model. And of course we're going to extend the data flow diagram.
26:49:1126 hours, 49 minutes, 11 secondsSo this is our process. Those are the main steps that we will do in order to build the code
26:49:1926 hours, 49 minutes, 19 secondslayer. Okay. So what is exactly data moduling? Usually the source system going to deliver for you row data
26:49:2526 hours, 49 minutes, 25 secondsunorganized messy not very useful in its current states. But now the data modeling is the process of taking this
26:49:3326 hours, 49 minutes, 33 secondsrow data and then organize it and structure it in meaningful way. So what we are doing we are putting the data in new friendly and easy to understand objects like customers, orders,
26:49:4526 hours, 49 minutes, 45 secondsproducts. Each one of them is focused on specific information and what is very important is we're going to describe the relationship between those objects. So
26:49:5426 hours, 49 minutes, 54 secondsby connecting them using lines. So what you have built on the right side we call it logical data model. If you compare to the left side you can see the data model
26:50:0226 hours, 50 minutes, 2 secondsmakes it really easy to understand our data and the relationship the processes behind them. Now in data modeling we have three different stages or let's say
26:50:0926 hours, 50 minutes, 9 secondsthree different ways on how to draw a data model. The first stage is the conceptual data model. Here the focus is only on the entity. So we have
26:50:1826 hours, 50 minutes, 18 secondscustomers, orders, products and we don't go in details at all. So we don't specify any columns or attributes inside those boxes. We just want to focus what
26:50:2626 hours, 50 minutes, 26 secondsare the entities that we have and as well the relationship between them. So the conceptual data model don't focus at all on the details. It just gives the
26:50:3526 hours, 50 minutes, 35 secondsbig picture. So the second data model that we can build is the logical data model. And here we start specifying what are the different columns that we can
26:50:4326 hours, 50 minutes, 43 secondsfind in each entity like we have the customer ID the first name last name and so on and we still draw the relationship between those entities and as well we
26:50:5126 hours, 50 minutes, 51 secondsmake it clear which columns are the primary key and so on. So as you can see we have here more details but one thing we don't describe a lot of details for
26:50:5926 hours, 50 minutes, 59 secondseach column and we are not worry how exactly we going to store those tables in the database. The third and last stage we have the physical data model.
26:51:0826 hours, 51 minutes, 8 secondsThis is where everything gets ready before creating it in the database. So here you have to add all the technical details like adding for each column the
26:51:1626 hours, 51 minutes, 16 secondsdata types and the length of each data type and many other database techniques and details. So again if you look to the conceptual data model it gives us the
26:51:2526 hours, 51 minutes, 25 secondsbig picture and in the logical data model we dive into details of what data we need and the physical layer model prepares everything for the
26:51:3326 hours, 51 minutes, 33 secondsimplementation in the database. And to be honest in my projects I only draw the conceptual and the logical data model because drawing and building the
26:51:4126 hours, 51 minutes, 41 secondsphysical data model needs a lot of efforts and time and there are many tools like in data bricks they automatically generate those models. So
26:51:4926 hours, 51 minutes, 49 secondsin this project what we're going to do we're going to draw the logical data model for the gold
26:51:5726 hours, 51 minutes, 57 secondslayer. All right. It's now for analytics and especially for data warehousing and business intelligence. We need a special data model that is optimized for
26:52:0526 hours, 52 minutes, 5 secondsreporting and analytics and it should be flexible, scalable and as well easy to understand. And for that we have two special data models. The first type of
26:52:1426 hours, 52 minutes, 14 secondsdata model we have the star schema. It has a central fact table in the middle and surrounded by dimensions. The fact table contains transactions, events, and
26:52:2326 hours, 52 minutes, 23 secondsthe dimensions contains descriptive informations. And the relationship between the fact table in the middle and the dimensions around it forms like a
26:52:3126 hours, 52 minutes, 31 secondsstar shape. And that's why we call it star schema. And we have another data model called snowflake schema. It looks very similar to the star schema. So we
26:52:3926 hours, 52 minutes, 39 secondshave again the fact in the middle and surrounded by dimensions. But the big difference is that we break the dimensions into smaller subdimensions.
26:52:4826 hours, 52 minutes, 48 secondsAnd the shape of this data model as you are extending the dimensions it's going to looks like a snowflake. So now if you compare them side by side you can see that the star schema looks easier right?
26:52:5826 hours, 52 minutes, 58 secondsSo it is usually easy to understand easy to query it is really perfect for analyzers but it has one issue with the
26:53:0526 hours, 53 minutes, 5 secondsdimension might contain duplicates and your dimensions get bigger with the time. Now if you compare it to the snowflake you can see the schema is more
26:53:1326 hours, 53 minutes, 13 secondscomplex. You saw you need a lot of knowledge and efforts in order to query something from the snowflake. But the main advantage here comes with the
26:53:2026 hours, 53 minutes, 20 secondsnormalization as you are breaking those redundancies in small tables. You can optimize the storage. But to be honest,
26:53:2626 hours, 53 minutes, 26 secondswho care about the storage? So for this project, I have chose to use the star schema because it is very commonly used.
26:53:3226 hours, 53 minutes, 32 secondsPerfect for reporting like for example if you're using PowerBI and we don't have to worry about the storage. So that's why we're going to adopt this model to build our gold layer.
26:53:4626 hours, 53 minutes, 46 secondsOkay. So now one more thing about those data models is that they contain two types of tables fact and dimensions. So when I say this is a fact table or a
26:53:5426 hours, 53 minutes, 54 secondsdimension table well the dimension contains descriptive informations or like categories that gives some context to your data. For example a product info you have product name, category,
26:54:0426 hours, 54 minutes, 4 secondssubcategories and so on. This is like a table that is describing the products and this we call it dimension. But in the other hand we have facts. They are
26:54:1326 hours, 54 minutes, 13 secondsevents like transactions. They contain three important informations. First you have multiple ids from multiple
26:54:2026 hours, 54 minutes, 20 secondsdimensions. Then we have like date informations like when the transaction or the event did happen. And the third type of information you're going to have
26:54:2826 hours, 54 minutes, 28 secondslike measures and numbers. So if you see those three types of data in one table,
26:54:3326 hours, 54 minutes, 33 secondsthen this is a fact. So if you have a table that answers how much or how many,
26:54:3826 hours, 54 minutes, 38 secondsthen this is a fact. But if you have a table that answers who, what, where,
26:54:4326 hours, 54 minutes, 43 secondsthen this is a dimension table. So this is what dimension and fact tables. All right my friends. So so far
26:54:5226 hours, 54 minutes, 52 secondsin the bronze layer and in the silver layer we didn't discuss anything about the business. So the bronze and silver were very technical. We are focusing on
26:55:0026 hours, 55 minutesdata ingestion. We are focusing on cleaning up the data quality of the data. But still the tables are very oriented to the source system. Now comes
26:55:0926 hours, 55 minutes, 9 secondsthe fun part in the god layer where we're going to go and break the whole data model of the sources. So we're going to create something completely new
26:55:1626 hours, 55 minutes, 16 secondsto our business that is easy to consume for business reporting and analyzes. And here it is very important to have a clear understanding of the business and
26:55:2526 hours, 55 minutes, 25 secondsthe processes. And if you don't know it already at this phase you have really to invest time by meeting maybe process experts, the domain experts in order to
26:55:3426 hours, 55 minutes, 34 secondshave clear understanding what we are talking about in the data. So now what we're going to do, we're going to try to detect what are the business objects that are hidden in the source systems.
26:55:4426 hours, 55 minutes, 44 secondsSo now let's go and explore that. All right. Now in order to build a new data model, I have to understand first the original data model. What are the main

27:11:4527 hours, 11 minutes, 45 secondsIs it a fact table or a dimension? Well,
27:11:4827 hours, 11 minutes, 48 secondsas we learned, dimensions hold descriptive informations about an object. And as you can see, we have here a descriptions about the customers. So
27:11:5627 hours, 11 minutes, 56 secondsall those columns are describing the customer information. And we don't have here like transactions and events. And we don't have like measures and so on.
27:12:0427 hours, 12 minutes, 4 secondsSo we cannot say this object is a fact.
27:12:0727 hours, 12 minutes, 7 secondsIt is clearly a dimension. So that's why we're going to go and call this object the dimension customer. Now there is one thing that if you are creating a new
27:12:1627 hours, 12 minutes, 16 secondsdimension you need always a primary key for the dimension. Of course we can go over here and depend on the primary key that we get from the source system but
27:12:2427 hours, 12 minutes, 24 secondssometimes you can have like dimensions where you don't have like a primary key that you can count on. So what we have to do is to go and generate a new
27:12:3327 hours, 12 minutes, 33 secondsprimary key in the data warehouse. And those primary keys we call it surrogate keys. Srogate keys are system generated
27:12:4027 hours, 12 minutes, 40 secondsunique identifier that is assigned to each records to make the record unique.
27:12:4527 hours, 12 minutes, 45 secondsIt is not a business key. It has no meaning and no one in the business knows about it. We only use it in order to
27:12:5227 hours, 12 minutes, 52 secondsconnect our data model. And in this way we have more control on how to connect our data model and we don't have to depend always on the source system. And
27:13:0127 hours, 13 minutes, 1 secondthere are different ways on how to generate surrogate keys like defining it in the DDL or maybe using the window function row number in this data
27:13:0927 hours, 13 minutes, 9 secondswarehouse. I'm going to go with a simple solution where we're going to go and use the window function. So now in order to generate a surrogate key for this
27:13:1727 hours, 13 minutes, 17 secondsdimension what we're going to do it is very simple. So we're going to say row number over and here we have to order by
27:13:2527 hours, 13 minutes, 25 secondssomething. You can order by the create date or the customer ID or the customer number. whatever you want but in this
27:13:3227 hours, 13 minutes, 32 secondsexample I'm going to go and order by the customer ID. So we have to follow the naming convention that all surrogate keys with a key at the end as a suffix.
27:13:4127 hours, 13 minutes, 41 secondsSo now let's go and query those informations. And as you can see at the start we have a customer key and this is a sequence. We don't have here of course

27:30:3727 hours, 30 minutes, 37 secondsimportant for reporting and analytics in order to understand how I'm going to go and use the data model. And we have different types of relationships. We
27:30:4427 hours, 30 minutes, 44 secondshave one to one, one to many. And in star schema data model the relationship between the dimension and the fact is one to many. And that's because in the
27:30:5227 hours, 30 minutes, 52 secondstable customers we have for a specific customer only one record describing the customer but in the fact table the customer might exist in multiple records
27:31:0127 hours, 31 minutes, 1 secondand that's because customers can order multiple times. So that's why in fact it is many and in the dimension side it is

27:31:1627 hours, 31 minutes, 16 secondshave different types of arrows. So for example we have zero to many, one to many, one to one and many different types of relations. So now which one we

27:31:2827 hours, 31 minutes, 28 secondsSo that means the customer must exist in the dimension table. Too many but it is optional. So here we have three scenarios. The customer didn't order
27:31:3627 hours, 31 minutes, 36 secondsanything or the customer did order only once or the customer did order many things. So that's why in the fact table it is optional. So we're going to take
27:31:4527 hours, 31 minutes, 45 secondsthis one and place it over here. So we're going to go and connect this part to the customer dimension and the many
27:31:5227 hours, 31 minutes, 52 secondsparts to the facts. Well actually we have to do it on the customers. So with that we are describing the relationship between the dimensions and fact with one
27:32:0127 hours, 32 minutes, 1 secondto many. One is mandatory for the customer dimension and many is optional to the facts. So we have the same story as well for the products. So the many
27:32:1027 hours, 32 minutes, 10 secondspart to the facts and the one goes to the products. So it's going to look like this. Each time you are connecting new dimension to the fact table, it is
27:32:1927 hours, 32 minutes, 19 secondsusually one to many relationship. So you can go and add anything you want to this model like for example a text like explaining something. For example, if
27:32:2727 hours, 32 minutes, 27 secondsyou have some complicated calculations and so on, you can go and write this information over here. So for example, we can say over here sales calculation,

27:33:1527 hours, 33 minutes, 15 secondstables in the database. You have as well like some kind of documentations and explanation. In one click we can see how the data model is built and how you can
27:33:2327 hours, 33 minutes, 23 secondsconnect the tables together. It is amazing really for all users of your data model. All right. So now with that we have really nice data model. And now
27:33:3227 hours, 33 minutes, 32 secondsin the next step we're going to go and create quickly a data catalog.
27:33:3927 hours, 33 minutes, 39 secondsAll right, great. So with that we have a data model and we can say we have something called a data products and we will be sharing this data product with
27:33:4727 hours, 33 minutes, 47 secondsdifferent types of users and there is something that every data products absolutely needs and that is the data catalog. It is a document that can
27:33:5527 hours, 33 minutes, 55 secondsdescribe everything about your data model. columns, the tables, maybe the relationship between the tables as well.
27:34:0227 hours, 34 minutes, 2 secondsAnd with that, you make your data product clear for everyone. And it's going to be for them way easier to derive more insights and reports from
27:34:0927 hours, 34 minutes, 9 secondsyour data product. And what is the most important one? It is time-saving because if you don't do that, what's going to happen? Each consumer, each user of your
27:34:1727 hours, 34 minutes, 17 secondsdata product will keep asking you the same questions about what do you mean with this column? What is this table?
27:34:2327 hours, 34 minutes, 23 secondsHow to connect the table A with the table P? and you will keep repeating yourself and explaining stuff. So instead of that you prepare a data
27:34:3027 hours, 34 minutes, 30 secondscatalog, a data model and you deliver everything together to the users and with that you are saving a lot of time and stress. I know it is annoying to
27:34:3827 hours, 34 minutes, 38 secondscreate a data catalog but it is investments and best practices. So now let's go and create one. Okay. So now in order to do that I have created a new
27:34:4527 hours, 34 minutes, 45 secondsfile called data catalog in the folder documents. And here what we're going to do is very straightforward. We're going to make a section for each table in the
27:34:5227 hours, 34 minutes, 52 secondscode layer. So for example we have here the table dimension customers. What you have to do first is to describe this table. So we are saying it stores
27:35:0027 hours, 35 minutesdetails about the customers with the demographics and geographics data. So you give a short description for the table and then after that you're going
27:35:0727 hours, 35 minutes, 7 secondsto go and list all your columns inside this table and maybe as well the data type. But what is way important is the description for each column. So you give
27:35:1527 hours, 35 minutes, 15 secondsa very short description like for example here the gender of the customer.
27:35:1927 hours, 35 minutes, 19 secondsAnd now one of the best practices of describing a column is to give examples because you can understand quickly the purpose of the columns by just seeing an

27:46:2327 hours, 46 minutes, 23 secondsprojects. All right my friends. So we're going to start with a secret, a little trick that I usually do by analyzing any
27:46:3027 hours, 46 minutes, 30 secondsdata sets. So let's start with little coffee before we start. H this is really hot. Okay. So the secret says as I'm
27:46:3727 hours, 46 minutes, 37 secondslooking to any data sets in any projects, I see the data always divided between dimensions and measures.
27:46:4527 hours, 46 minutes, 45 secondsWhat truth? You take the blue pill, you take the red pill. All I'm offering is the truth. Nothing more.
27:46:5427 hours, 46 minutes, 54 secondsIf you see your data like me as dimensions and measures, you can generate like endless amount of insights from any projects from any data sets and
27:47:0327 hours, 47 minutes, 3 secondsyou will find me through the projects that I'm always speaking about measures and dimensions. So I'm going to show you how I usually do it. So now usually by
27:47:1127 hours, 47 minutes, 11 secondslooking to any data sets in any projects. So you have like multiple columns and rows here I see the data always splitted into two categories
27:47:1827 hours, 47 minutes, 18 secondseither a dimension or a measure. And now of course the question is here is my column a dimension or a measure? Well in
27:47:2627 hours, 47 minutes, 26 secondsorder to assign it to one of those categories you have to ask the first question is it a numeric value? If it's not so you have like string or date or
27:47:3527 hours, 47 minutes, 35 secondsany other data type then it is a dimension and if it is yes in numeric then you have to ask the second question
27:47:4227 hours, 47 minutes, 42 secondsdoes it make sense to aggregate it. So if the answer for both questions is yes,
27:47:4627 hours, 47 minutes, 46 secondsit is numeric and it makes sense to aggregate it then it is a measure otherwise it is a dimension. Now let's practice and have some examples. So now
27:47:5527 hours, 47 minutes, 55 secondsby looking to the values of the column category you can see all the values are characters. So it is not numeric that means this column is a dimension. So it
27:48:0427 hours, 48 minutes, 4 secondsis very simple. Let's take another column. We have the sales amount. So now as you can see the values are numeric and as well it makes sense to aggregate
27:48:1227 hours, 48 minutes, 12 secondsthose values. we can get the total sales or the average sales and so on. So it fulfill both of the conditions. It is numeric and it makes sense to aggregate
27:48:2027 hours, 48 minutes, 20 secondsit. That's why we say sales is a measure. Now if you're checking the values of the product name, you can see that all of them are characters and
27:48:2827 hours, 48 minutes, 28 secondsnames. So it is not numeric. That means the product is a dimension. Moving on to the next one, we have the quantity. The values are numeric and as well it makes
27:48:3627 hours, 48 minutes, 36 secondssense to aggregate it. Can summarize all those values to have the total quantity.
27:48:4027 hours, 48 minutes, 40 secondsSo quantity is a measure. Now if you're looking to the values of the birth dates you can see this is a date information it is not numeric so that means it is a
27:48:4927 hours, 48 minutes, 49 secondsdimension right but if you calculate the age from the birth dates age of the customer going to be in numeric and it
27:48:5627 hours, 48 minutes, 56 secondsmakes sense to aggregate it for example finding the average age of customers. So if we derive a numeric value from a
27:49:0427 hours, 49 minutes, 4 secondsdimension then we can use it as a measure. So age is measure and now we come to something really tricky. This is the ID. So for example if you are
27:49:1227 hours, 49 minutes, 12 secondschecking the customer ID you can see all those values are numeric. So the first condition is fulfilled. Now the very
27:49:1927 hours, 49 minutes, 19 secondsimportant question does it make sense to aggregate the ids? Well those ids are unique identifier for a customer and if you find like the average of that it is
27:49:2827 hours, 49 minutes, 28 secondsnot like helpful right I cannot think of one use case of aggregating the customer ID like having the average of all those
27:49:3627 hours, 49 minutes, 36 secondsids or summarizing the ids. So it makes no sense to aggregate it. That's why we can consider the ID of a customer as a
27:49:4427 hours, 49 minutes, 44 secondsdimension not as a measure. So as you can see it is very simple. If it is numeric and it makes sense to aggregate then it is measure otherwise it is a
27:49:5227 hours, 49 minutes, 52 secondsdimension. And this is the foundations of any data analytics. If you see your data as dimensions and measures you can
27:50:0027 hours, 50 minutesgenerate a lot of use cases and insights from your data sets. Now I totally understand if you are still confused about dimensions and measures and you
27:50:0827 hours, 50 minutes, 8 secondsmight be asking why do I need measures and dimensions. Well if you are doing any type of data analysis or you are exploring any data sets you will be end
27:50:1627 hours, 50 minutes, 16 secondsup always like grouping up the data by something like you are grouping the data by countries or grouping the data by for example products or categories. So we
27:50:2527 hours, 50 minutes, 25 secondsneed dimensions to group up our data and in the other sides you will be asking questions like how much how many what is the total of something. So you always
27:50:3427 hours, 50 minutes, 34 secondsneed to aggregate or calculate something right and for that you need the measure.
27:50:3827 hours, 50 minutes, 38 secondsSo we need the measures in order to answer the question how many and how much and we need the dimensions in order to group up the data by something. So
27:50:4727 hours, 50 minutes, 47 secondsthat's why almost in any type of data analyzes you need dimensions and measures and this going to be more clear as we progress in the projects. All
27:50:5427 hours, 50 minutes, 54 secondsright. So now I'm going to walk you through the project road map and I have split that into six steps. So we're going to do different types of explorations like the database
