28:39:2028 hours, 39 minutes, 20 secondsSo that's all about how to analyze the trends and the change over time. Now in the next step we're going to do some kind of advanced aggregations by doing
28:39:2728 hours, 39 minutes, 27 secondscumulative analyszis. Okay. So what is cumulative
28:39:3428 hours, 39 minutes, 34 secondsanalyszis? It is aggregating the data progressively over the time and this is very important technique in order to understand how our business is growing
28:39:4428 hours, 39 minutes, 44 secondsover the time. So how our business is progressing over the time whether it is growing or declining it is very interesting analyszis. So the formula
28:39:5228 hours, 39 minutes, 52 secondsgoing to be very similar to the changes over time but instead of having a simple aggregations on the measure we're going to aggregate our measure but this time
28:39:5928 hours, 39 minutes, 59 secondscumulative. So we are like adding stuff on top of each others and the data again can split it by the date dimension cuz we want to track the progress over the
28:40:0828 hours, 40 minutes, 8 secondstime. For example, we can find the running total of sales or the moving average of sales by a month. So now let's have again our simple example
28:40:1628 hours, 40 minutes, 16 secondswhere our sales is splitted by the years. Now this is the classic change over time. But in order now to make it cumulative what can happen? We're going
28:40:2428 hours, 40 minutes, 24 secondsto take the measure and add to it. For example, 2024 we have 300. And now for 2025, we're going to add the 300
28:40:3128 hours, 40 minutes, 31 secondstogether with the 100 in order to make it cumulative. So for 2025, we're going to have 400. And the same thing for
28:40:3828 hours, 40 minutes, 38 seconds2026, we're going to go and add the 400 together with the 200. And with that, we will get 600. So as you can see, we are keep adding the values in order to
28:40:4728 hours, 40 minutes, 47 secondsgenerate something called cumulative value. Now for this type of analysis, we use in SQL the aggregate window functions. in order to find out the
28:40:5528 hours, 40 minutes, 55 secondscumulative values. So now let's go and apply our formula in order to find whether our business is growing or declining. So let's go. Okay, so now we
28:41:0228 hours, 41 minutes, 2 secondshave to analyze the following. We're going to calculate the total sales for each month and as well the running total of sales over time in order to analyze
28:41:1028 hours, 41 minutes, 10 secondsthe trends. So let's see how we're going to do that. Let's start with the easy stuff where we're going to calculate the total sales for each month. So we are calculating the changes over time and we
28:41:1828 hours, 41 minutes, 18 secondshave already done that. So all what we need is a date and a measure. Our date going to be the order date and the measure going to be the sales amount
28:41:2628 hours, 41 minutes, 26 secondsfrom our fact table. So let's query this. And now we want to find the total sales for each month. That means we're going to change
28:41:3328 hours, 41 minutes, 33 secondsthe granularity of the order date from a day to a month. And I usually like using the date rank for this kind of tasks.
28:41:4028 hours, 41 minutes, 40 secondsAnd the granularity going to be the month. So this is the order dates. And now for the sales we're going to use aggregate function sum sales as
28:41:4928 hours, 41 minutes, 49 secondstotal sales. And of course we have to go and group up the data by the date. So let's go and execute it. So as
28:41:5928 hours, 41 minutes, 59 secondsyou can see we have now the total sales for each month. And don't forget to get rid of the nulls. So where we can say
28:42:0528 hours, 42 minutes, 5 secondswhere order date is not null. Now it looks better. We don't have nulls. And of course we can go and order the data
28:42:1228 hours, 42 minutes, 12 secondsby our date. Now our measure is just aggregated for each month individually.
28:42:1728 hours, 42 minutes, 17 secondsRight? But we don't want that. We want to have like a running total. So we'd like to have like commumulative metric.
28:42:2428 hours, 42 minutes, 24 secondsIn order to do that, we have to use window function. So let's go and do that. We will use a subquery for that.
28:42:3028 hours, 42 minutes, 30 secondsIn order just to make it simple. So what we need? We need the order date and let's say the total sales and here we have to have our window
28:42:3928 hours, 42 minutes, 39 secondsfunction. Then we're going to put the rest in a subquery. And of course we can go and get rid of the order by because
28:42:4628 hours, 42 minutes, 46 secondsanyway our data going to be sorted using the window function. So now let's start writing our window function. We will have the sum of total sales. So we want
28:42:5628 hours, 42 minutes, 56 secondsto summarize those new values. And we're going to build a window function like this over. We don't have to go and partition anything. So we can go
28:43:0328 hours, 43 minutes, 3 secondsimmediately and say order by our new order date that we have calculated. And we want it to be ascending. So actually
28:43:1028 hours, 43 minutes, 10 secondsthat's it. So as running total sales. So let's try that out. Now if you look to the result you can see that all those
28:43:1928 hours, 43 minutes, 19 secondsvalues are cumulative and it is working like this. The first total sales is equal to the total sales because previously we don't have anything. Now
28:43:2728 hours, 43 minutes, 27 secondsfor the next row what going to happen is going to go and add this value to the previous one. And with that we get the
28:43:3428 hours, 43 minutes, 34 secondsrunning total value. Now moving on to the third row is going to go and add all those three values together. And of course this going to give us the running
28:43:4228 hours, 43 minutes, 42 secondstotal for this month and so on. So as SQL is moving through the window it is always adding the current value to all
28:43:5028 hours, 43 minutes, 50 secondsprevious values. And this is because of the default frame of the window. The frame going to be between the unbounded preceding and the current row. So that
28:43:5928 hours, 43 minutes, 59 secondsmeans for example if we are at this row over here current total sales for this month is this one and the unbounded preceding is all the values before this
28:44:0828 hours, 44 minutes, 8 secondsmonth. So that means we are getting all the previous values together with the current value and with that we will get the effect of the running total sales.
28:44:1728 hours, 44 minutes, 17 secondsAnd now of course as you can see it is going through all the years. Right now we can go and limit the running total
28:44:2428 hours, 44 minutes, 24 secondsfor only one year. So for each new year it has to reset and start from the scratch. So that means we are partitioning the data. For each year we
28:44:3328 hours, 44 minutes, 33 secondswould like to have partition. For the first year, it's going to be 2010. It is one row. And for the 2011, we're going to get the whole partition over here.
28:44:4128 hours, 44 minutes, 41 secondsSo, in order to partition our window,
28:44:4328 hours, 44 minutes, 43 secondsit's very simple. We're going to go and say partition by the order date. That's it. Let's go and execute it. Now, let's
28:44:5128 hours, 44 minutes, 51 secondsgo and check for the first partition for 2010. You can see the running total is the same as the first month. But since we have only one month, that's it for
28:44:5928 hours, 44 minutes, 59 secondsthis year. Now, as we go to the next year, as you can see, it resets. So you can see the running total sales for
28:45:0628 hours, 45 minutes, 6 seconds2011. It is exactly as January. It is not adding up now the value of the current value with the previous one
28:45:1328 hours, 45 minutes, 13 secondsbecause the previous one is outside of the window. So as you can see we are getting running total for the whole year and once we hit a new year it is going
28:45:2228 hours, 45 minutes, 22 secondsto reset. So it is working and this is how you can create cumulative values in SQL. And of course if you would like to change the granularity of our data it is
28:45:3028 hours, 45 minutes, 30 secondsvery simple. All what you have to do is to go over here and say instead of month we're going to make it as a year. And of course don't forget to change as well

28:45:5428 hours, 45 minutes, 54 secondscumulative metric over the years. So as you can see it is very simple. Now we can go and add like another measure and another aggregation like for example

29:07:0329 hours, 7 minutes, 3 secondsstart doing data segmentations using SQL. So let's go. Okay. So now what is data segmentations?
29:07:1229 hours, 7 minutes, 12 secondsWhat we're going to do here is we're going to go and group up the data based on specific range. So that means we're going to go and create a new categories
29:07:1929 hours, 7 minutes, 19 secondsand then go and aggregate the data based on the new category. And the formula for that going to be very interesting. So it's going to be this time we're going
29:07:2629 hours, 7 minutes, 26 secondsto have a measure by a measure not by dimension. So you have to go and pick two different measures and convert one of those measures to a range or to a
29:07:3529 hours, 7 minutes, 35 secondsgroup and then aggregate the data by this measure. So for example, we're going to go and calculate the total number of products by the sales range or
29:07:4329 hours, 7 minutes, 43 secondsthe total number of customers by the age group. So as you can see we have two measures and we are trying to combine them together in order to create new insights. Let's have the following
29:07:5129 hours, 7 minutes, 51 secondsexample. So here for example we have like two measures and now the first step is that we're going to take one of those measures and convert it to a dimension.
29:07:5929 hours, 7 minutes, 59 secondsconverted to a category. For example,
29:08:0129 hours, 8 minutes, 1 secondwe're going to say if the values are like equal or below 100, it will be converted to a category called low. And
29:08:0829 hours, 8 minutes, 8 secondsbetween 100 and 200, it's going to be assigned to a new category called medium. And everything above 200, it's going to be large. So, as you can see
29:08:1629 hours, 8 minutes, 16 secondswhat we are doing, we are taking one measure and based on the range of this measure, we are building a new categories, new dimension. And now the
29:08:2429 hours, 8 minutes, 24 secondsfinal step is the easiest one. We're going to go and aggregate another measure based on the new category. So we're going to have seven for low, six
29:08:3129 hours, 8 minutes, 31 secondsfor medium, and 15 for large. So with that, as you can see, we are creating new categories or segments based on a measure. And then we are aggregating
29:08:3929 hours, 8 minutes, 39 secondsanother measure based of this new segments. And in SQL, in order to create those new categories and segments, we
29:08:4629 hours, 8 minutes, 46 secondsuse the amazing case when statements because it's going to help us to define the rules and based on the range, it's going to go and create a new category
29:08:5429 hours, 8 minutes, 54 secondsand labels. So now let's go and apply this formula on our data set in order to segment our data. So let's go. Okay. So now let's go and segment our data and
29:09:0329 hours, 9 minutes, 3 secondsall what we need is two measures. So now we have the following task and it says segment products into cost ranges and count how many products fall into each
